AI.tldr: SPADE is a novel algorithm that dramatically improves drug discovery efficiency, requiring only 40 tests on average to find 10 high-quality ligands, while being 10x faster than competitors.
AI.motivation: Traditional drug discovery has low success rates (less than 5% of candidates pass early stages) and needs methods that work for novel proteins with no prior data. The field requires more efficient iterative selection and testing of candidate ligands.
AI.method: SPADE (the proposed algorithm) introduces a novel approach to ligand selection that learns from sparse data. It enables efficient iterative testing starting from scratch without prior data on target proteins.
AI.result: SPADE requires only 40 tests on average to find 10 high-quality ligands. It outperforms deep learning and Bayesian optimization methods in 1-vs-1 comparisons on more proteins, achieving median improvements of 7%-32% in sample efficiency, and is 10x faster than its closest competitor.
AI.conclusion: SPADE represents a significant advancement in drug discovery efficiency, particularly for novel proteins, by dramatically reducing the number of tests needed while improving speed and performance compared to existing methods.

title: Who Prices Cognitive Labor in the Age of Agents? A Position on Compute-Anchored Wages
summary: A natural intuition about the economics of AI agents is that, because agents can be replicated at near-zero marginal cost, they constitute a labor input in infinitely elastic supply, and therefore drive cognitive-labor wages to zero. We argue this framing is wrong in mechanism but partially correct in conclusion, and that the correction matters for both theory and policy. \textbf{Agents are not labor; they are a production technology that converts compute capital $K_c$ into effective units of cognitive labor $L_A$.} Once this is recognized, the elastic-supply margin that anchors the equilibrium wage migrates from the labor market to the compute capital market. Building on the textbook factor-pricing framework \citep{mankiw2020}, we derive a \emph{Compute-Anchored Wage} (CAW) bound stating that, on tasks where human and agent cognitive labor are substitutes, the competitive human wage is bounded above by $λ\cdot k \cdot r_c$, where $r_c$ is the rental rate of compute capital, $k$ is the compute intensity of one effective agent-labor unit, and $λ$ is the relative human-to-agent productivity. We generalize the result through CES aggregation, separate substitutable from complementary tasks (yielding a directional inversion of skill-biased technical change), and discuss factor-share consequences. The position is concise: \emph{the price-setter for cognitive labor is no longer the labor market.}

AI.tldr: AI agents are not labor but a production technology that converts compute capital into cognitive labor, meaning wages are bounded by compute costs rather than labor supply.
AI.motivation: To correct the common intuition that AI agents drive cognitive wages to zero by showing they're infinitely elastic labor supply. The paper argues this framing is wrong and that understanding the true mechanism matters for theory and policy.
AI.method: Reconceptualize AI agents as a production technology converting compute capital into cognitive labor. Apply textbook factor-pricing framework to derive a Compute-Anchored Wage (CAW) bound using factor pricing equations. Generalize through CES aggregation and analyze substitutable vs. complementary tasks.
AI.result: Derives the CAW bound: on substitutable tasks, competitive human wage is bounded above by λ·k·r_c (where r_c is compute rental rate, k is compute intensity, λ is relative human-to-agent productivity). Shows wage anchor shifts from labor to compute capital market, with implications for skill-biased technical change and factor shares.
AI.conclusion: The price-setter for cognitive labor is no longer the labor market but the compute capital market. This corrected understanding has important theoretical and policy implications for how AI will affect wages and labor markets.

title: Neural Co-state Policies: Structuring Hidden States in Recurrent Reinforcement Learning
summary: A key capability of intelligent agents is operating under partial observability: reasoning and acting effectively despite missing or incomplete state observations. While recurrent (memory-based) policies learned via reinforcement learning address this by encoding history into latent state representations, their internal dynamics remain uninterpretable black boxes. This paper establishes a formal link between these hidden states and the Pontryagin minimum principle (PMP) from optimal control. We demonstrate that for standard recurrent architectures, latent representations map directly to PMP co-states, which allows the readout layer to be interpreted as performing Hamiltonian minimization. Because standard reward maximization does not naturally discover this alignment, we introduce a PMP-derived co-state loss to explicitly structure the internal dynamics. Empirically, this approach matches or improves performance on partially observable DMControl tasks, and is robust against zero-shot out-of-distribution sensor masking. By framing recurrent networks as dynamic processes governed by the minimum principle, we provide a principled approach to designing robust continuous control policies.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that hidden states in recurrent RL policies correspond to PMP co-states, enabling Hamiltonian-based interpretation and improved robustness in partially observable continuous control.
AI.motivation: Recurrent policies for partially observable RL are black boxes - their internal dynamics are uninterpretable, which limits understanding and robustness in continuous control tasks.
AI.method: Establishes formal link between hidden states and Pontryagin Minimum Principle co-states, then introduces PMP-derived co-state loss to structure internal dynamics of recurrent architectures.
AI.result: Approach matches or improves performance on partially observable DMControl tasks, shows robustness against zero-shot OOD sensor masking, and enables Hamiltonian minimization interpretation of readout layers.
AI.conclusion: Framing recurrent networks as PMP-governed processes provides principled approach to designing robust continuous control policies with interpretable internal dynamics.

title: BitCal-TTS: Bit-Calibrated Test-Time Scaling for Quantized Reasoning Models
comment: 17 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables. Code and reproducibility materials at https://github.com/Saibabu7770/bitcal-tts
summary:
Post-training quantization makes large reasoning models practical under tight memory and latency budgets, but it can distort the online signals that drive adaptive test-time compute allocation. Under a fixed cap on the number of newly generated tokens, miscalibrated confidence can lead to harmful early halting: the model may surface a plausible final line while the underlying reasoning is still wrong, or the controller may stop before the trace has stabilized. We study this interaction for greedy 4-bit inference and propose BitCal-TTS, a lightweight runtime controller that combines (i) inexpensive online proxies for token-level uncertainty and reasoning-trace stability, (ii) a bit-conditioned confidence rescaling that is conservative at low nominal precision, and (iii) a bit-aware post-marker confirmation horizon designed for GSM8K-style structured outputs. The method requires no fine-tuning of the base model and integrates with standard Hugging Face 4-bit inference using forward hooks for logits and last-layer hidden states.
  On small evaluation shards of GSM8K with Qwen2.5 Instruct models, BitCal-TTS improves exact-match accuracy over a non-bit-aware adaptive baseline at the 7B and 14B scales while preserving substantial token savings relative to fixed-budget decoding. At a token cap of B=512, on the evaluation shards we report (N=54 for 7B and N=35 for 14B; not the full GSM8K test set), accuracy gains are +3.7 points (7B) and +2.8 points (14B), with the premature-stop rate falling from 14.8% to 11.1% on 7B and from 17.1% to 11.4% on 14B. We report Wilson 95% confidence intervals throughout and explicitly discuss the limited statistical power of the partial-shard comparisons. We release code and figure-generation scripts to support full reproduction.

AI.tldr: BitCal-TTS: A lightweight runtime controller for 4-bit quantized models that prevents premature stopping during reasoning by combining uncertainty proxies, bit-aware confidence scaling, and confirmation horizons.
AI.motivation: Post-training quantization distorts online signals that drive adaptive test-time compute allocation, causing harmful early halting where models may output plausible but incorrect reasoning due to miscalibrated confidence under fixed token budgets.
AI.method: BitCal-TTS combines (1) inexpensive online proxies for token-level uncertainty and reasoning-trace stability, (2) bit-conditioned confidence rescaling that's conservative at low precision, and (3) bit-aware post-marker confirmation horizons for GSM8K-style outputs. Requires no fine-tuning and integrates with Hugging Face 4-bit inference using forward hooks.
AI.result: On GSM8K evaluation shards, BitCal-TTS improves exact-match accuracy over non-bit-aware baselines: +3.7 points for 7B and +2.8 points for 14B models at B=512 token cap. Premature-stop rate reduced from 14.8% to 11.1% (7B) and 17.1% to 11.4% (14B). Preserves substantial token savings relative to fixed-budget decoding.
AI.conclusion: BitCal-TTS effectively addresses the interaction between quantization and adaptive stopping, providing a lightweight solution that improves reasoning reliability of 4-bit quantized models without requiring model fine-tuning, though evaluations are limited to small shards and statistical power is constrained.

title: Conditional Diffusion Under Linear Constraints: Langevin Mixing and Information-Theoretic Guarantees
summary:
We study zero-shot conditional sampling with pretrained diffusion models for linear inverse problems, including inpainting and super-resolution. In these problems, the observation determines only part of the unknown signal. The remaining degrees of freedom must be sampled according to the correct conditional data distribution. Existing projection-based samplers enforce measurement consistency by correcting the observed component during reverse diffusion. However, measurement consistency alone does not determine how probability mass should be distributed along the feasible set, and this can lead to biased conditional samples.
  We analyze this issue through a normal--tangent decomposition of the score function. For Gaussian noising, the observed-direction score is exactly determined by the measurement; only the tangent conditional score is unknown. We prove that the error from replacing this score by the unconditional tangent score is upper bounded by a dimension-free conditional mutual information between observed and unobserved components. This gives an information-theoretic decomposition into initialization and pathwise score-mismatch errors. Motivated by the theory, we propose a projected-Langevin initialization followed by guided reverse denoising, which outperforms a strong projection-based baseline in inpainting and super-resolution experiments.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes and improves conditional sampling for linear inverse problems using pretrained diffusion models, proposing a projected-Langevin initialization and guided reverse denoising to address bias in existing projection-based methods.
AI.motivation: Existing projection-based samplers for zero-shot conditional sampling with diffusion models enforce measurement consistency but fail to properly distribute probability mass along the feasible set, leading to biased conditional samples for linear inverse problems like inpainting and super-resolution.
AI.method: The authors analyze the issue through a normal-tangent decomposition of the score function. For Gaussian noising, they show only the tangent conditional score is unknown. They propose a two-stage approach: 1) projected-Langevin initialization, and 2) guided reverse denoising to properly sample the conditional distribution.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows the error from using unconditional tangent score is bounded by dimension-free conditional mutual information between observed and unobserved components. Experimental results demonstrate the proposed method outperforms strong projection-based baselines in inpainting and super-resolution tasks.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides both theoretical understanding and practical improvement for conditional sampling with diffusion models in linear inverse problems, showing that proper initialization and guidance are crucial for unbiased conditional sampling beyond just enforcing measurement consistency.

title: Nonsense Helps: Prompt Space Perturbation Broadens Reasoning Exploration
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards, particularly Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), has significantly advanced the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, in complex tasks, GRPO frequently suffers from the ``zero-advantage problem'': when all sampled rollouts for a query fail, the relative advantage collapses to zero. Consequently, the model loses effective training signals for these questions, wasting the training data and computational budget. While simply increasing the sampling budget for these questions is a common remedy, the static sampling policy inherently constrains reasoning exploration, limiting the success rate. In this paper, we propose Lorem Perturbation for Exploration (LoPE), a simple yet effective training framework to break this exploration bottleneck. We posit that task-irrelevant prompt-space perturbations can shift the model's output distribution enough to unlock orthogonal reasoning pathways for hard questions. Specifically, LoPE prepends sequences stochastically assembled from Lorem Ipsum vocabulary (a pseudo-Latin placeholder text) to the prompts before resampling. Experiments across 1.7B, 4B, and 7B models demonstrate that LoPE significantly outperforms resampling with the original prompts. Further analysis reveals that other Latin-based random sequences with low perplexity are also effective perturbations. Our results establish LoPE as a strong baseline for broadening exploration in LLM reinforcement learning.

AI.tldr: LoPE (Lorem Perturbation for Exploration) is a training framework that uses task-irrelevant Latin-based prompt perturbations to solve the "zero-advantage problem" in LLM reinforcement learning, enabling better exploration of reasoning pathways for hard questions.
AI.motivation: GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) suffers from the "zero-advantage problem" where all sampled rollouts for a query fail, causing the relative advantage to collapse to zero. This wastes training data and computational budget, and simply increasing sampling budget doesn't solve the exploration bottleneck.
AI.method: LoPE prepends sequences stochastically assembled from Lorem Ipsum vocabulary (pseudo-Latin placeholder text) to prompts before resampling. This creates task-irrelevant prompt-space perturbations that shift the model's output distribution to unlock orthogonal reasoning pathways for hard questions.
AI.result: Experiments across 1.7B, 4B, and 7B models show LoPE significantly outperforms resampling with original prompts. Further analysis reveals other Latin-based random sequences with low perplexity are also effective perturbations.
AI.conclusion: LoPE establishes a strong baseline for broadening exploration in LLM reinforcement learning by solving the zero-advantage problem through simple yet effective prompt perturbations that enhance reasoning exploration.

title: Two-Stage Learned Decomposition for Scalable Routing on Multigraphs
summary: Most neural methods for Vehicle Routing Problems (VRPs) are limited to Euclidean settings or simple graphs. In this work, we instead consider multigraphs, where parallel edges represent distinct travel options with varying trade-offs (e.g., distance vs time). Few methods are designed for such formulations and those that do exist face major scalability issues. We mitigate these scalability issues via a Node-Edge Policy Factorization (NEPF) approach, which splits the routing policy into a node permutation stage and an edge selection stage. To enable the decomposition, we introduce a pre-encoding edge aggregation scheme and a non-autoregressive architecture for the edge stage, as well as a hierarchical reinforcement learning method to train the stages jointly. Our experiments across six VRP variants demonstrate that NEPF matches or outperforms the state-of-the-art in terms of solution quality, while being significantly faster in training and inference.

AI.tldr: NEPF (Node-Edge Policy Factorization) is a neural method for Vehicle Routing Problems on multigraphs that splits routing policy into node permutation and edge selection stages for scalability, matching or outperforming SOTA with faster training/inference.
AI.motivation: Most neural VRP methods work only on Euclidean settings or simple graphs, but not on multigraphs where parallel edges represent distinct travel options with varying trade-offs (distance vs time). Existing methods for multigraphs face major scalability issues.
AI.method: Node-Edge Policy Factorization (NEPF) approach splits routing policy into: 1) node permutation stage, and 2) edge selection stage. Uses pre-encoding edge aggregation scheme, non-autoregressive architecture for edge stage, and hierarchical reinforcement learning to train stages jointly.
AI.result: Experiments across six VRP variants show NEPF matches or outperforms state-of-the-art in solution quality while being significantly faster in both training and inference.
AI.conclusion: NEPF effectively addresses scalability issues in neural VRP methods for multigraphs, providing a practical approach that maintains solution quality while dramatically improving computational efficiency.

title: Locality-aware Private Class Identification for Domain Adaptation with Extreme Label Shift
summary: Domain adaptation aims to transfer knowledge from a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain with different distributions. In real-world scenarios, the label spaces of the two domains often have an inclusion relationship, where some classes exist only in one domain but not the other. These non-overlapping classes are referred to as private classes. Identifying private class samples and mitigating their adverse effects is critical in the literature. Existing methods rely on the assumption that shifts in private classes are large enough to be considered outliers. However, the variance within a single shared class can be significantly larger than the difference between a private class and another shared class, challenging this assumption. Consequently, private classes substantially increase the difficulty of cross-domain classification. To address these issues, based on local transportation and metric properties of optimal transport (OT), a locality-aware private class identification approach is proposed in the form of a score function on transport mass. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is theoretically proven, highlighting the score function's strong ability to distinguish between shared and private class samples. Building on this, we introduce a reliable OT-based method (ReOT) for domain adaptation under severe label shift. ReOT minimizes classification risk while learning the separated cluster structure between the identified shared classes and private classes, effectively avoiding mismatch between shared-private sample pairs, thus ensuring that important knowledge is reliably transported intra-class to mitigate class-conditional discrepancy. Furthermore, a generalization upper bound of the target risk is provided for extreme label shift scenarios, which can be minimized by ReOT. Extensive experiments on benchmarks validate the effectiveness of ReOT.

AI.tldr: A new domain adaptation method called ReOT that identifies private classes via local optimal transport and mitigates their negative effects to handle severe label shifts between domains.
AI.motivation: Domain adaptation faces challenges when source and target domains have non-overlapping private classes. Existing methods assume private classes are outliers with large shifts, but in reality, variance within shared classes can exceed differences between private and shared classes, making private class identification difficult and worsening cross-domain classification.
AI.method: Proposes a locality-aware private class identification approach using optimal transport (OT) properties. Develops a score function on transport mass to distinguish shared vs. private classes. Introduces ReOT method that minimizes classification risk while learning separated cluster structures between identified shared and private classes, avoiding mismatch between shared-private sample pairs and ensuring reliable intra-class knowledge transport.
AI.result: Theoretical proof shows the score function effectively distinguishes shared and private classes. ReOT is validated through extensive experiments on benchmarks. Provides generalization upper bound for target risk in extreme label shift scenarios, which ReOT can minimize.
AI.conclusion: ReOT effectively addresses domain adaptation under severe label shift by reliably identifying private classes and transporting knowledge within shared classes, outperforming existing methods and handling challenging cases where traditional assumptions fail.

title: Differentiable Parameter Optimization for DAEs with State-Dependent Events
summary: Differential-algebraic equations (DAEs) with state-dependent events arise in systems whose continuous dynamics are constrained by algebraic equations and interrupted by mode changes, switching logic, impacts, or state reinitializations. Gradient-based parameter learning for such systems is challenging because algebraic variables are implicitly defined, event times depend on the parameters, and reset maps introduce discontinuities. This paper studies differentiable parameter optimization for semi-explicit DAEs with events. We formulate the learning problem as a constrained least-squares problem with DAE dynamics, algebraic constraints, guard equations, and reset maps. We then develop two complementary gradient-computation strategies. The first is an automatic-differentiation-through-simulation method that solves algebraic variables inside the vector field, differentiates the algebraic solve using the implicit function theorem, and handles events through segmented differentiable integration. The second is an explicit discrete-adjoint method that represents the forward simulation as an event-split residual system and computes gradients by solving for the Lagrange multipliers of smooth-segment and event residuals. The formulation clarifies that residual terms in the adjoint method are equality constraints, not heuristic penalties. We compare the two approaches in terms of gradient interpretation, event-time handling, implementation complexity, and local validity. Both methods provide gradients for the event path selected by the forward simulation and are valid under fixed event ordering and transversal guard crossings.

AI.tldr: This paper develops two differentiable gradient computation methods for parameter learning in semi-explicit differential-algebraic equations (DAEs) with state-dependent events, addressing challenges with algebraic variables, event timing, and discontinuities.
AI.motivation: Gradient-based parameter learning for DAEs with state-dependent events is challenging due to: (1) algebraic variables being implicitly defined, (2) event times depending on parameters, and (3) reset maps introducing discontinuities. These difficulties motivate developing systematic gradient computation methods for such hybrid systems.
AI.method: Two complementary gradient computation strategies: (1) Automatic-differentiation-through-simulation that solves algebraic variables via implicit function theorem and handles events through segmented differentiable integration. (2) Explicit discrete-adjoint method that represents forward simulation as event-split residual system and computes gradients by solving for Lagrange multipliers of smooth-segment and event residuals.
AI.result: Both methods successfully provide gradients for event paths selected by forward simulation. The formulation clarifies that residual terms in adjoint method are equality constraints rather than heuristic penalties. The approaches are valid under fixed event ordering and transversal guard crossings.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents systematic differentiable parameter optimization for semi-explicit DAEs with events, comparing two gradient computation approaches in terms of gradient interpretation, event-time handling, implementation complexity, and local validity, advancing gradient-based learning for hybrid constrained systems.

title: AlphaCrafter: A Full-Stack Multi-Agent Framework for Cross-Sectional Quantitative Trading
summary: Financial markets are inherently non-stationary, driven by complex interactions among macroeconomic regimes, microstructural frictions, and behavioral dynamics. Building quantitative strategies that remain profitable demands the continuous coupling of factor discovery, regime-adaptive selection, and risk-constrained execution. Prevailing approaches, however, optimize these components under static or isolated assumptions. Factor mining frameworks typically treat alpha discovery as a one-time search process, implicitly assuming that factor efficacy persists across market regimes. Execution-oriented systems often adopt role-playing agent architectures that simulate anthropomorphic trading committees, introducing behavioral noise rather than systematic rationality. Consequently, a fully automated, rationality-driven framework unifying a coherent quantitative pipeline remains absent. We introduce AlphaCrafter, a full-stack multi-agent framework that closes this gap through a continuously adaptive factor-to-execution pipeline, designed to track and respond to evolving market conditions without manual intervention. AlphaCrafter operates via three specialized agents: a Miner that continuously expands the factor pool via LLM-guided search, a Screener that assesses prevailing market conditions to construct regime-conditioned factor ensembles, and a Trader that translates these ensembles into quantitative strategies under explicit risk constraints. Together, these three agents form a closed-loop cross-sectional trading system that adapts holistically to evolving market dynamics. Extensive experiments on CSI 300 and S&P 500 demonstrate that AlphaCrafter consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in risk-adjusted returns while exhibiting the lowest cross-trial variance, confirming that integrated and adaptive factor-to-execution design yields robust trading performance.

AI.tldr: AlphaCrafter is an automated multi-agent framework that unifies factor discovery, regime-adaptive selection, and risk-constrained execution in a closed-loop system for quantitative trading.
AI.motivation: Current approaches in quantitative trading optimize components under static assumptions - factor mining assumes persistent efficacy across regimes, while execution systems use anthropomorphic agents introducing behavioral noise rather than systematic rationality. There's a need for a fully automated, rationality-driven framework that unifies the entire quantitative pipeline.
AI.method: AlphaCrafter uses three specialized agents: 1) Miner continuously expands factor pool via LLM-guided search, 2) Screener assesses market conditions to construct regime-conditioned factor ensembles, 3) Trader translates ensembles into quantitative strategies under explicit risk constraints. Together they form a closed-loop cross-sectional trading system.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on CSI 300 and S&P 500 show AlphaCrafter consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in risk-adjusted returns while exhibiting the lowest cross-trial variance.
AI.conclusion: An integrated and adaptive factor-to-execution design yields robust trading performance, addressing the limitations of existing fragmented approaches in quantitative finance.

title: Information Theoretic Adversarial Training of Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) remain vulnerable to adversarial prompting despite advances in alignment and safety, often exhibiting harmful behaviors under novel attack strategies. While adversarial training can improve robustness, existing approaches are computationally expensive and difficult to scale. Recent continuous adversarial training methods, such as Continuous adversarial training (CAT) and Continuous Adversarial Preference Optimization (CAPO), address this challenge by leveraging gradient-based perturbations in the embedding space, enabling more efficient and expressive attacks. Building on this paradigm, we propose WARDEN, a distributionally robust adversarial training framework for LLMs that dynamically reweights adversarial examples through an f -divergence ambiguity set around the empirical training distribution. Our method optimizes the worst-case adversarial loss within a divergence ball around the empirical data distribution, automatically emphasizing harder adversarial examples. Using the convex dual formulation, the objective reduces to a log-sum-exp form under the KL divergence, with a dynamical parameter controlling the strength of reweighting. This study leads to a new class of information-theoretic objectives that significantly reduce attack success rates while maintaining model utility. Across multiple LLMs and attack settings, WARDEN substantially reduces attack success rates with computational and utility costs comparable to CAT-, CAPO-, and MixAT-based baselines, making it a practical approach for scalable robust alignment.

AI.tldr: WARDEN is a distributionally robust adversarial training framework for LLMs that optimizes worst-case adversarial loss using f-divergence ambiguity sets, significantly reducing attack success rates while maintaining computational efficiency and model utility.
AI.motivation: LLMs remain vulnerable to adversarial prompting despite safety improvements, and existing adversarial training methods are computationally expensive and difficult to scale. There's a need for more efficient and effective robustness techniques.
AI.method: Proposes WARDEN - a distributionally robust adversarial training framework that dynamically reweights adversarial examples through an f-divergence ambiguity set around the empirical training distribution. Uses convex dual formulation to optimize worst-case adversarial loss within a divergence ball, reducing to a log-sum-exp form under KL divergence with dynamic parameter control.
AI.result: WARDEN significantly reduces attack success rates across multiple LLMs and attack settings, with computational and utility costs comparable to existing baselines (CAT-, CAPO-, and MixAT-based methods).
AI.conclusion: WARDEN represents a practical approach for scalable robust alignment of LLMs, introducing a new class of information-theoretic objectives that effectively balance robustness with computational efficiency and model utility.

title: Belief Memory: Agent Memory Under Partial Observability
summary: LLM agents that operate over long context depend on external memory to accumulate knowledge over time. However, existing methods typically store each observation as a single deterministic conclusion (e.g., inferring "API~X failed" from temporary errors), even though such observations are inherently partial and potentially ambiguous. By committing to one conclusion and discarding uncertainty, these methods introduce self-reinforcing error: the agent acts on the stored conclusion, never revisits alternatives, and reinforces the conclusion over time. To address this issue, we propose BeliefMem, which shifts the memory paradigm from committing to a single conclusion per observation to retaining multiple candidate conclusions with their probabilities. Concretely, BeliefMem stores the candidate conclusions as separate memory entries, each carrying a probability that is updated via Noisy-OR rules as new observations arrive. At retrieval, all candidates surface together with their probabilities, keeping alternatives visible to the agent. Since each conclusion in memory retains its probability, BeliefMem preserves the uncertainty that the deterministic paradigm discards, enabling the agent to act with high confidence on well-evidenced knowledge while retaining the capacity to update its confidence when new evidence arrives. Empirical evaluations on LoCoMo and ALFWorld benchmarks show that, even with limited data, BeliefMem achieves the best average performance, remarkably outperforming well-known baselines. More broadly, such probabilistic memory produces substantial gains and explores a new direction for agent memory in partially observable environments.

AI.tldr: BeliefMem is a probabilistic memory system for LLM agents that stores multiple candidate conclusions with probabilities instead of single deterministic facts, addressing self-reinforcing errors in partially observable environments.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agent memory systems store each observation as a single deterministic conclusion, which introduces self-reinforcing errors when observations are partial or ambiguous. By committing to one conclusion and discarding uncertainty, agents act on potentially wrong information and never revisit alternatives, leading to error reinforcement over time.
AI.method: BeliefMem shifts from storing single conclusions to retaining multiple candidate conclusions with probabilities. It stores candidates as separate memory entries, each with a probability updated via Noisy-OR rules as new observations arrive. During retrieval, all candidates surface together with their probabilities, keeping alternatives visible to the agent.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on LoCoMo and ALFWorld benchmarks show BeliefMem achieves the best average performance even with limited data, remarkably outperforming well-known baselines. The probabilistic memory approach produces substantial gains in agent performance.
AI.conclusion: BeliefMem explores a new direction for agent memory in partially observable environments by preserving uncertainty that deterministic paradigms discard, enabling agents to act with confidence on well-evidenced knowledge while retaining the capacity to update confidence when new evidence arrives.

title: Active Learning for Conditional Generative Compressed Sensing
summary: Generative compressed sensing uses the range of a pretrained generator as a nonlinear model for recovering structured signals from limited measurements. We study a conditional version of this problem for image recovery from subsampled Fourier measurements using prompt-conditioned generative models. Our framework separates two roles of conditioning: the prompt used to design the sampling distribution and the prompt used to define the recovery model. For ReLU and Lipschitz conditional generators, we prove stable recovery bounds showing that prompt-matched Christoffel sampling retains the same Christoffel complexity constant as existing near-optimal generative compressed sensing theory, while prompt mismatch incurs an explicit compatibility penalty. Experiments with Stable Diffusion show that prompts meaningfully reshape Christoffel sampling distributions and influence image recovery. Overall, our results suggest that prompts should be treated as design variables with distinct effects on sensing, approximation, and recovery.

AI.tldr: A framework for conditional generative compressed sensing that separates sampling distribution prompts from recovery model prompts, with theoretical bounds showing prompt-matched sampling retains optimal complexity while mismatched prompts incur compatibility penalties.
AI.motivation: To develop a conditional version of generative compressed sensing for image recovery from limited Fourier measurements, addressing how different prompts should be used for designing sampling distributions versus defining recovery models in prompt-conditioned generative systems.
AI.method: The framework separates two conditioning roles: prompt for designing sampling distribution and prompt for defining recovery model. Uses prompt-conditioned generative models with ReLU and Lipschitz generators, analyzes prompt-matched Christoffel sampling, and evaluates prompt mismatch effects. Experimental validation with Stable Diffusion.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows prompt-matched Christoffel sampling retains the same Christoffel complexity constant as existing near-optimal generative compressed sensing theory. Prompt mismatch incurs an explicit compatibility penalty. Experiments demonstrate prompts meaningfully reshape Christoffel sampling distributions and influence image recovery.
AI.conclusion: Prompts should be treated as design variables with distinct effects on sensing, approximation, and recovery in conditional generative compressed sensing systems, with prompt-matched sampling maintaining optimal theoretical guarantees while mismatched prompts degrade performance through quantifiable compatibility penalties.

title: Causal Probing for Internal Visual Representations in Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Despite the remarkable success of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) across diverse tasks, the internal mechanisms governing how they encode and ground distinct visual concepts remain poorly understood. To bridge this gap, we propose a causal framework based on activation steering to actively probe and manipulate internal visual representations. Through systematic intervention across four visual concept categories, our results reveal a divergence in concept encoding: entities exhibit distinct localized memorization, whereas abstract concepts are globally distributed across the network. Critically, this divergence uncovers a mechanistic driver of scaling laws: increasing model depth is indispensable for encoding distributed and complex abstract concepts, whereas entity localization remains remarkably invariant to scale. Furthermore, reverse steering uncovers that blocking explicit output triggers a surge in latent activations, exposing a compensatory mechanism between perception and generation. Finally, extending our analysis to visual reasoning, we expose a disconnect between perception and reasoning although MLLMs successfully recognize geometric relations, they treat them merely as static visual features, failing to trigger the procedural execution necessary for abstract problem-solving.

AI.tldr: MLLMs encode visual concepts differently: entities are locally memorized while abstract concepts are globally distributed across layers. Scaling benefits abstract concepts more, and there's a perception-reasoning disconnect.
AI.motivation: Despite MLLMs' success, there's poor understanding of how they internally encode and ground different visual concepts. The researchers want to understand the mechanistic differences between how entities vs. abstract concepts are represented.
AI.method: Propose a causal framework using activation steering to probe and manipulate internal visual representations. Systematic interventions across four visual concept categories.
AI.result: 1) Divergent encoding: entities show localized memorization, abstract concepts are globally distributed. 2) Scaling laws driver: model depth is crucial for abstract concepts while entity localization is scale-invariant. 3) Reverse steering reveals compensatory mechanism between perception/generation. 4) Visual reasoning disconnect: MLLMs treat geometric relations as static features rather than triggering procedural reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The paper reveals fundamental differences in how MLLMs encode different visual concepts, explains scaling law mechanisms, exposes perception-generation tradeoffs, and identifies a critical gap between perception and reasoning in visual problem-solving.

title: On Semantic Loss Fine-Tuning Approach for Preventing Model Collapse in Causal Reasoning
summary: Standard fine-tuning of transformer models on causal reasoning tasks leads to catastrophic model collapse, where models learn trivial solutions such as always predicting "Yes" or "No" regardless of input structure. We demonstrate that fine-tuning Gemma 270M on transitivity and d-separation tasks without semantic loss results in 100% collapse rate, with models achieving misleadingly high accuracy (73.9%) while learning no causal reasoning. We propose a semantic loss function with graph-based logical constraints and dynamic lambda scheduling that prevents this collapse. Our approach achieves 70.4% accuracy on transitivity tasks and 68.6% on d-separation tasks with stable, context-dependent predictions, representing a 42.7% improvement over collapsed baselines. Adversarial evaluation on 1,000 structural reasoning samples shows semantic models achieve 67-70% accuracy while collapsed models fail catastrophically at 43-71%. We validate our findings through comprehensive benchmarking on 200,000+ evaluation samples across five model variants, demonstrating that semantic loss is essential and not optional, for stable causal reasoning in transformers.

AI.tldr: Standard fine-tuning causes catastrophic collapse in causal reasoning models; proposed semantic loss with graph constraints prevents collapse and enables stable reasoning.
AI.motivation: Standard transformer fine-tuning on causal reasoning tasks leads to catastrophic model collapse where models learn trivial solutions like always predicting "Yes" or "No" instead of learning actual causal reasoning.
AI.method: Proposed semantic loss function with graph-based logical constraints and dynamic lambda scheduling to prevent model collapse; comprehensive evaluation on 200,000+ samples across five model variants.
AI.result: Semantic loss prevents 100% collapse rate seen in baselines; achieves 70.4% accuracy on transitivity and 68.6% on d-separation tasks (42.7% improvement); adversarial evaluation shows 67-70% accuracy vs. collapsed models' 43-71% catastrophic failure.
AI.conclusion: Semantic loss is essential, not optional, for stable causal reasoning in transformers; prevents trivial solutions and enables models to learn actual causal structure rather than exploiting statistical patterns.

title: Prober.ai: Gated Inquiry-Based Feedback via LLM-Constrained Personas for Argumentative Writing Development
comment: Prototype awarded second place at the NYEdTech Hackathon (March 2026) https://www.nyedtechhackathon.com/2026-submissions
summary: The proliferation of large language models (LLMs) in educational settings has paradoxically undermined the cognitive processes they purport to support. Students increasingly outsource critical thinking to AI assistants that generate polished text on demand, resulting in measurable cognitive debt and diminished argumentative reasoning skills. We present Prober.ai, a web-based writing environment that inverts the conventional AI-tutoring paradigm: rather than generating or rewriting student text, the system constrains an LLM (Gemini 3 Flash Preview) through persona-specific system prompts and structured JSON output schemas to produce only targeted, inquiry-based questions about argumentative weaknesses. A two-phase interaction architecture -- Challenge and Unlock -- implements a pedagogical friction mechanism whereby revision suggestions are gated behind mandatory student reflection. The system's design is grounded in Toulmin's argumentation theory, research on peer feedforward questioning mechanisms, and evidence on AI-supported feedback in writing instruction. A functional prototype was developed in 36 hours during the NY EdTech Hackathon (March 2026), where it was awarded second place. We describe the system architecture, the prompt engineering methodology for constraining LLM output to pedagogically aligned JSON schemas, and discuss implications for scalable, cognition-preserving AI integration in writing education.

AI.tldr: Prober.ai is an AI writing tool that uses constrained LLMs to ask targeted questions about argumentative weaknesses rather than generating text, implementing pedagogical friction through a Challenge-Unlock architecture to preserve student cognition.
AI.motivation: Current AI tools in education (especially LLMs) undermine critical thinking by allowing students to outsource cognitive work to AI assistants, leading to cognitive debt and diminished argumentative reasoning skills.
AI.method: Developed Prober.ai, a web-based writing environment that constrains Gemini 3 Flash Preview LLM through persona-specific system prompts and structured JSON output schemas to produce only targeted, inquiry-based questions. Uses a two-phase Challenge-Unlock architecture that gates revision suggestions behind mandatory student reflection.
AI.result: Created a functional prototype in 36 hours during the NY EdTech Hackathon (March 2026) where it won second place. The system successfully implements a pedagogical friction mechanism based on Toulmin's argumentation theory, peer feedforward questioning, and AI-supported feedback research.
AI.conclusion: Prober.ai demonstrates a scalable approach to cognition-preserving AI integration in writing education by inverting the conventional AI-tutoring paradigm, using constrained questioning rather than text generation to maintain student cognitive engagement.

title: Robustness of Graph Self-Supervised Learning to Real-World Noise: A Case Study on Text-Driven Biomedical Graphs
summary: Graph Self-Supervised Learning (GSSL) offers a powerful paradigm for learning graph representations without labeled data. However, existing work assumes clean, manually curated graphs. Recent advances in NLP enable the large-scale automatic extraction of knowledge graphs from text, opening new opportunities for GSSL while introducing substantial real-world noise. This type of noise remains largely unexplored, as prior robustness studies typically rely on synthetic perturbations. To address this gap, we present the first comprehensive evaluation of GSSL methods on text-driven graphs for unsupervised term typing. We introduce Noise-Aware Text-Driven Graph GSSL (NATD-GSSL), a unified framework that combines automatic graph construction, graph refinement, and GSSL. Our evaluation follows a dual-graph protocol that contrasts a noisy graph derived from MedMentions with a clean Unified Medical Language System (UMLS) reference graph, aligned through a shared gold standard. Our results reveal variability in robustness across both pretext tasks and Graph Neural Network (GNN) architectures. Relation reconstruction is highly sensitive to noise and benefits from well-defined schemas, whereas feature reconstruction is considerably more robust, achieving performance comparable to clean-graph settings. Contrastive objectives are generally less affected by noise but depend strongly on alignment with downstream tasks. GNN architecture also plays a critical role: bidirectional relational message-passing designs are better suited to noisy, text-driven graphs, while unidirectional relational ones perform best on clean graphs. Overall, NATD-GSSL provides practical guidance for applying GSSL to real-world, noisy graphs and achieves up to a 7\% improvement over pretrained language model baselines. All code and benchmarks are publicly available at https://github.com/OthmaneKabal/MC2GAE.

AI.tldr: Graph Self-Supervised Learning for unsupervised term typing using text-driven graphs, with a focus on robustness to real-world noise through a dual-graph evaluation protocol comparing noisy vs. clean graphs.
AI.motivation: Existing GSSL methods assume clean, manually curated graphs, but recent NLP advances enable automatic extraction of knowledge graphs from text, introducing substantial real-world noise. This type of noise remains unexplored as prior robustness studies rely on synthetic perturbations.
AI.method: NATD-GSSL framework combining automatic graph construction, graph refinement, and GSSL. Uses dual-graph protocol comparing noisy graph from MedMentions with clean UMLS reference graph, aligned through shared gold standard. Evaluates various pretext tasks and GNN architectures.
AI.result: 1) Pretext task matters: relation reconstruction is highly sensitive to noise and benefits from well-defined schemas; feature reconstruction is more robust; contrastive objectives are less affected but depend on task alignment. 2) GNN architecture matters: bidirectional relational message-passing performs better on noisy graphs; unidirectional relational ones work best on clean graphs. 3) NATD-GSSL achieves up to 7% improvement over pretrained language model baselines.
AI.conclusion: The study provides practical guidance for applying GSSL to real-world, noisy graphs, highlighting that different pretext tasks and architectures exhibit varying robustness to noise. The findings can inform better design choices when working with automatically extracted text-driven graphs.

title: Text-Graph Synergy: A Bidirectional Verification and Completion Framework for RAG
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become a core paradigm for enhancing factual grounding and multi-hop reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs). Traditional text-based RAG often retrieves logically irrelevant pseudo-evidence, while graph-based RAG is frequently hindered by search-time pruning, which may discard potentially valid reasoning paths. Existing hybrid approaches primarily adopt simple evidence concatenation or unidirectional enhancement, which fails to address the fundamental "Information Island" problem caused by asymmetric reasoning flows between unstructured text and structured graphs. We propose \textbf{TGS-RAG}, a unified framework for \textbf{T}ext-\textbf{G}raph \textbf{S}ynergistic enhancement. TGS-RAG introduces a bidirectional mechanism: (i) a \textbf{Graph-to-Text} channel that employs a Global Voting strategy from visited graph nodes to re-rank and refine textual evidence, filtering out semantic noise; and (ii) a \textbf{Text-to-Graph} channel that utilizes the \textbf{Memory-based Orphan Entity Bridging} algorithm. This algorithm utilizes textual cues to proactively resurrect valid but previously pruned reasoning paths from the search history without additional database overhead. Experimental results on multiple multi-hop reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that TGS-RAG significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a superior balance between retrieval precision and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: TGS-RAG is a new framework that synergistically enhances text and graph retrieval for RAG systems using bidirectional channels to address the "Information Island" problem and improve multi-hop reasoning.
AI.motivation: Current RAG systems have limitations: text-based RAG often retrieves irrelevant evidence, graph-based RAG suffers from search-time pruning that discards valid reasoning paths, and existing hybrid approaches fail to address the "Information Island" problem caused by asymmetric reasoning flows between unstructured text and structured graphs.
AI.method: TGS-RAG introduces a bidirectional synergistic framework with: 1) Graph-to-Text channel using Global Voting strategy to re-rank and refine textual evidence by filtering semantic noise, and 2) Text-to-Graph channel using Memory-based Orphan Entity Bridging algorithm to proactively resurrect valid but previously pruned reasoning paths from search history without additional database overhead.
AI.result: Experimental results on multiple multi-hop reasoning benchmarks show that TGS-RAG significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving superior balance between retrieval precision and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: TGS-RAG effectively addresses the limitations of traditional RAG approaches by synergistically enhancing text and graph evidence through bidirectional channels, solving the "Information Island" problem and improving multi-hop reasoning performance.

title: A Unified Benchmark for Evaluating Knowledge Graph Construction Methods and Graph Neural Networks
summary: Knowledge graphs automatically constructed from text are increasingly used in real-world applications. However, their inherent noise, fragmentation, and semantic inconsistencies significantly affect the performance of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) on downstream tasks. Assessing their performance and robustness remains difficult, as it is often unclear whether observed results stem from the learning model or from the quality of the constructed graph itself. In this work, we introduce a dual-purpose benchmark designed to jointly evaluate (i) the performance of GNNs on noisy, text-derived graphs and (ii) the effectiveness of graph construction methods on a downstream task. The benchmark is built in the biomedical domain from a single textual corpus and includes two automatically constructed graphs generated using different extraction methods, alongside a high-quality reference graph curated by experts that serves as an upper performance bound. This design enables controlled comparison of construction methods and systematic evaluation of GNN robustness through semi-supervised node classification. We further provide a standardized, reproducible, and extensible evaluation framework, facilitating the integration of new graph extraction methods and learning models.

AI.tldr: A benchmark for evaluating Graph Neural Networks on noisy knowledge graphs from text, plus assessing graph construction methods in biomedical domain.
AI.motivation: Knowledge graphs from text are noisy/fragmented/inconsistent, affecting GNN performance; hard to tell if poor results come from learning models or graph quality itself.
AI.method: Create benchmark with two automatically constructed graphs from different extraction methods, plus expert-curated reference graph as upper bound; use semi-supervised node classification for systematic evaluation.
AI.result: Provides standardized, reproducible, extensible framework for comparing construction methods and evaluating GNN robustness on noisy text-derived graphs.
AI.conclusion: Benchmark enables controlled comparison of graph construction methods and systematic evaluation of GNN robustness, facilitating better assessment in real-world applications.

title: GRALIS: A Unified Canonical Framework for Linear Attribution Methods via Riesz Representation
comment: 25 pages, 6 tables, 2 figures. Theoretical framework with preliminary experimental validation on BreaKHis (1,187 images, DenseNet-121). Extended empirical comparison in preparation
summary: The main XAI attribution methods for deep neural networks -- GradCAM, SHAP, LIME, Integrated Gradients -- operate on separate theoretical foundations and are not formally comparable. We present GRALIS (Gradient-Riesz Averaged Locally-Integrated Shapley), a mathematical framework establishing a representation theory for attributions: every additive, linear, and continuous attribution functional on L^2(Q,mu) admits a unique canonical representation (Q, w, Delta), proved necessary by the Riesz Representation Theorem. This class encompasses SHAP, IG, LIME and linearized GradCAM, but excludes nonlinear functionals such as standard GradCAM or attention maps. Seven formal theorems provide simultaneous guarantees absent in any individual method: (T1) necessary canonical form; (T2) exact completeness; (T3) Monte Carlo convergence O(1/sqrt(m))+O(1/k); (T4) exact Shapley Interaction Values; (T5) Hoeffding ANOVA decomposition; (T6) Sobol sensitivity generalization; (T7) multi-scale extension (MS-GRALIS) with minimum-variance weights. An algebraic appendix justifies the GRALIS-SIV correspondence via the Mobius transform without circularity. GRALIS satisfies 13.5/14 axiomatic properties vs. 2.5-6/14 for individual methods, including completeness, sensitivity, locality, order-k interactions and optimal multi-scale aggregation simultaneously. Preliminary validation on BreaKHis (1,187 histology images, DenseNet-121) reports deletion faithfulness AUC +0.015 (malignant), 96% class-conditional consistency, SAL = 0.762+/-0.109 and sparsity index 0.39. Extended comparison with baseline XAI methods is planned for a companion paper.

AI.tldr: GRALIS is a unified mathematical framework that establishes a representation theory for XAI attribution methods, proving all additive, linear, continuous attribution functionals have a unique canonical form, enabling formal comparison and providing seven theoretical guarantees that individual methods lack.
AI.motivation: Current XAI attribution methods (GradCAM, SHAP, LIME, Integrated Gradients) operate on separate theoretical foundations and are not formally comparable, making it difficult to systematically evaluate and compare attribution approaches.
AI.method: Proposed GRALIS (Gradient-Riesz Averaged Locally-Integrated Shapley) framework based on Riesz Representation Theorem, establishing that every additive, linear, continuous attribution functional on L^2(Q,mu) admits unique canonical representation (Q, w, Delta). The framework includes extensions like multi-scale GRALIS with minimum-variance weights and provides algebraic justification via Mobius transform.
AI.result: GRALIS satisfies 13.5/14 axiomatic properties vs. 2.5-6/14 for individual methods, includes seven formal theorems with guarantees like canonical form, exact completeness, Monte Carlo convergence, exact Shapley Interaction Values, ANOVA decomposition, Sobol sensitivity generalization, and multi-scale extension. Preliminary validation on BreaKHis dataset shows improved deletion faithfulness AUC (+0.015), 96% class-conditional consistency, SAL=0.762±0.109 and sparsity index 0.39.
AI.conclusion: GRALIS provides a unified mathematical foundation for XAI attribution methods, enabling formal comparison and offering comprehensive theoretical guarantees that individual methods cannot achieve, representing a significant advance in the formalization and evaluation of attribution techniques in deep learning.

title: Large Vision-Language Models Get Lost in Attention
summary: Despite the rapid evolution of training paradigms, the decoder backbone of large vision--language models (LVLMs) remains fundamentally rooted in the residual-connection Transformer architecture. Therefore, deciphering the distinct roles of internal modules is critical for understanding model mechanics and guiding architectural optimization. While prior statistical approaches have provided valuable attribution-based insights, they often lack a unified theoretical basis. To bridge this gap, we propose a unified framework grounded in information theory and geometry to quantify the geometric and entropic nature of residual updates. Applying this unified framework reveals a fundamental functional decoupling: Attention acts as a subspace-preserving operator focused on reconfiguration, whereas FFNs serve as subspace-expanding operators driving semantic innovation. Strikingly, further experiments demonstrate that replacing learned attention weights with predefined values (e.g., Gaussian noise) yields comparable or even superior performance across a majority of datasets relative to vanilla models. These results expose severe misallocation and redundancy in current mechanisms, suggesting that state-of-the-art LVLMs effectively ``get lost in attention'' rather than efficiently leveraging visual context.

AI.tldr: Attention in LVLMs serves as subspace-preserving reconfiguration while FFNs drive semantic innovation, but attention weights are often redundant and can be replaced with predefined values without performance loss.
AI.motivation: To understand the distinct roles of internal modules in large vision-language models (LVLMs) and guide architectural optimization, as current approaches lack a unified theoretical basis for analyzing residual Transformer architectures.
AI.method: Proposes a unified framework grounded in information theory and geometry to quantify the geometric and entropic nature of residual updates in Transformer decoders, applying this framework to analyze attention and FFN modules.
AI.result: Reveals fundamental functional decoupling: attention acts as subspace-preserving operator for reconfiguration, while FFNs serve as subspace-expanding operators driving semantic innovation. Experiments show learned attention weights can be replaced with predefined values (e.g., Gaussian noise) with comparable or superior performance across most datasets.
AI.conclusion: Current LVLMs suffer from severe misallocation and redundancy in attention mechanisms, effectively "getting lost in attention" rather than efficiently leveraging visual context, suggesting attention weights may not be as critical as assumed and highlighting potential architectural optimizations.

title: Approximate Next Policy Sampling: Replacing Conservative Target Policy Updates in Deep RL
summary: We revisit a classic "chicken-and-egg" problem in reinforcement learning: to safely improve a policy, the value function must be accurate on the state-visitation distribution of the updated policy. That distribution over states is unknown and cannot be sampled for the purposes of training the value function. Conservative updates solve this problem, but at the cost of shrinking the policy update. This paper explores an alternative solution, Approximate Next Policy Sampling (ANPS), which addresses the problem by modifying the training distribution rather than constraining the policy update. ANPS is satisfied if the distribution of the training data approximates that of the next policy. To demonstrate the feasibility and efficacy of ANPS, we introduce Stable Value Approximate Policy Iteration (SV-API). SV-API modifies the standard approximate policy iteration loop to hold the target policy fixed while an iteratively updated behavioral policy gathers relevant experience. It only commits to a new policy once a convergence criterion has been met. If certain stability criteria are met, the update is guaranteed to be safe; otherwise, it remains no less safe than standard approximate policy iteration. Applying SV-API to PPO yields Stable Value PPO (SV-PPO), which matches or improves performance on high-dimensional discrete (Atari) and continuous control benchmarks while executing substantially larger target policy updates. These results demonstrate the viability of ANPS as a new solution to this classic challenge in RL.

AI.tldr: ANPS modifies training distribution instead of policy constraints to solve RL's chicken-and-egg problem, enabling larger policy updates while maintaining safety.
AI.motivation: To solve the classic reinforcement learning problem where value functions need accuracy on unknown future state distributions, without resorting to conservative policy updates that limit improvement.
AI.method: Introduces Approximate Next Policy Sampling (ANPS) and Stable Value Approximate Policy Iteration (SV-API), which keeps target policy fixed while behavioral policy collects experience, only updating policy after convergence criteria met.
AI.result: SV-PPO (PPO with SV-API) matches or improves performance on Atari and continuous control benchmarks while executing substantially larger target policy updates.
AI.conclusion: ANPS provides a viable alternative to conservative updates for solving the distribution mismatch problem in RL, enabling safer and more aggressive policy improvements.

title: Chain of Risk: Safety Failures in Large Reasoning Models and Mitigation via Adaptive Multi-Principle Steering
summary: Large reasoning models (LRMs) increasingly expose chain-of-thought-like reasoning for transparency, verification, and deliberate problem solving. This creates a safety blind spot: harmful or policy-violating content may appear in reasoning traces even when final answers appear safe. We test whether final-answer safety is a sufficient proxy for the full reasoning-answer trajectory by scoring both stages under a unified twenty-principle safety rubric. Using prompts from seven public harmfulness and jailbreak sources, plus four out-of-distribution (OOD) sources, we evaluate 15 open-weight and API-based LRMs across 41K prompts per model. Reasoning traces consistently reveal additional safety risks beyond final answers, especially in high-severity stage-wise failures: leak cases, where unsafe reasoning precedes a safe-looking answer, and escape cases, where benign-looking reasoning precedes an unsafe final response. Principle-level analysis shows that risk concentrates in misinformation, legal compliance, discrimination, physical harm, and psychological harm. We further propose adaptive multi-principle steering, a white-box test-time mitigation that learns one unsafe-to-safe activation direction per safety principle and activates only directions whose current hidden state is closer to the unsafe than safe centroid. On three steerable open reasoning models, adaptive steering reduces unsafe counts in both reasoning traces and final answers on held-out and OOD benchmarks. DeepSeek-R1-Qwen-7B achieves a 40.8% average unsafe-count reduction while retaining 97.7% macro-averaged accuracy on BBH, GSM8K, and MMLU. These results suggest that LRM safety should be evaluated and mitigated over the full exposed reasoning-answer trajectory, not only at the final-answer stage.

AI.tldr: Large reasoning models expose chain-of-thought reasoning, creating a safety blind spot where harmful content appears in reasoning traces even when final answers look safe. Testing reveals reasoning traces consistently show additional safety risks beyond final answers, particularly in leak cases (unsafe reasoning → safe answer) and escape cases (safe reasoning → unsafe answer).
AI.motivation: Large reasoning models increasingly expose chain-of-thought-like reasoning for transparency, but this creates a safety blind spot where harmful or policy-violating content may appear in reasoning traces even when final answers appear safe. The paper tests whether final-answer safety is sufficient for evaluating the full reasoning-answer trajectory.
AI.method: Used a unified twenty-principle safety rubric to score both reasoning traces and final answers. Evaluated 15 open-weight and API-based LRMs across 41K prompts per model from seven public harmfulness/jailbreak sources plus four OOD sources. Proposed adaptive multi-principle steering - a white-box test-time mitigation that learns unsafe-to-safe activation directions per safety principle and activates only when hidden states are closer to unsafe than safe centroids.
AI.result: Reasoning traces consistently reveal additional safety risks beyond final answers, especially in high-severity stage-wise failures: leak cases (unsafe reasoning → safe answer) and escape cases (safe reasoning → unsafe answer). Risk concentrates in misinformation, legal compliance, discrimination, physical harm, and psychological harm. Adaptive steering reduces unsafe counts in both reasoning traces and final answers on held-out and OOD benchmarks. DeepSeek-R1-Qwen-7B achieves 40.8% average unsafe-count reduction while retaining 97.7% macro-averaged accuracy.
AI.conclusion: LRM safety should be evaluated and mitigated over the full exposed reasoning-answer trajectory, not only at the final-answer stage. The proposed adaptive multi-principle steering effectively reduces safety risks while maintaining model performance.

title: A Robust Foundation Model for Conservation Laws: Injecting Context into Flux Neural Operators via Recurrent Vision Transformers
summary: We propose an architecture that augments the Flux Neural Operator (Flux NO), which combines the classical finite volume method (FVM) with neural operators, with ViT-based context injection. Our model is formulated as a hypernetwork: it extracts solution dynamics over a finite temporal window, encodes them with a recurrent Vision Transformer, and generates the parameters of a context-conditioned neural operator. This enables the model to infer and solve conservation laws without explicit access to the governing equation or PDE coefficients. Experimentally, we show that the proposed method preserves the robustness, generalization ability, and long-time prediction advantages of Flux NO over standard neural operators, while delivering reliable numerical solutions across a broad range of conservative systems, including previously unseen fluxes. Our code is available at https://github.com/xx257xx/CONTEXT_FLUX_NO.

AI.tldr: A hypernetwork architecture that combines finite volume methods with neural operators and ViT-based context injection to solve conservation laws without explicit PDE knowledge.
AI.motivation: To create a neural operator model that can solve conservation laws without requiring explicit access to governing equations or PDE coefficients, while maintaining robustness and generalization capabilities.
AI.method: Augments Flux Neural Operator with ViT-based context injection. Uses hypernetwork formulation: extracts solution dynamics over temporal window, encodes with recurrent Vision Transformer, generates parameters for context-conditioned neural operator.
AI.result: Preserves Flux NO's robustness, generalization, and long-time prediction advantages while delivering reliable numerical solutions across broad range of conservative systems, including unseen fluxes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed architecture successfully enables inference and solution of conservation laws without explicit equation access, demonstrating strong performance across various conservative systems.

title: Attractor Geometry of Transformer Memory: From Conflict Arbitration to Confident Hallucination
summary: Language models draw on two knowledge sources: facts baked into weights (parametric memory, PM) and information in context (working memory, WM). We study two mechanistically distinct failure modes--conflict, when PM and WM disagree and interfere; and hallucination, when the queried fact was never learned. Both produce confident output regardless, making output-based monitoring blind by design. We show both failures share a unified geometric account. In the hidden-state space of autoregressive generation, learned facts form attractor basins. Conflict is basin competition: WM disrupts convergence to the correct basin without raising output entropy. Hallucination is basin absence: the hidden state drifts freely when no memorized basin exists. The frozen LM head, designed for next-token prediction, cannot distinguish these cases and fires confidently either way. We verify this account in a controlled synthetic task--entity identifiers mapped to unique codes with PM installed via LoRA adapters--where ground truth is exact and component roles can be causally isolated through targeted adapter placement. Geometric margin--the hidden state's distance to the nearest memorized basin--reads this geometry directly and separates correct recall from hallucination far more cleanly than output entropy, with zero false refusals where entropy-based detection cannot avoid rejecting the vast majority of correct outputs. The separation holds on natural-language factual queries from the pretrained model with no adaptation, confirming attractor geometry is structural rather than a fine-tuning artifact. The fraction of confident hallucinations follows a scaling law $C = \exp(-c/\barΔ)$, growing with scale even as overall error rates fall. Hidden states reliably encode epistemic state; the frozen output head systematically erases it--and this erasure worsens with scale.

AI.tldr: Language models fail in two ways: conflict (when internal knowledge conflicts with context) and hallucination (when knowledge was never learned), both produce confident outputs. Hidden state geometry reveals these failures through distance to memorized attractor basins, providing better detection than output entropy.
AI.motivation: Current language models fail in two mechanistically distinct ways but produce similarly confident outputs, making output-based monitoring ineffective. There's a need to understand the underlying geometric mechanisms behind these failures and develop better detection methods.
AI.method: The authors use a unified geometric framework where learned facts form attractor basins in hidden-state space. They study conflict as basin competition and hallucination as basin absence. They verify this using controlled synthetic tasks with entity identifiers mapped to unique codes, using LoRA adapters for precise control over parametric memory placement. They measure geometric margin (distance to nearest memorized basin) versus output entropy.
AI.result: Geometric margin separates correct recall from hallucination much more cleanly than output entropy, with zero false refusals where entropy-based detection rejects most correct outputs. Hidden states reliably encode epistemic state, but the frozen output head systematically erases this information. The fraction of confident hallucinations follows a scaling law C = exp(-c/Δ̄), increasing with model scale despite falling overall error rates.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a unified geometric account of LM failure modes through attractor basins in hidden-state space. Hidden state geometry offers superior failure detection compared to output-based methods, revealing that the frozen output head systematically hides epistemic uncertainty, and this problem worsens with increasing model scale.

title: MEMOA: Massive Mixtures of Online Agents via Mean-Field Decentralized Nash Equilibria
summary: In the modern age of large-scale AI, federated learning has become an increasingly important tool for training large populations of AI agents; however, its computational and communication costs can rapidly fail to scale with the number of agents. This is precisely where decentralized agentic strategies shine: each agent acts autonomously, using only its own state together with a minimal summary of the ensemble, namely the mean-field. We derive the unique optimal decentralized policy in closed form. Optimality is characterized through a worst-client/minimax criterion: minimizing the under-performer regret, namely the maximal online cost incurred by the weakest agent in the ensemble. We further prove that the resulting decentralized policy asymptotically converges, in the large-population limit, to the Nash-optimal centralized policy, whose direct computation is not scalable. We use an online weighting mechanism to optimize the server-computed mixture of client predictions, thereby improving the mean prediction in addition to the previously optimized weakest-client prediction. Numerical experiments verify our theoretical guarantees and demonstrate that our decentralized policy typically outperforms natural greedy decentralized baselines.

AI.tldr: Federated learning faces scalability issues with large AI populations, so the paper proposes a decentralized mean-field approach with optimal policy derived in closed form that minimizes worst-client regret and converges to Nash-optimal centralized policy.
AI.motivation: Federated learning has become important for training large populations of AI agents, but suffers from computational and communication costs that don't scale well with increasing numbers of agents. Decentralized strategies are needed where each agent acts autonomously using only local state and minimal ensemble summary (mean-field).
AI.method: Derives unique optimal decentralized policy in closed form using worst-client/minimax criterion to minimize under-performer regret (maximal online cost incurred by weakest agent). Uses online weighting mechanism to optimize server-computed mixture of client predictions, improving both mean prediction and weakest-client prediction.
AI.result: Proves decentralized policy asymptotically converges to Nash-optimal centralized policy in large-population limit (direct computation of which is not scalable). Numerical experiments verify theoretical guarantees and show decentralized policy outperforms natural greedy decentralized baselines.
AI.conclusion: Decentralized mean-field approach provides scalable solution for federated learning with large agent populations, achieving optimality through minimax criterion while maintaining computational efficiency and converging to centralized optimal in large-scale limit.

title: DataDignity: Training Data Attribution for Large Language Models
summary: Auditing language-model outputs often requires more than judging correctness: an auditor may need to identify which source document most likely supports the knowledge expressed in a response. We study this as pinpoint provenance: given a prompt, a target-model response, and a candidate corpus, rank the documents that best support the response. We introduce FakeWiki, a controlled benchmark of 3,537 fabricated Wikipedia-style articles designed to preserve ground-truth provenance while weakening lexical shortcuts. FakeWiki includes QA probes, source-preserving paraphrases, retro-generated variants, hard anti-documents that remain topically similar while removing answer-critical facts, and five query conditions: clean prompting plus four jailbreak-inspired transformations. We evaluate seven retrieval baselines, a training-free activation-steering retrieval-fusion method, SteerFuse, and a supervised contrastive provenance ranker, ScoringModel. ScoringModel maps response and document features into a shared space and is trained with InfoNCE using in-batch, retrieval-mined, and anti-document negatives. Across nine open-weight instruction-tuned LLMs and five query conditions, ScoringModel improves mean Recall@10 from 35.0 for the strongest retrieval baseline to 52.2, without inference-time fusion, and wins 41/45 model-by-condition cells. SteerFuse is usually second-best despite requiring no supervised training, showing that activation-space evidence can efficiently complement text retrieval. On jailbreak-inspired transformed queries, ScoringModel improves Recall@10 by 15.7 points on average over the best baseline. Overall, our work shows that robust training data attribution requires evaluation settings that separate true answer support from topical or lexical resemblance.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FakeWiki, a benchmark for evaluating pinpoint provenance (identifying which source documents support LLM responses), and proposes two methods: SteerFuse (activation-steering retrieval-fusion) and ScoringModel (supervised contrastive provenance ranker), which significantly outperform retrieval baselines.
AI.motivation: Current language model auditing requires identifying specific source documents that support generated responses, not just judging correctness. There's a need for robust evaluation that separates true answer support from topical or lexical resemblance.
AI.method: 1) Created FakeWiki benchmark with 3,537 fabricated Wikipedia-style articles with QA probes, paraphrases, variants, and hard anti-documents. 2) Developed SteerFuse (training-free activation-steering retrieval-fusion). 3) Built ScoringModel (supervised contrastive provenance ranker trained with InfoNCE using multiple negative types).
