AI.result: ReFlect achieves task success rates from 41% (gpt-4o-mini) to 56% (Claude Sonnet 4.5) across six models, with gains over Direct CoT ranging from +7pp to +29pp. It raises SWE-bench patch-structural quality from 0% to 82-87%. Harness gain is inversely proportional to baseline success rate (-1.69 slope, r=-0.76), meaning each pp lost in baseline is recovered by 1.69pp of harness gain.
AI.conclusion: ReFlect provides an effective solution for error detection and recovery in long-horizon reasoning tasks, showing that structured reasoning state approaches are limited at current model scales, while deterministic harness systems offer significant improvements across various LLMs.

title: When Can Voting Help, Hurt, or Change Course? Exact Structure of Binary Test-Time Aggregation
summary: Majority voting is one of the few black-box interventions that can improve a fixed stochastic predictor: repeated access can be cheaper than changing a high-capability model. Classical fixed-competence theory makes this intervention look monotone -- more votes help above the majority threshold and hurt below it. We show that this picture is fundamentally incomplete. Under the de Finetti representation for exchangeable repeated correctness, voting is governed by a latent distribution of per-example correctness probabilities. Even simple latent mixtures can generate sharply different voting curves, including nonmonotone behavior and, in an explicit construction, infinitely many trend changes. The full latent law determines the curve, but the curve does not determine the law. The exact object recovered by voting is a signed voting signature: at each binomial variance scale, it records excess latent mass above rather than below the majority threshold. Our main theorem proves that the complete odd-budget curve and this signature are equivalent: the curve increments are signed Hausdorff moments, and the full curve recovers the signature uniquely. This viewpoint explains shape phenomena, branch-symmetric nonidentifiability, realizability, variation, and endpoint rates. It also separates estimation regimes: direct per-example success-probability information targets the full signature, whereas fixed-depth grouped labels reveal only a finite prefix.

AI.tldr: Voting curves in ensemble predictors can have complex, non-monotone behavior due to latent per-example correctness distributions, with complete characterization through signed voting signatures and Hausdorff moments.
AI.motivation: Classical voting theory assumes monotone behavior where more votes always help above majority threshold and hurt below it, but this is incomplete. Understanding the true behavior of voting interventions on stochastic predictors requires examining the latent distributions governing per-example correctness.
AI.method: Using de Finetti representation for exchangeable repeated correctness, analyzing voting through latent distributions of per-example correctness probabilities. Characterizing voting curves via signed voting signatures and showing equivalence with odd-budget voting curves through signed Hausdorff moments.
AI.result: Voting curves can exhibit non-monotone behavior and infinitely many trend changes. The full voting curve uniquely determines a signed voting signature (excess latent mass above vs below majority threshold at each variance scale), and these are equivalent via Hausdorff moments. Reveals shape phenomena, nonidentifiability, realizability, and separates estimation regimes.
AI.conclusion: Voting behavior is more complex than classical theory suggests, governed by latent per-example correctness distributions. Complete characterization is possible through signed voting signatures and Hausdorff moments, explaining observed phenomena and clarifying different estimation approaches for ensemble predictors.

title: HyperLens: Quantifying Cognitive Effort in LLMs with Fine-grained Confidence Trajectory
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance across diverse tasks, their inference dynamics remain poorly understood because of the limited resolution of existing analysis tools. In this work, we identify an intrinsic magnification mechanism in transformer architectures: deeper layers inherently magnify the small changes of layer-wise confidence, providing a fine-grained confidence trajectory. Building on this insight, we introduce HyperLens, a high-resolution probe designed to trace confidence trajectories and quantify the cognitive effort during inference. Across LLMs and datasets, HyperLens reveals a consistent divergence in confidence trajectories that separates complex from simple tasks. We abstract this pattern into a quantitative cognitive effort metric. Our analysis reveals a fundamental principle: complex tasks consistently require higher cognitive effort. Finally, we provide a mechanistic diagnosis of a common side effect of standard Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT): it can reduce cognitive effort and consequently degrade performance on in-domain tasks.

AI.tldr: HyperLens is a high-resolution probe that traces confidence trajectories in LLMs, revealing that complex tasks require higher cognitive effort and identifying a side effect of SFT that reduces cognitive effort and degrades performance.
AI.motivation: LLM inference dynamics remain poorly understood due to limited resolution of existing analysis tools. There's a need for finer-grained analysis to understand how LLMs process different types of tasks and measure cognitive effort during inference.
AI.method: Identified intrinsic magnification mechanism in transformers where deeper layers amplify small confidence changes. Built HyperLens probe to trace confidence trajectories and quantify cognitive effort. Applied across various LLMs and datasets to analyze patterns.
AI.result: HyperLens reveals consistent divergence in confidence trajectories separating complex from simple tasks. This pattern is abstracted into a quantitative cognitive effort metric showing complex tasks consistently require higher cognitive effort. Also identified that standard SFT can reduce cognitive effort and degrade in-domain task performance.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a fundamental principle: complex tasks require higher cognitive effort, and introduces a diagnostic tool that reveals a problematic side effect of standard SFT - it reduces cognitive effort and harms performance. This offers new insights into LLM inference dynamics.

title: Optimal Contextual Pricing under Agnostic Non-Lipschitz Demand
summary: We study contextual dynamic pricing with linear valuations and bounded-support agnostic noise, whose induced demand curve may be non-Lipschitz with arbitrary jumps and atoms. Such discontinuities break the cross-context interpolation arguments used by smooth-demand pricing algorithms, while the best previous method achieved only $\tilde O(T^{3/4})$ regret. We propose Conservative-Markdown Redirect-UCB Pricing, a polynomial-time algorithm that combines randomized parameter estimation, conservative residual-grid probing, and confidence-based one-step redirection. Our algorithm achieves $\tilde O(T^{2/3})$ optimal regret, matching the known lower bounds of Kleinberg and Leighton (2003) up to logarithmic factors and improving over the previous upper bound of Xu and Wang (2022). Under stochastic well-conditioned contexts, this closes the long-existing open regret gap in linear-valuation contextual pricing under agnostic non-Lipschitz noise distribution.

AI.tldr: Conservative-Markdown Redirect-UCB Pricing algorithm achieves $\tilde O(T^{2/3})$ regret for contextual dynamic pricing with non-Lipschitz noise, matching known lower bounds and improving previous upper bounds.
AI.motivation: Addressing the challenge of contextual dynamic pricing with linear valuations and bounded-support agnostic noise where induced demand curves may be non-Lipschitz with arbitrary jumps and atoms. These discontinuities break interpolation arguments used by smooth-demand algorithms, creating a significant performance gap.
AI.method: Proposes Conservative-Markdown Redirect-UCB Pricing algorithm that combines randomized parameter estimation, conservative residual-grid probing, and confidence-based one-step redirection in polynomial time.
AI.result: Achieves $\tilde O(T^{2/3})$ optimal regret, matching the known lower bounds of Kleinberg and Leighton (2003) up to logarithmic factors, improving over the previous upper bound of $\tilde O(T^{3/4})$ by Xu and Wang (2022).
AI.conclusion: The algorithm closes the long-existing open regret gap in linear-valuation contextual pricing under agnostic non-Lipschitz noise distribution, achieving optimal performance under stochastic well-conditioned contexts.

title: Best Arm Identification in Generalized Linear Bandits via Hybrid Feedback
summary: We study fixed-confidence best arm identification in generalized linear bandits under a hybrid feedback model: at each round, the learner may query either (i) absolute reward feedback from a single arm or (ii) relative (dueling) feedback from an arm pair, both governed by generalized linear models. We introduce a likelihood-ratio--based confidence sequence that unifies heterogeneous generalized linear observations and yields an explicit ellipsoidal confidence set under a self-concordance assumption. Building on this confidence set, we propose a hybrid Track-and-Stop algorithm that adaptively allocates queries by tracking a minimax-optimal design over a joint action space of arms and pairs. We establish $δ$-correctness and provide high-probability upper bounds on the stopping time. We further extend the framework to a cost-aware setting that accounts for heterogeneous acquisition costs across feedback modalities. Empirical experiments demonstrate that the proposed algorithms significantly improve sample efficiency over baseline methods.

AI.tldr: A hybrid bandit algorithm combining absolute and relative feedback for generalized linear models with improved sample efficiency and cost-awareness.
AI.motivation: To address the practical scenario where learning agents can choose between different feedback modalities (absolute rewards from single arms or relative comparisons between arm pairs) in generalized linear bandits, which has not been well-studied in fixed-confidence best arm identification settings.
AI.method: Develop a unified likelihood-ratio-based confidence sequence for heterogeneous generalized linear observations, create an explicit ellipsoidal confidence set using self-concordance assumptions, and design a hybrid Track-and-Stop algorithm that adaptively allocates queries by tracking a minimax-optimal design over the joint action space.
AI.result: Proved δ-correctness of the algorithm and provided high-probability upper bounds on the stopping time. Extended the framework to handle heterogeneous acquisition costs across feedback modalities, and demonstrated through empirical experiments that the proposed algorithms significantly improve sample efficiency over baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hybrid feedback framework for generalized linear bandits effectively combines different feedback modalities with theoretical guarantees and practical performance improvements, offering a flexible approach that can adapt to different cost structures while maintaining statistical efficiency.

title: LLMSpace: Carbon Footprint Modeling for Large Language Model Inference on LEO Satellites
summary: Large language models (LLMs) impose rapidly growing energy demands, creating an emerging energy and carbon crisis driven by large-scale inference. Solar-powered, AI-enabled low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites have been proposed to mitigate terrestrial electricity consumption, but their lifecycle carbon footprint remains poorly understood due to launch emissions, satellite manufacturing, and radiation-hardened hardware requirements. This paper presents \textit{LLMSpace}, the first carbon modeling framework for LLM inference on AI-enabled LEO satellites. LLMSpace jointly models operational and embodied carbon, peripheral subsystems, radiation-hardened accelerators and memories, and LLM-specific workload characteristics such as prefill-decode behavior and token generation. Using realistic satellite and GPU configurations, LLMSpace reveals key trade-offs among carbon footprint, inference latency, hardware design, and operational lifetime for sustainable space-based LLM inference. Source code: https://github.com/UnchartedRLab/LLMSpace.

AI.tldr: LLMSpace is the first carbon modeling framework that evaluates the lifecycle carbon footprint of running large language models (LLMs) on solar-powered LEO satellites, considering both operational and embodied emissions from launch, hardware manufacturing, and radiation-hardened components.
AI.motivation: LLMs are creating an emerging energy and carbon crisis due to their growing energy demands for inference. While solar-powered LEO satellites have been proposed as a solution to reduce terrestrial electricity consumption, their complete lifecycle carbon footprint (including launch emissions, satellite manufacturing, and radiation-hardened hardware) remains poorly understood.
AI.method: The authors developed LLMSpace, a carbon modeling framework that jointly models operational and embodied carbon for LLM inference on AI-enabled LEO satellites. The framework accounts for peripheral subsystems, radiation-hardened accelerators and memories, and LLM-specific workload characteristics including prefill-decode behavior and token generation patterns.
AI.result: Using realistic satellite and GPU configurations, LLMSpace reveals key trade-offs among carbon footprint, inference latency, hardware design, and operational lifetime. The framework provides insights into sustainable space-based LLM inference and helps identify optimal configurations balancing environmental impact and performance.
AI.conclusion: LLMSpace represents the first comprehensive approach to understanding the full carbon implications of deploying LLMs on LEO satellites. The framework enables analysis of sustainability trade-offs and informs the design of more environmentally responsible space-based AI infrastructure, addressing both energy demands and lifecycle carbon emissions of large-scale LLM inference.

title: Evaluating Explainability in Safety-Critical ATR Systems: Limitations of Post-Hoc Methods and Paths Toward Robust XAI
summary: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) is increasingly rec ognized as essential for deploying machine learning systems in safety critical environments. In Automatic Target Recognition (ATR), where models operate on image, video, radar, and multisensor data, high pre dictive performance alone is insufficient. Model decisions must also be interpretable, reliable, and suitable for validation. This paper presents a structured evaluation of explainability methods in the context of safety-critical ATR systems: We identify major XAI paradigms, including saliency-based, attention-based, and surrogate ap proaches, as well as recent detection-aware extensions. Based on this, we formalize explainability as an assurance-oriented assessment problem, introduce a taxonomy, and assess these methods with respect to four key dimensions: interpretability, robustness, vulnerability to manipula tion, and suitability for validation and verification. The analysis identifies systematic limitations of current post-hoc explanation methods. In par ticular, we derive critical failure modes such as spurious explanations, instability under perturbations, and overtrust induced by visually con vincing outputs. These findings indicate that widely used XAI techniques may be insufficient for safety-critical deployment. Finally, we discuss implications for ATR systems and outline directions toward more robust, causally grounded, and physically informed explain ability methods. Our results emphasize the need to move beyond visually plausible explanations toward approaches that support reliable decision making and system-level assurance.

AI.tldr: Current XAI methods have critical limitations for safety-critical ATR systems, often providing visually convincing but unreliable explanations that can lead to overtrust and insufficient validation.
AI.motivation: XAI is essential for deploying machine learning in safety-critical environments like Automatic Target Recognition (ATR), where high predictive performance alone is insufficient. Models must be interpretable, reliable, and suitable for validation in these critical applications.
AI.method: The paper presents a structured evaluation of explainability methods for safety-critical ATR systems. It identifies major XAI paradigms (saliency-based, attention-based, surrogate approaches), formalizes explainability as an assurance-oriented assessment problem, introduces a taxonomy, and assesses methods across four key dimensions: interpretability, robustness, vulnerability to manipulation, and suitability for validation/verification.
AI.result: The analysis reveals systematic limitations of current post-hoc explanation methods, identifying critical failure modes including spurious explanations, instability under perturbations, and overtrust induced by visually convincing outputs. Widely used XAI techniques may be insufficient for safety-critical deployment.
AI.conclusion: There's a need to move beyond visually plausible explanations toward more robust, causally grounded, and physically informed explainability methods that support reliable decision making and system-level assurance in safety-critical ATR systems.

title: Region-adaptable retrieval of coastal biogeochemical parameters from near-surface hyperspectral remote sensing reflectance using physics-aware meta-learning
summary: Hyperspectral in situ sensing has shown promise in retrieving aquatic biogeochemical (BGC) parameters, such as total suspended solids, dissolved organic carbon, and total chlorophyll-a, for cost-effective monitoring of coastal water quality. However, generalising such retrieval algorithms across water bodies remains challenging, as the relationship between remote sensing reflectance (Rrs) and BGC parameters can vary considerably from one region to another due to regional distinctions in environmental conditions and biogeochemistry that lead to different BGC ranges and bio-optical properties. In this study, we propose a two-stage physics-aware meta-learning framework for retrieving coastal BGC parameters from near-surface Rrs observations. In the first stage, a bio-optical forward model is used to generate a large synthetic dataset based on an in situ bio-optical spectral library with broad representativeness of Australian coastal waters. This dataset is then used to pretrain a region-agnostic base model with meta-learning, allowing the model to learn fundamental physical relationships. In the second stage, the pretrained base model is fine-tuned for specific regions with local samples. We collected in situ hyperspectral Rrs and BGC measurements from five geographically distinct sites in Australian coastal waters. Our experimental results suggest: (1) the BGC parameters and their corresponding hyperspectral Rrs signatures exhibited clear regional distinctions among the experimental sites; (2) the synthetic dataset was physically plausible and closely aligned with real-world samples in both parameter distributions and inter-parameter correlations; (3) the proposed approach outperformed five benchmark models in BGC retrieval; and (4) time series of in situ measured and model-predicted BGC parameters showed good agreement in both magnitude and temporal dynamics.

AI.tldr: A physics-aware meta-learning framework for retrieving coastal biogeochemical parameters from hyperspectral in situ data, using synthetic training data from bio-optical models and region-specific fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Generalizing retrieval algorithms for aquatic biogeochemical parameters across different water bodies is challenging due to regional variations in environmental conditions, biogeochemistry, and bio-optical properties that affect relationships between remote sensing reflectance and BGC parameters.
AI.method: Two-stage physics-aware meta-learning approach: 1) Generate large synthetic dataset using bio-optical forward model based on spectral library representing Australian coastal waters, then pretrain region-agnostic base model with meta-learning; 2) Fine-tune pretrained model for specific regions with local in situ samples.
AI.result: Experimental results from five geographically distinct Australian coastal sites show: 1) Clear regional distinctions in BGC parameters and hyperspectral signatures; 2) Synthetic dataset aligned well with real-world samples in distributions and correlations; 3) Proposed approach outperformed five benchmark models; 4) Time series predictions showed good agreement with measured BGC parameters in magnitude and dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The physics-aware meta-learning framework effectively addresses regional generalization challenges in coastal BGC parameter retrieval by combining synthetic training for fundamental physical relationships with region-specific fine-tuning.

title: Confidence is the key: how conformal prediction enhances the generative design of permeable peptides
summary: Generative models coupled with reinforcement learning (RL), such as REINVENT and PepINVENT, have emerged as a powerful framework for de novo molecular design. During the ideation process these generative frameworks utilize various predictive models as part of the optimization objectives. However, the utility of the predictive models can be limited by their domain of applicability. When RL is used to explore the chemical space with predictive models, it can suggest molecules that lie outside the predictor's domain of applicability. As a result, the predictions may become less reliable, potentially steering designs into high reward but also high uncertainty chemical spaces. This is particularly pronounced for cyclic peptides which show therapeutic promise due to their modifiability and large interaction surfaces but are understudied compared to small molecules. While passive membrane permeation in cyclic peptides has attracted interest, identifying optimal permeable designs remains challenging yet crucial for targeting intracellular sites. We present an RL-guided generative framework that designs permeable cyclic peptides using an uncertainty-aware permeability predictor as the scoring component. To address predictive uncertainty, especially impacted by novel chemistry, we integrate conformal prediction (CP) as our uncertainty quantification method. CP assesses designs based on the calibrated model under a user-defined confidence level. We demonstrate that rewarding generated peptides with CP-informed predictions improves both reliability and efficiency of peptide optimization process. This also discourages exploration outside the predictor's applicability domain. This approach bridges the gap between predictive uncertainty and RL-guided exploration, showing how generative modelling and conformal prediction can be combined for the first time.

AI.tldr: RL-guided generative framework combines conformal prediction uncertainty quantification to design permeable cyclic peptides while avoiding unreliable predictions outside the model's domain.
AI.motivation: Generative models with RL can suggest molecules outside predictor's applicability domain, leading to unreliable predictions. This is particularly problematic for cyclic peptides which have therapeutic promise but are understudied compared to small molecules, making optimal permeable designs challenging to identify for intracellular targeting.
AI.method: RL-guided generative framework using uncertainty-aware permeability predictor with conformal prediction (CP) as uncertainty quantification method. CP assesses designs based on calibrated model under user-defined confidence level, allowing for CP-informed predictions to guide optimization.
AI.result: Rewarding generated peptides with CP-informed predictions improves both reliability and efficiency of peptide optimization process and discourages exploration outside predictor's applicability domain.
AI.conclusion: This approach successfully bridges the gap between predictive uncertainty and RL-guided exploration, demonstrating for the first time how generative modeling and conformal prediction can be effectively combined for more reliable molecular design.

title: Scaling Pretrained Representations Enables Label-Free Out-of-Distribution Detection Without Fine-Tuning
summary: Models trained with deep learning often fail to signal when inputs fall outside their training data manifold, leading to unreliable predictions under distribution shift. Prior work suggests that effective out-of-distribution (OOD) detection often requires class-conditional modeling or specialized models obtained through supervised fine-tuning. We revisit this assumption in modern pretrained models and show that their frozen representations already encode sufficient geometric structure for accurate label-free OOD detection. Across 59 backbone-task pairings spanning vision and language, we compare two complementary label-free detectors: a global Mahalanobis estimator fit on unlabeled latent representations, and ReSCOPED, a lightweight, diffusion-based typicality estimator operating on the same features at a local level. Despite their different detection mechanisms, representation scaling reveals a consistent regime-dependent pattern: both local and global detectors' absolute performance improves with better representation quality, and performance gaps between the two detectors disappear across both language and vision tasks as representations scale. These results suggest that label-free OOD detection depends strongly on the geometry exposed by frozen pretrained backbones, reducing the importance of detector choice as backbone scale increases and enabling efficient deployment directly on frozen models.

AI.tldr: Label-free OOD detection works well with frozen pretrained models: performance improves with better representations and detector choice matters less at scale, enabling efficient deployment.
AI.motivation: Deep learning models often fail to signal when inputs are outside their training distribution, leading to unreliable predictions. Prior work suggests effective OOD detection requires class-conditional modeling or supervised fine-tuning, but this may not be necessary with modern pretrained models.
AI.method: The authors examine frozen pretrained representations across 59 backbone-task pairings in vision and language. They compare two label-free detectors: a global Mahalanobis estimator on latent representations, and ReSCOPED, a lightweight diffusion-based typicality estimator operating at local level. They analyze how representation scaling affects detector performance.
AI.result: Both local and global detectors' performance improves with better representation quality. Performance gaps between detectors disappear as representations scale across both language and vision tasks. Frozen pretrained backbones encode sufficient geometric structure for accurate label-free OOD detection.
AI.conclusion: Label-free OOD detection depends strongly on the geometry exposed by frozen pretrained backbones. As backbone scale increases, detector choice becomes less important, enabling efficient deployment directly on frozen models without specialized training or fine-tuning.

title: CircuitFormer: A Circuit Language Model for Analog Topology Design from Natural Language Prompt
summary: Automating analog circuit design remains a longstanding challenge in Electronic Design Automation (EDA). While Transformer-based Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized software code generation, their application to analog hardware design is hindered by two critical limitations: (i) the scarcity of analog design datasets containing natural language description of a design and its corresponding netlist, and (ii) the inefficiency of general-purpose tokenizers (e.g., Byte Pair Encoding (BPE)) in capturing the inherent graph structure of circuits. To bridge this gap, first, we curate the largest annotated dataset of analog circuit netlists to date, comprising 31,341 netlist-natural language description pairs across all major circuit classes. Furthermore, we propose Circuit Tokenizer (CKT), a novel circuit graph tokenizer designed to encode netlist connectivity by explicitly mining frequent subcircuits. In terms of scalability, CKT overcomes the bottleneck of prior circuit graph serialization methods where vocabulary size scales linearly with maximum number of components in the dataset, n_max, (O(n_max)); instead, CKT decouples vocabulary growth from circuit complexity, achieving a constant O(1) complexity. Empirically, CKT outperforms standard BPE on circuit topology representation, reducing sequence length by 57% and achieving a 2.3x superior compression ratio using a compact, fixed vocabulary of size 512. Leveraging this optimized tokenization, we train a circuit-specific language model, CircuitFormer, a 511M parameter encoder-decoder transformer. Our model achieves 100% syntactic correctness and an 83% functional success rate across all major analog circuit categories, outperforming state-of-the-art open-source LLMs by 10% and 14%, respectively, while requiring 240x fewer parameters. The dataset is publicly available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/touhid314/cktformer-dataset.

AI.tldr: A novel circuit-specific language model called CircuitFormer is developed for analog circuit design automation, featuring a specialized Circuit Tokenizer (CKT) that encodes netlist connectivity by mining frequent subcircuits, achieving superior performance over general-purpose LLMs with far fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: Transformers and LLMs have revolutionized software code generation but face two critical limitations when applied to analog hardware design: (1) scarcity of analog design datasets with natural language descriptions and corresponding netlists, and (2) inefficiency of general-purpose tokenizers like BPE in capturing the graph structure of circuits.
AI.method: Two main contributions: First, creation of the largest annotated dataset of analog circuit netlists (31,341 netlist-description pairs). Second, development of Circuit Tokenizer (CKT) that mines frequent subcircuits to encode netlist connectivity with constant complexity (O(1)), unlike previous methods with linear complexity (O(n_max)). The tokenizer achieves 57% reduction in sequence length and 2.3x better compression ratio using a fixed vocabulary of 512 tokens.
AI.result: CircuitFormer, a 511M parameter transformer trained on this optimized tokenization, achieves 100% syntactic correctness and 83% functional success rate across all major analog circuit categories. This outperforms state-of-the-art open-source LLMs by 10% and 14% respectively, while requiring 240x fewer parameters.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Circuit Tokenizer and CircuitFormer model successfully bridge the gap between LLMs and analog circuit design by addressing dataset scarcity and tokenization inefficiency. The approach demonstrates that circuit-specific tokenization and language modeling can significantly outperform general-purpose LLMs for analog hardware design automation while being much more parameter-efficient.

title: Information-Preserving Domain Transfer with Unlabeled Data in Misspecified Simulation-Based Inference
summary: Simulation-based inference (SBI) provides amortized Bayesian parameter inference from simulator-generated data without requiring explicit likelihood evaluation. Its reliability can degrade under model misspecification, where real-world observations are not well represented by the simulator used for training. Existing methods using unlabeled real-world data often align simulated and real-world data distributions, but marginal alignment alone does not directly preserve parameter-relevant information needed for posterior inference. We propose SPIN, an SBI framework with parameter-relevant information-preserving domain transfer using unlabeled, unpaired real-world observations. During training, SPIN translates labeled simulator observations toward the real-world domain and back to the simulator domain, using the original simulator labels to encourage domain transfer that preserves parameter-relevant mutual information. At test time, the learned real-to-simulator transport maps real-world observations into the simulator domain for posterior inference, without requiring real-world parameter labels or paired real--simulator observations. Across controlled synthetic and physical real-world benchmarks, SPIN improves real-world posterior inference, with the improvement becoming clearer as misspecification increases.

AI.tldr: SPIN is a simulation-based inference framework that improves parameter inference under model misspecification by preserving parameter-relevant information during domain transfer between simulated and real-world data.
AI.motivation: Current SBI methods can degrade under model misspecification where real-world observations differ from simulator training data. Existing domain alignment methods don't preserve parameter-relevant information needed for accurate posterior inference.
AI.method: SPIN translates labeled simulator observations to real-world domain and back, using original simulator labels to encourage domain transfer that preserves mutual information about parameters. Uses learned transport maps to convert real-world observations to simulator domain for posterior inference.
AI.result: SPIN improves real-world posterior inference across synthetic and physical benchmarks, with greater improvements as model misspecification increases. Works without requiring real-world parameter labels or paired observations.
AI.conclusion: SPIN addresses a key limitation in SBI under model misspecification by preserving parameter-relevant information during domain transfer, enabling more reliable posterior inference using only unlabeled real-world data.

title: HEDP: A Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt-based Framework for Domain Incremental Learning
summary: Domain Incremental Learning is a critical scenario that requires models to continuously adapt to new data domains without retraining. However, domain shifts often cause severe performance degradation. To address this, we propose Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt, a domain-incremental framework inspired by Helmholtz free energy. HEDP introduces an energy regularization loss to enhance the separability of domain representations and a hybrid energy-distance weighted mechanism that fuses energy-based and distance-based cues to improve domain selection and generalization. Experiments on multiple benchmarks, including CORe50, show that HEDP achieves superior performance on unseen domains with a 2.57\% accuracy gain, effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting and enhancing open-world adaptability. Our code is \href{https://github.com/dannis97500/HEDP/}{available here}.

AI.tldr: HEDP is a domain-incremental learning framework that uses hybrid energy-distance prompts with Helmholtz free energy inspiration to improve domain adaptation and mitigate forgetting.
AI.motivation: Domain incremental learning faces severe performance degradation due to domain shifts, requiring models to adapt to new domains without retraining while maintaining performance on previous domains.
AI.method: Proposes Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt (HEDP) framework with energy regularization loss for better domain representation separability, and a hybrid energy-distance weighted mechanism that fuses energy-based and distance-based cues for improved domain selection and generalization.
AI.result: Achieves superior performance on multiple benchmarks including CORe50, with 2.57% accuracy gain on unseen domains, effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting and enhancing open-world adaptability.
AI.conclusion: HEDP provides an effective solution for domain incremental learning by combining energy-based regularization with distance-based mechanisms, demonstrating strong performance in mitigating forgetting and improving adaptability to new domains.

title: Structural Correspondence and Universal Approximation in Diagonal plus Low-Rank Neural Networks
summary: The massive computational costs of scaling modern deep learning architectures have driven the widespread use of parameter-efficient low-rank structures, such as LoRA and low-rank factorization. However, theoretical guarantees for their expressive power are less explored, often relying on restrictive priors like a pretrained base matrix, ReLU activations or non-verifiable singularity conditions. We first investigate the limits of neural networks constrained strictly to low-rank manifolds without pretrained dense priors. We demonstrate a theoretical paradox: while purely rank-1 layers can exactly interpolate arbitrary scalar datasets, they collapse for function approximations. To overcome this bottleneck without surrendering parameter efficiency, we introduce a unified \textit{Structural Correspondence} framework. We prove that augmenting low-rank layers with only a minimal sparse diagonal component, say a Diagonal plus Low-Rank (DLoR) structure, is sufficient to reach Universal Approximation. We show that any full-rank transformation can be exactly reconstructed using these DLoR components by trading off network width (additive decomposition) or depth (multiplicative decomposition). By tracking asymptotic Taylor remainders, we prove that DLoR neural networks fully restore the Universal Approximation Theorem for general activation functions. Finally, we establish that multiplicative depth provides superior parameter-to-expressivity scaling compared to additive width. Our results show that dense matrices and specific activation functions are not topological prerequisites for universal expressivity.

AI.tldr: Low-rank neural networks struggle with universal approximation without dense priors; adding minimal diagonal sparse components to low-rank structures restores full expressivity with better parameter efficiency through depth rather than width.
AI.motivation: While parameter-efficient low-rank structures (like LoRA) are widely used due to computational cost savings, their theoretical expressive power lacks guarantees without restrictive priors like pretrained base matrices or specific activation functions. The paper aims to investigate the fundamental limits of purely low-rank neural networks without such priors.
AI.method: Introduces a Structural Correspondence framework that augments low-rank layers with minimal sparse diagonal components (DLoR - Diagonal plus Low-Rank). Shows exact reconstruction of full-rank transformations using DLoR components through either additive decomposition (increasing width) or multiplicative decomposition (increasing depth). Uses asymptotic Taylor remainder analysis to prove universal approximation.
AI.result: Proves that purely rank-1 layers can exactly interpolate scalar data but fail at function approximation. Demonstrates that adding minimal diagonal components to low-rank structures (DLoR) restores Universal Approximation Theorem for general activation functions. Shows multiplicative depth decomposition provides superior parameter-to-expressivity scaling compared to additive width decomposition.
AI.conclusion: Dense matrices and specific activation functions are not topological prerequisites for universal expressivity. Parameter-efficient low-rank architectures can achieve full expressive power when minimally augmented with sparse diagonal components, with depth-based decomposition offering better parameter efficiency than width-based approaches.

title: Von Neumann Networks
summary: In the mid-twentieth century, mathematician and polymath John von Neumann created a computational system on an array of cells as a simple model of the human brain, where each cell had one of a finite set of roles or states that he predicted would be modelled by a diffusion process. In this work, we show that such a system, when developed in a modern deep learning setting, enables the construction of an artificial neuron having specialized roles that can be learnt. We refer to this neuron as the Von Neumann neuron, and the resulting neural network from such neurons result in a self-engineered design whose architecture is only dependent on the structure and locations of its inputs and outputs on this cellular array. The mathematical framework for these Von Neumann Networks (VNNs) is also constructed and shows that they are based on the extension of neural operators and the learning of Green's functions with convolutions on a cellular topology having a diffusion signature. We also prove that these VNNs are part of a more general computational system called Cellular Machines that are computationally universal. Initial experiments show that VNN based multi-layered perceptrons outperform their equivalent deep learning variant on basic tasks, while being more parameter efficient and are capable of learning new types of tasks. This includes the ability to solve for and construct an extension of the Von Neumann (hardware) architecture common to all modern computers to cells and suggests new opportunities that could be explored.

AI.tldr: A new neural network architecture called Von Neumann Networks (VNNs) based on John von Neumann's cellular computational model, featuring self-engineered architectures that outperform traditional deep learning models while being more parameter efficient.
AI.motivation: To develop a modern deep learning implementation of John von Neumann's computational cellular array concept, which was originally conceived as a simple model of the human brain based on diffusion processes, and explore its potential for creating self-engineered neural architectures.
AI.method: Created Von Neumann neurons with specialized learnable roles based on von Neumann's cellular array system, developed Von Neumann Networks (VNNs) as neural operators that learn Green's functions with convolutions on cellular topology having diffusion signature, and proved VNNs are part of computationally universal Cellular Machines.
AI.result: VNN-based multi-layered perceptrons outperform equivalent deep learning variants on basic tasks, demonstrate greater parameter efficiency, can learn new types of tasks including solving for and constructing extensions of Von Neumann hardware architecture to cells.
AI.conclusion: Von Neumann Networks represent a promising new direction in neural network design that combines von Neumann's computational cellular concepts with modern deep learning, offering self-engineered architectures with better performance and efficiency while opening new research opportunities.

title: Distributionally Robust Multi-Objective Optimization
summary: Multi-objective optimization (MOO) has received growing attention in applications that require learning under multiple criteria. However, the existing MOO formulations do not explicitly account for distributional shifts in the data. We introduce distributionally robust multi-objective optimization (DR-MOO), which minimizes multiple objectives under their respective worst-case distributions. We propose Pareto-type solution concepts for DR-MOO and develop multi-gradient descent algorithms (MGDA) with provable guarantees. Leveraging a Lagrangian dual reformulation, we first design a double-loop MGDA that uses an inner loop to estimate dual variables and achieves a total sample complexity $\mathcal{O}(ε^{-12})$ for reaching an $ε$-Pareto-stationary point. To further improve efficiency, we incorporate gradient clipping to handle generalized-smooth and biased gradient estimates, removing the need for double sampling. This yields a single-loop double-clip MGDA with substantially improved sample complexity $\mathcal{O}(ε^{-4})$. Our theory applies to the nonconvex setting and does not require bounded objectives or gradients. Experiments demonstrate that our methods are competitive with state-of-the-art MGDA baselines.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Distributionally Robust Multi-Objective Optimization (DR-MOO), which minimizes multiple objectives under worst-case distributions, and develops efficient multi-gradient descent algorithms with improved sample complexity guarantees.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-objective optimization formulations do not account for distributional shifts in data, limiting their robustness in real-world applications where data distributions can change.
AI.method: The authors propose DR-MOO with Pareto-type solution concepts and develop multi-gradient descent algorithms: 1) a double-loop MGDA using Lagrangian dual reformulation with inner loop for dual variable estimation, and 2) a more efficient single-loop double-clip MGDA that incorporates gradient clipping to handle generalized-smooth and biased gradient estimates.
AI.result: The double-loop MGDA achieves sample complexity O(ε^{-12}) for reaching ε-Pareto-stationary points, while the single-loop double-clip MGDA substantially improves this to O(ε^{-4}). The theory applies to nonconvex settings without requiring bounded objectives or gradients, and experiments show competitiveness with state-of-the-art MGDA baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DR-MOO framework and algorithms effectively address distributional robustness in multi-objective optimization, with significant improvements in sample efficiency and theoretical guarantees that maintain practical competitiveness with existing methods.

title: Sheet as Token: A Graph-Enhanced Representation for Multi-Sheet Spreadsheet Understanding
summary: Workbook-scale spreadsheet understanding is increasingly important for language-model-based data analysis agents, but remains challenging because relevant information is often distributed across multiple sheets with heterogeneous schemas, layouts, and implicit relationships. Existing retrieval-augmented approaches typically decompose spreadsheets into rows, columns, or blocks to improve scalability; however, such chunk-centric representations can fragment worksheets into isolated text spans and weaken global sheet-level semantics. We propose Sheet as Token, a graph-enhanced framework that treats each worksheet as a unified semantic unit for multi-sheet spreadsheet retrieval. Our method extracts schema-aware records from sheet names, column headers, representative values, and layout features, and encodes each worksheet into a compact dense token. Given a natural-language query, a Graph Retriever constructs a query-specific candidate graph over sheet tokens using semantic, query-conditioned, schema-consistency, and shape-compatibility relations, and composes these channels through a multi-stage graph transformer to retrieve supporting sheet sets. Experiments on a constructed multi-sheet spreadsheet corpus show that sheet-level tokenization learns stable representations, and that graph-enhanced cross-sheet reasoning improves listwise retrieval over a shallow graph baseline with limited additional graph-side computation. These results suggest that sheet-level tokenization is a promising abstraction for scalable multi-sheet spreadsheet understanding.

AI.tldr: Sheet as Token treats each worksheet as a unified semantic unit for multi-sheet spreadsheet retrieval, using graph-enhanced cross-sheet reasoning to improve retrieval performance.
AI.motivation: Workbook-scale spreadsheet understanding is challenging due to distributed information across multiple sheets with heterogeneous schemas, layouts, and implicit relationships. Existing chunk-centric approaches fragment worksheets and weaken global sheet-level semantics.
AI.method: Sheet as Token extracts schema-aware records from sheet names, column headers, representative values, and layout features, encoding each worksheet into a compact dense token. A Graph Retriever constructs query-specific candidate graphs over sheet tokens using multiple relation types (semantic, query-conditioned, schema-consistency, shape-compatibility) and composes them through a multi-stage graph transformer to retrieve supporting sheet sets.
AI.result: Experiments on a constructed multi-sheet spreadsheet corpus show that sheet-level tokenization learns stable representations, and graph-enhanced cross-sheet reasoning improves listwise retrieval over shallow graph baselines with limited additional computation.
AI.conclusion: Sheet-level tokenization is a promising abstraction for scalable multi-sheet spreadsheet understanding, providing a unified semantic representation that preserves worksheet-level semantics while enabling effective cross-sheet reasoning.

title: Temporal Functional Circuits: From Spline Plots to Faithful Explanations in KAN Forecasting
comment: 9 pages, 4 figures, 6 tables, plus appendix. Under review at NeurIPS 2026
summary: Unlike MLPs, Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) expose explicit learnable edge functions on every connection, enabling mechanistic explanation in time-series forecasting. This paper introduces Temporal Functional Circuits, a framework that transforms KAN edge functions from latent visualizations into faithful, temporally grounded explanations. Built on a gated residual KAN that decomposes forecasts into a linear base and a sparsely activated KAN correction, the framework (i) maps each edge to input lags via output-aware attribution, (ii) ranks edges by learned activation range, and (iii) validates faithfulness through edge-level interventions including zeroing and spline removal. Removing the learned B-spline component while retaining the base SiLU term degrades forecasts, providing evidence that the spline shape itself carries predictive value beyond the base activation. On four synthetic regimes of increasing complexity, the learned gate opens progressively wider as signal complexity grows. On regime-switching signals, gated KAN achieves 59% lower MSE than linear-only models. Across eight benchmarks, the gated architecture is competitive with linear, attention, and MLP alternatives, while providing interpretable edge functions that MLP-based corrections cannot offer.

AI.tldr: KANs enable temporal explanations via Temporal Functional Circuits framework that transforms edge functions into interpretable, temporally grounded explanations through attribution mapping, activation ranking, and faithfulness validation.
AI.motivation: Unlike MLPs, KANs have explicit learnable edge functions but lack temporal grounding. The paper aims to transform KAN edge functions from latent visualizations into faithful, temporally grounded explanations for time-series forecasting.
AI.method: Uses gated residual KAN that decomposes forecasts into linear base and sparsely activated KAN correction. Framework maps edges to input lags via output-aware attribution, ranks edges by learned activation range, and validates faithfulness through edge-level interventions (zeroing and spline removal).
AI.result: Removing spline component degrades forecasts, showing spline shape has predictive value beyond base activation. On synthetic regimes, learned gate opens wider as complexity grows. Gated KAN achieves 59% lower MSE than linear models on regime-switching signals. Competitive with linear, attention, and MLP alternatives across eight benchmarks while offering interpretable edge functions.
AI.conclusion: Temporal Functional Circuits framework successfully transforms KANs into interpretable models for time-series forecasting, providing faithful temporal explanations through edge function analysis while maintaining competitive performance with traditional architectures.

title: Long-Horizon Q-Learning: Accurate Value Learning via n-Step Inequalities
summary: Off-policy, value-based reinforcement learning methods such as Q-learning are appealing because they can learn from arbitrary experience, including data collected by older policies or other agents. In practice, however, bootstrapping makes long-horizon learning brittle: estimation errors at later states propagate backward through temporal-difference (TD) updates and can compound over time. We propose long-horizon Q-learning (LQL), which introduces a principled backstop against compounding error when learning the optimal action-value function. LQL builds on a prior optimality tightening observation: any realized action sequence lower-bounds what the optimal policy can achieve in expectation, so acting optimally earlier should not be worse than following the observed actions for several steps before switching to optimal behavior. Our contribution is to turn this inequality into a practical stabilization mechanism for Q-learning by using a hinge loss to penalize violations of these bounds. Importantly, LQL computes these penalties using network outputs already produced for the TD error, requiring no auxiliary networks and no additional forward passes relative to Q-learning. When combined with multiple state-of-the-art methods on a range of online and offline-to-online benchmarks, LQL consistently outperforms both 1-step TD and n-step TD learning at similar runtime.

AI.tldr: Long-horizon Q-learning (LQL) stabilizes Q-learning against compounding error by using optimality tightening bounds with hinge loss penalties, improving performance over 1-step and n-step TD learning without additional computational cost.
AI.motivation: Off-policy, value-based RL methods like Q-learning are attractive for learning from arbitrary experience, but they suffer from brittleness in long-horizon learning due to compounding estimation errors that propagate backward through TD updates.
AI.method: LQL introduces optimality tightening bounds: any realized action sequence lower-bounds what the optimal policy can achieve in expectation. The method turns this inequality into a practical stabilization mechanism using a hinge loss to penalize violations of these bounds, requiring no auxiliary networks or additional forward passes beyond standard Q-learning.
AI.result: When combined with state-of-the-art methods on online and offline-to-online benchmarks, LQL consistently outperforms both 1-step TD and n-step TD learning at similar runtime.
AI.conclusion: LQL provides a principled, computationally efficient approach to stabilize Q-learning against compounding error in long-horizon problems, making value-based reinforcement learning more robust while maintaining the benefits of off-policy learning.

title: Budgeted Attention Allocation: Cost-Conditioned Compute Control for Efficient Transformers
summary: Transformers usually expose one inference cost per trained model, while deployed systems often need multiple cost-quality operating points. We study Budgeted Attention Allocation, a monotone head-gating mechanism conditioned on a requested attention budget. Dense warm-starting is important for stability: on a robust synthetic sequence task, one budgeted model reaches 99.7% accuracy at 0.303 estimated attention cost and 100.0% accuracy at 0.504 cost. On held-out AG News with a custom word-level transformer, hard-gate adaptation turns soft cost control into measured single-thread CPU speed, reaching 82.1% accuracy with 1.28x speedup at budget 0.50. In pretrained BERT-Mini AG News, budgeted structural pruning reaches 87.6% accuracy with 1.20x speedup at budget 0.50; a validation-ranked zero-shot dense post-hoc structural baseline reaches 86.1%, and one recovery epoch raises that per-budget specialist to 87.9%. On DBpedia14, BERT-Mini budgeted gates reach 97.4% at exact budget 0.50 versus 96.6% for dense full attention. Static fixed-budget gates and recovered dense specialists remain strong. The contribution is therefore not universal dominance, but a reproducible feasibility study of one controllable checkpoint across budgets that can trade attention cost for accuracy and be converted into measured structural speedups on small CPU benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Budgeted Attention Allocation enables controllable attention cost-quality tradeoffs in transformers via monotone head-gating conditioned on attention budgets, achieving measured CPU speedups with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Transformers typically offer only one inference cost per trained model, while real-world deployment needs multiple cost-quality operating points for different resource constraints.
AI.method: Proposes Budgeted Attention Allocation with monotone head-gating mechanism conditioned on requested attention budget. Uses dense warm-starting for stability, hard-gate adaptation to convert soft cost control into measured CPU speed, and budgeted structural pruning in pretrained models.
AI.result: On synthetic task: 99.7% accuracy at 0.303 attention cost, 100% at 0.504 cost. On AG News with custom transformer: 82.1% accuracy with 1.28x CPU speedup at budget 0.50. BERT-Mini AG News: 87.6% accuracy with 1.20x speedup at budget 0.50. DBpedia14: 97.4% accuracy at exact budget 0.50 vs 96.6% for full attention.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates reproducible feasibility of controllable checkpoints across budgets that trade attention cost for accuracy and convert to structural speedups on CPU benchmarks, though not claiming universal dominance over specialized approaches.

title: AGPO: Asymmetric Group Policy Optimization for Verifiable Reasoning and Search Ads Relevance at JD
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has demonstrated notable success in enhancing the reasoning performance of large language models (LLMs). However, recent studies reveal that while current RLVR methods improve sampling efficiency towards correct paths, they do not elicit fundamentally new reasoning patterns. Instead, the reasoning capability boundary of trained models often narrows compared to their base models, with base models achieving higher coverage at large sample sizes. In this work, we propose Asymmetric Group Policy Optimization (AGPO) to counteract this boundary shrinkage. AGPO adopts a negative-dominant reinforcement strategy to suppress incorrect reasoning paths, maintaining the base model's exploration capacity. For positive reinforcement, AGPO adopts a group advantage mechanism, which scales positive updates based on intra-group variance, allowing the model to focus on rare correct paths while suppressing updates from trivial paths. Our experiments on five mathematical benchmarks demonstrate that AGPO achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while consistently improving pass@$k$ performance at scale. In a large-scale industrial application for search ads relevance optimization, AGPO effectively enhances the quality of the data annotation, leading to substantial performance gains in downstream student models.

AI.tldr: AGPO is a new RLVR method that uses asymmetric negative-dominant reinforcement and group advantage scaling to prevent reasoning capability shrinkage while improving performance at scale.
AI.motivation: Current RLVR methods for LLMs improve sampling efficiency but narrow reasoning capability boundaries compared to base models, which achieve better coverage at large sample sizes. There's a need to counteract this boundary shrinkage while maintaining exploration capacity.
AI.method: AGPO uses asymmetric reinforcement: negative-dominant strategy to suppress incorrect reasoning paths while preserving base model exploration, and positive reinforcement with group advantage mechanism that scales updates based on intra-group variance to focus on rare correct paths while suppressing trivial ones.
AI.result: Experiments on five mathematical benchmarks show AGPO achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and consistently improves pass@k performance at scale. In industrial search ads relevance optimization, AGPO enhances data annotation quality, leading to substantial performance gains in downstream student models.
AI.conclusion: AGPO effectively addresses the boundary shrinkage problem in RLVR by maintaining exploration capacity while focusing reinforcement on meaningful reasoning patterns, demonstrating practical benefits in both academic benchmarks and real-world industrial applications.

title: On the Blessing of Pre-training in Weak-to-Strong Generalization
summary: The paradigm of Weak-to-Strong Generalization (W2SG) suggests that a pre-trained strong model can surpass its weak supervisor, yet the decisive role of pre-training remains theoretically and empirically under-explored. In this work, we identify pre-training as the essential prerequisite for the emergence of W2SG. Theoretically, we formalize the W2SG problem within a high-dimensional single-index model framework using spiked Gaussian data, modeling pre-training as a spectral initialization step. Building upon prior impossibility results regarding the failure of learning under random initialization, we prove that W2SG is achievable when pre-training provides a geometric warm start that places the model within an "effective region" characterized by a perturbed strong-convexity geometry. Within this region, we derive a rigorous generalization bound that naturally captures the optimization dynamics: an initial performance improvement followed by a saturation bottleneck dictated by the weak supervisor's bias. Empirically, we first validate all our assumptions and theoretical insights through controlled synthetic simulations. Finally, through a massive-scale evaluation of hundreds of intermediate pre-training checkpoints from large language models, we demonstrate that W2SG is not an innate capability but emerges via a phase transition tightly coupled with the progression of pre-training.

AI.tldr: Pre-training is identified as the essential prerequisite for Weak-to-Strong Generalization (W2SG), which emerges via a phase transition during pre-training rather than being an innate capability.
AI.motivation: The decisive role of pre-training in Weak-to-Strong Generalization (W2SG) remains theoretically and empirically under-explored, despite the paradigm suggesting that strong models can surpass weak supervisors.
AI.method: Theoretical framework formalizes W2SG within a high-dimensional single-index model using spiked Gaussian data, modeling pre-training as spectral initialization. Empirical validation through controlled synthetic simulations and massive-scale evaluation of hundreds of intermediate pre-training checkpoints from large language models.
AI.result: W2SG is achievable when pre-training provides a geometric warm start placing the model within an "effective region" with perturbed strong-convexity geometry. Performance shows initial improvement then saturation bottleneck dictated by weak supervisor's bias. W2SG emerges via phase transition tightly coupled with pre-training progression.
AI.conclusion: Pre-training is the essential prerequisite for W2SG emergence, which is not an innate capability but arises through a phase transition during pre-training progression, with theoretical conditions requiring geometric warm starts within specific effective regions.

title: MolRecBench-Wild: A Real-World Benchmark for Optical Chemical Structure Recognition
summary: Optical Chemical Structure Recognition (OCSR) aims to translate molecular diagrams in scientific literature into machine-readable formats, but current systems remain unreliable on real-world images due to substantial visual and chemical complexity. We introduce MOSAIC, a dual-dimensional difficulty framework with 37 fine-grained labels that jointly characterize visual interference and chemical semantic challenges in molecular diagrams. Based on this framework, we construct MolRecBench-Wild, a benchmark of 5,029 structures from 820 recent chemistry papers, covering the full difficulty spectrum observed in real publications. To enable faithful semantic evaluation beyond SMILES and MolFile, we propose CARBON, a representation language capable of expressing valence variations, icon-based groups, and other non-standard chemical semantics. We further adopt a dual-track evaluation protocol supporting both CARBON and SMILES outputs for broad model compatibility. Comprehensive experiments over 18 OCSR-capable models reveal severe performance degradation on MolRecBench-Wild, exposing a large gap between previous patent benchmarks and real-world academic scenarios.

AI.tldr: MOSAIC introduces a dual-dimensional difficulty framework with 37 labels for molecular diagram complexity, creates MolRecBench-Wild benchmark with 5,029 structures, proposes CARBON representation language for semantic evaluation, and shows current OCSR models perform poorly on real-world academic images.
AI.motivation: Current Optical Chemical Structure Recognition (OCSR) systems are unreliable on real-world molecular diagrams due to visual and chemical complexity. There's a gap between existing benchmarks (often based on patents) and actual academic literature with complex structures, valence variations, and non-standard semantics.
AI.method: 1) Introduced MOSAIC framework with 37 fine-grained labels for visual interference and chemical semantic challenges. 2) Built MolRecBench-Wild benchmark with 5,029 structures from 820 recent chemistry papers covering full difficulty spectrum. 3) Proposed CARBON representation language for expressing valence variations, icon-based groups, and non-standard semantics. 4) Established dual-track evaluation protocol supporting both CARBON and SMILES outputs. 5) Evaluated 18 OCSR-capable models on the new benchmark.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments reveal severe performance degradation of OCSR models on MolRecBench-Wild, exposing a large gap between previous patent benchmarks and real-world academic scenarios. Current systems struggle with the visual and chemical complexity found in actual chemistry publications.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that existing OCSR benchmarks don't reflect real-world academic complexity, and proposes a more comprehensive evaluation framework (MOSAIC difficulty labels, MolRecBench-Wild benchmark, CARBON representation) to better assess and improve OCSR systems for practical use in chemistry research.

title: Enabling Federated Inference via Unsupervised Consensus Embedding
comment: 18 pages, 15 figures, submitted to IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing (TMC) (under review)
summary: Cooperative inference across independently deployed machine learning models is increasingly desirable in distributed environments, as there is a growing need to leverage multiple models while keeping their data and model parameters private. However, existing cooperative frameworks typically rely on sharing input data, model parameters, or a common encoder, which limits their applicability in privacy-sensitive or cross-organizational settings. To address this challenge, we propose Consensus Embedding-based Federated Inference (CE-FI), a framework that enables pretrained models to cooperate at inference time without sharing model parameters or raw inputs and without assuming a common encoder. CE-FI introduces two components: a Consensus Embedding (CE) layer that maps heterogeneous intermediate representations into a common embedding space, and a Cooperative Output (CO) layer that produces predictions from these embeddings. Both layers are trained using shared unlabeled data only, so the cooperative stage does not require additional labeled data. Experiments on image classification benchmarks -- CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 -- under diverse non-IID conditions show that CE-FI consistently outperforms solo inference and performs comparably to conventional methods that require stronger sharing assumptions. Additional evaluations on text and time-series tasks indicate applicability beyond image classification, although performance depends on the ensemble strategy. Further analysis identifies representation alignment as the primary bottleneck.

AI.tldr: CE-FI enables pretrained models to cooperate during inference without sharing parameters, data, or requiring a common encoder, using consensus embeddings and cooperative output layers trained on shared unlabeled data.
AI.motivation: There's a growing need for cooperative inference across distributed ML models while maintaining privacy. Existing methods require sharing data, parameters, or a common encoder, limiting their use in privacy-sensitive or cross-organizational settings.
AI.method: CE-FI introduces two components: 1) Consensus Embedding (CE) layer that maps heterogeneous intermediate representations into a common embedding space, and 2) Cooperative Output (CO) layer that produces predictions from these embeddings. Both are trained using shared unlabeled data only.
AI.result: Experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 under diverse non-IID conditions show CE-FI consistently outperforms solo inference and performs comparably to conventional methods requiring stronger sharing. Additional evaluations on text and time-series tasks show broader applicability.
AI.conclusion: CE-FI provides a practical framework for cooperative inference without privacy compromises, though representation alignment remains a bottleneck and performance depends on ensemble strategy selection.

title: On the Role of Language Representations in Auto-Bidding: Findings and Implications
summary: Auto-bidding is a crucial task in real-time advertising markets, where policies must optimize long-horizon value under delivery constraints (e.g., budget and CPA). Existing methods for auto-bidding rely on compact numerical state representations: while they can implicitly capture delivery dynamics, they offer limited support for explicitly representing and controlling high-level intent, evolving feedback, and operator-style strategic guidance in real campaigns. Meanwhile, Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a powerful method for encoding semantic information, it remains unclear when LLMs help and how to integrate them without sacrificing numerical precision. Through systematic preliminary studies, we find that (1) LLM embeddings contain bidding-relevant cues yet cannot replace numerical features, and (2) gains emerge only with careful semantic--numeric integration rather than naive concatenation. Motivated by these findings, we propose \textit{SemBid}, a novel auto-bidding framework that injects LLM-encoded semantics into offline bidding trajectories at the token level. SemBid introduces three semantic inputs: \textit{Task}, \textit{History}, and \textit{Strategy}. It injects these semantics as tokens alongside numerical trajectory tokens and uses self-attention to integrate them, improving controllability and generalization across objectives. Across diverse scenarios and budget regimes, SemBid outperforms competitive baselines from offline RL and generative sequence modeling, with more consistent gains in overall performance, constraint satisfaction, and robustness. Our code is available at: \href{https://github.com/AlanYu04/SemBid-KDD2026}{\textcolor{blue}{here}}.

AI.tldr: SemBid is an auto-bidding framework that integrates LLM-encoded semantics (Task, History, Strategy) with numerical trajectory tokens using self-attention, improving controllability and generalization across diverse advertising scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing auto-bidding methods use compact numerical state representations that implicitly capture delivery dynamics but offer limited support for explicitly representing and controlling high-level intent, evolving feedback, and operator-style strategic guidance in real campaigns. While LLMs can encode semantic information, it's unclear when they help and how to integrate them without sacrificing numerical precision.
AI.method: Proposes SemBid framework that injects LLM-encoded semantics into offline bidding trajectories at token level. Introduces three semantic inputs: Task (campaign objective), History (past performance patterns), and Strategy (operator guidance). Injects these semantics as tokens alongside numerical trajectory tokens and uses self-attention to integrate them, allowing bidirectional interaction between semantic and numerical information.
AI.result: Across diverse scenarios and budget regimes, SemBid outperforms competitive baselines from offline RL and generative sequence modeling, showing more consistent gains in overall performance, constraint satisfaction, and robustness. Systematic studies show LLM embeddings contain bidding-relevant cues but cannot replace numerical features, and gains emerge only with careful semantic-numeric integration.
AI.conclusion: SemBid successfully integrates LLM-encoded semantics with numerical bidding trajectories, improving auto-bidding performance through better controllability and generalization. The framework demonstrates that careful integration of semantic and numeric information, rather than naive concatenation, is key to leveraging LLMs' strengths while maintaining numerical precision in advertising auto-bidding systems.

title: WARP: A Benchmark for Primal-Dual Warm-Starting of Interior-Point Solvers
summary: Solving AC Optimal Power Flow (AC-OPF) is of central importance in electricity market operations, where interior-point methods (IPMs) such as IPOPT are the standard solvers. A growing body of work uses machine learning to predict primal warm-start iterates, reporting iteration reductions of 30-46\%. We show that these reported gains rest on an inappropriate evaluation baseline: prior methods benchmark against the flat start $V_m = 1, V_a = 0$, whereas the solver's actual default - the variable-bound midpoint $(l+u)/2$ - is near-optimal for log-barrier centrality. Against this corrected baseline, no primal-only warm-start method reduces solver iterations. We trace the failure to a geometric property of interior-point methods: primal prediction accuracy is anticorrelated with convergence speed, and providing the ground-truth optimal solution $x^*$ without dual variables causes the solver to diverge. Oracle experiments establish that the complete primal-dual-barrier state $(x^*, λ^*, z^*, μ^*)$ reduces IPOPT iterations from 23 to 3 - an 85\% reduction that is structurally inaccessible to primal-only methods. To enable rigorous evaluation of warm-start methods on this task, we release a benchmark suite comprising dual-labeled AC-OPF datasets with IPOPT-extracted solutions, a corrected evaluation protocol, and WARP - a topology-conditioned encode-process-decode interaction network that predicts the full interior-point state $(\hat{x}, \hatλ, \hat{z}, \hatμ)$ on the heterogeneous constraint graph. WARP achieves a 76\% reduction in IPOPT iterations while natively accommodating N-1 contingency topology variations without retraining.

AI.tldr: Previous claims of ML reducing AC-OPF solver iterations by 30-46% were based on flawed benchmarks against flat starts; against the solver's actual default (variable-bound midpoint), primal-only warm-starts show no improvement. The paper reveals primal-dual-barrier state prediction is essential for real speedup (85% reduction), and introduces WARP to achieve this.
AI.motivation: There's growing interest in using machine learning to predict warm-start iterates for AC Optimal Power Flow (AC-OPF) solvers to reduce computational time. However, prior research uses inappropriate evaluation baselines (flat start vs. solver's actual default), inflating reported improvements. The authors aim to establish proper evaluation methods and demonstrate that primal-only approaches fundamentally cannot achieve real speedups due to geometric properties of interior-point methods.
AI.method: The paper analyzes why primal-only warm-start methods fail, showing primal accuracy is anticorrelated with convergence speed. They conduct oracle experiments to establish the potential of full primal-dual-barrier state prediction. They then develop WARP - a topology-conditioned encode-process-decode interaction network that predicts the complete interior-point state (x, λ, z, μ) on the heterogeneous constraint graph, handling N-1 contingency topology variations without retraining.
AI.result: When evaluated against the corrected baseline (solver's variable-bound midpoint default), no primal-only warm-start method reduces solver iterations. Oracle experiments show the complete primal-dual-barrier state can reduce IPOPT iterations from 23 to 3 (85% reduction). WARP achieves a 76% reduction in IPOPT iterations while handling topology variations without retraining. The authors release a benchmark suite with dual-labeled AC-OPF datasets and corrected evaluation protocol.
AI.conclusion: Primal-only warm-start methods for AC-OPF are fundamentally limited and previous reported gains were artifacts of improper benchmarking. True acceleration requires predicting the full interior-point state including dual and barrier variables. The proposed WARP model demonstrates practical effectiveness with significant iteration reductions while handling topology variations, providing a foundation for rigorous evaluation of future warm-start methods.

title: Taklif.AI: LLM-Powered Platform for Interest-Based Personalized College Assignments
summary: Educators face significant challenges in creating engaging, personalized assignments that accommodate students' diverse interests and cognitive abilities. Traditional one-size-fits-all assignments frequently lead to decreased student engagement and increased reliance on unethical practices such as plagiarism. To address these challenges, we present Taklif.AI, a platform that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to automatically generate personalized assignments tailored to individual student interests. Unlike existing AI-powered educational platforms that personalize based on academic performance metrics alone, Taklif.AI incorporates students' extracurricular interests and cultural contexts into the assignment generation process through a structured prompt engineering pipeline with input and output guardrails. The platform employs a serverless architecture on AWS with Next.js, using Llama 3.3 70B as the primary LLM via LiteLLM for multi-provider load balancing and LangChain for prompt orchestration. We describe the system architecture, the prompt design methodology, and the guardrails framework that ensures output quality. Preliminary user acceptance testing with 68 participants (65 students and 3 educators) indicates positive reception, with 84% of participants rating the personalization feature as beneficial. We discuss the platform's current capabilities and limitations, and outline directions for rigorous empirical evaluation of learning outcomes.

AI.tldr: Taklif.AI is an AI-powered platform that generates personalized assignments based on students' extracurricular interests and cultural contexts using LLMs, moving beyond traditional performance-based personalization to increase engagement and reduce plagiarism.
AI.motivation: Traditional one-size-fits-all assignments lead to decreased student engagement and increased plagiarism. Educators struggle to create engaging, personalized assignments that accommodate diverse student interests and cognitive abilities. Existing AI educational platforms only personalize based on academic performance metrics, missing important personalization dimensions.
AI.method: Taklif.AI uses a structured prompt engineering pipeline with input/output guardrails and LLMs (Llama 3.3 70B) to generate assignments incorporating extracurricular interests and cultural contexts. Built on AWS serverless architecture with Next.js, using LiteLLM for multi-provider load balancing and LangChain for prompt orchestration.
