AI.method: Proposes a Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust (TSDR) framework that combines: (1) a doubly robust formulation with propensity and imputation models for bias correction, (2) theoretical analysis showing estimator variance accumulates over time, and (3) smoothness regularization to control variance while preserving unbiasedness.
AI.result: TSDR consistently enhances various state-of-the-art KT backbones across multiple real-world benchmarks. The framework effectively reduces variance accumulation while maintaining the unbiasedness guarantee of doubly robust estimation.
AI.conclusion: Principled bias correction is vital for knowledge tracing. The proposed TSDR framework successfully addresses selection bias and variance accumulation through doubly robust estimation combined with temporal smoothness regularization, leading to improved performance across diverse KT models.

title: MDN: Parallelizing Stepwise Momentum for Delta Linear Attention
summary: Linear Attention (LA) offers a promising paradigm for scaling large language models (LLMs) to long sequences by avoiding the quadratic complexity of self-attention. Recent LA models such as Mamba2 and GDN interpret linear recurrences as closed-form online stochastic gradient descent (SGD), but naive SGD updates suffer from rapid information decay and suboptimal convergence in optimization. While momentum-based optimizers provide a natural remedy, they pose challenges in simultaneously achieving training efficiency and effectiveness. To address this, we develop a chunkwise parallel algorithm for LA with a stepwise momentum rule by geometrically reordering the update coefficients. Further, from a dynamical systems perspective, we analyze the momentum-based recurrence as a second-order system that introduces complex conjugate eigenvalues. This analysis guides the design of stable gating constraints. The resulting model, Momentum DeltaNet (MDN), leverages Triton kernels to achieve comparable training throughput with competitive linear models such as Mamba2 and KDA. Extensive experiments on the 400M and 1.3B parameter models demonstrate consistent performance improvements over strong baselines, including Transformers, Mamba2 and GDN, across diverse downstream evaluation benchmarks. Code: https://github.com/HuuYuLong/MomentumDeltaNet .

AI.tldr: Momentum DeltaNet (MDN) introduces momentum into linear attention models via a chunkwise parallel algorithm with stepwise momentum, improving long sequence modeling while maintaining training efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing linear attention models that interpret linear recurrences as SGD suffer from rapid information decay and suboptimal convergence. Momentum-based optimizers could help but pose challenges in achieving both training efficiency and effectiveness simultaneously.
AI.method: Developed a chunkwise parallel algorithm for linear attention with stepwise momentum rule by geometrically reordering update coefficients. Analyzed momentum-based recurrence as a second-order system with complex conjugate eigenvalues to guide stable gating constraints. Implemented using Triton kernels for efficiency.
AI.result: MDN achieves comparable training throughput with competitive linear models (Mamba2, KDA) while demonstrating consistent performance improvements over Transformers, Mamba2 and GDN across diverse benchmarks on 400M and 1.3B parameter models.
AI.conclusion: Momentum DeltaNet successfully integrates momentum into linear attention models, addressing optimization limitations while maintaining efficiency, leading to improved performance for long sequence modeling tasks.

title: From Coordinate Matching to Structural Alignment: Rethinking Prototype Alignment in Heterogeneous Federated Learning
summary: Heterogeneous federated learning (HtFL) aims to enable collaboration among clients that differ in both data distributions and model architectures. Prototype-based methods, which communicate class-level feature centers (prototypes) instead of full model parameters, have recently shown strong potential for HtFL. Existing prototype-based HtFL methods typically reuse the MSE-based or cosine-based alignment mechanism developed for homogeneous FL when aligning client-specific representations with global prototypes. These approaches are essentially coordinate alignment, where representations of clients are forced to match the global prototypes in the embedding space in an element-wise manner. Such alignment implicitly assumes that all clients should map their representations into the feature subspace defined by the global prototypes. This assumption is reasonable in homogeneous FL, where all clients share the same feature extractor. However, it becomes problematic in HtFL, since heterogeneous feature extractors naturally induce client-specific feature subspaces, and forcing all clients to optimize within a single global subspace unnecessarily suppresses their learning capacity. We observe that coordinate alignment implicitly couples two distinct objectives: aligning inter-class semantic structure, which is directly beneficial for classification, and enforcing a shared feature basis, which is unnecessary and even harmful under model heterogeneity. Building on this insight, we design FedSAF, which shifts the alignment objective from absolute coordinates to inter-class relational structure. We demonstrate that structural alignment consistently outperforms coordinate alignment in heterogeneous settings. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that our structural alignment outperforms state-of-the-art prototype-based HtFL methods by up to 3.52\%.

AI.tldr: FedSAF introduces structural alignment instead of coordinate alignment for heterogeneous federated learning, achieving better performance by focusing on inter-class relational structure rather than forcing clients into a single global feature subspace.
AI.motivation: Existing prototype-based HtFL methods use coordinate alignment (MSE or cosine-based) developed for homogeneous FL, which forces all clients to map representations into the same global feature subspace. This is problematic for HtFL because heterogeneous feature extractors naturally create client-specific feature subspaces, and forcing alignment to a single global subspace suppresses learning capacity.
AI.method: FedSAF shifts from coordinate alignment to structural alignment. Instead of aligning absolute coordinates, it focuses on aligning inter-class relational structure. This decouples the beneficial semantic structure alignment from the unnecessary shared feature basis requirement.
AI.result: Structural alignment consistently outperforms coordinate alignment in heterogeneous settings. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show FedSAF outperforms state-of-the-art prototype-based HtFL methods by up to 3.52%.
AI.conclusion: Coordinate alignment is suboptimal for HtFL because it couples semantic structure alignment with unnecessary feature subspace constraints. Structural alignment better preserves client-specific learning capacity while enabling effective collaboration across heterogeneous models.

title: Hypothesis generation and updating in large language models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly help people solve problems, from debugging code to repairing machinery. This process requires generating plausible hypotheses from partial descriptions, then updating them as more information arrives. Yet how LLMs perform this form of inference, and how close it is to optimal, remains unclear. We study this question in the number game, a controlled setting in which a learner infers the hypothesis supported by a few positive integers, such as $\{16, 8, 2, 64\}$: a rule like powers of 2 or an interval like numbers near 20. We measure the posterior over hypotheses using three complementary probes: posterior prediction, hypothesis evaluation, and hypothesis generation. We then compare LLM behavior with an optimal Bayesian model and human behavior, and test whether the same posterior is expressed across probes. LLMs are often well described by a two-parameter Bayesian fit, but with systematic offsets: by default they show a strong-sampling assumption that creates an implicit Occam's razor, favoring narrower hypotheses, while thinking mode shifts them toward greater prior reliance. We also find a robust evaluation--generation gap: LLMs select more correct hypotheses during hypothesis evaluation but generate simpler, more rule-like hypotheses. Finally, this Bayesian-with-bias pattern does not extrapolate. Models can behave as if they hold rule-like hypotheses over observed examples, yet generalize poorly to parts of the hypothesis domain not covered by those examples. Our results highlight a limitation of LLMs as general problem solvers, especially for scientific inference, where hypotheses must go beyond the data.

AI.tldr: LLMs show Bayesian-like inference with systematic biases in hypothesis testing, favoring simpler hypotheses and showing an evaluation-generation gap, with poor generalization beyond observed examples.
AI.motivation: To understand how LLMs perform inference in problem-solving scenarios involving hypothesis generation and updating from partial information, and to compare their performance with optimal Bayesian models and human behavior.
AI.method: Used the number game as a controlled setting, measuring posterior over hypotheses using three probes: posterior prediction, hypothesis evaluation, and hypothesis generation. Compared LLM behavior with optimal Bayesian models and human behavior, testing consistency across probes.
AI.result: LLMs are often well described by a two-parameter Bayesian fit with systematic offsets: they show a strong-sampling assumption favoring narrower hypotheses, and thinking mode shifts them toward greater prior reliance. Found a robust evaluation-generation gap where LLMs select more correct hypotheses during evaluation but generate simpler hypotheses. Bayesian-with-bias pattern doesn't extrapolate well to unseen parts of the hypothesis domain.
AI.conclusion: LLMs have limitations as general problem solvers for scientific inference, particularly in hypothesis generation that must go beyond observed data, showing systematic biases and poor generalization.

title: TheraAgent: Self-Improving Therapeutic Agent for Precise and Comprehensive Treatment Planning
summary: Formulating a treatment plan is inherently a complex reasoning and refinement task rather than a simple generation problem. However, existing large language models (LLMs) mainly rely on one-shot output without explicit verification, which may result in rough, incomplete, and potentially unsafe treatment plans. To address these limitations, we propose TheraAgent, an agentic framework that replaces one-shot generation with an iterative generate-judge-refine pipeline. By mirroring the actual reasoning process of human experts who iteratively revise treatment plans, our framework progressively transforms coarse and incomplete drafts into precise, comprehensive, and safer therapeutic regimens. To facilitate the critical judge component, we introduce TheraJudge, a treatment-specific evaluation module integrated into the inference loop to enforce clinical standards. Experiments show TheraAgent achieves state-of-the-art results on HealthBench, leading in Accuracy and Completeness. In expert evaluations, it attains an 86% win rate against physicians, with superior Targeting and Harm Control. Moreover, the highly agreement between TheraJudge and HealthBench evaluations confirms the reliability of our framework.

AI.tldr: TheraAgent is an agentic framework that replaces one-shot LLM generation with iterative generate-judge-refine pipelines for creating safer, more comprehensive treatment plans by mimicking human expert reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing LLMs produce treatment plans through one-shot generation without verification, resulting in rough, incomplete, and potentially unsafe plans that lack the iterative refinement process used by human experts.
AI.method: TheraAgent uses an agentic framework with iterative generate-judge-refine pipeline, where TheraJudge serves as a treatment-specific evaluation module integrated into the inference loop to enforce clinical standards, mimicking how human experts revise plans.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on HealthBench (leading in Accuracy and Completeness), 86% win rate against physicians in expert evaluations with superior Targeting and Harm Control, and high agreement between TheraJudge and HealthBench evaluations.
AI.conclusion: The iterative agentic framework significantly improves treatment plan quality and safety over one-shot LLM generation, demonstrating the value of mimicking human expert reasoning processes with specialized evaluation modules integrated into the inference loop.

title: Measuring Learning Progress via Gradient-Momentum Coupling
summary: Measuring learning progress is essential for curiosity-driven exploration in reinforcement learning, but widely used signals such as prediction error often fail to distinguish meaningful, learnable patterns from random noise. This paper proposes Gradient-Momentum Coupling (GMC), a signal derived from optimization dynamics that quantifies how useful each sample's gradient is for ongoing learning by measuring its per-parameter normalized absolute product with the momentum from previous gradients. By leveraging momentum's natural filtering of noise and oscillations, GMC identifies samples that contribute to ongoing parameter updates. Controlled experiments demonstrate noise robustness and emergent curriculum learning, with the signal prioritizing tasks by learning speed rather than difficulty. Experiments on MiniGrid suggest that replacing prediction error with GMC within existing curiosity-driven architectures can improve robustness to observation noise.

AI.tldr: Gradient-Momentum Coupling (GMC) is a new learning progress signal that uses optimization dynamics instead of prediction error, making curiosity-driven RL more robust to noise by measuring how gradients contribute to ongoing parameter updates.
AI.motivation: Existing learning progress signals like prediction error in curiosity-driven RL often fail to distinguish meaningful learnable patterns from random noise, which limits their effectiveness in exploration.
AI.method: The paper proposes Gradient-Momentum Coupling (GMC) - a signal derived from optimization dynamics that measures per-parameter normalized absolute product between each sample's gradient and the momentum from previous gradients. This leverages momentum's natural noise filtering to identify samples that actually contribute to ongoing learning.
AI.result: Controlled experiments show GMC has noise robustness and enables emergent curriculum learning by prioritizing tasks based on learning speed rather than difficulty. Experiments on MiniGrid demonstrate that replacing prediction error with GMC in existing curiosity-driven architectures improves robustness to observation noise.
AI.conclusion: GMC provides a more reliable learning progress signal than prediction error for curiosity-driven exploration in RL, offering better noise robustness and enabling more effective task prioritization through its optimization dynamics approach.

title: BehaviorGuard: Online Backdoor Defense for Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: Backdoor attacks pose a serious threat to deep reinforcement learning (DRL). Current defenses typically rely on reward anomalies to reverse-engineer triggers and model finetuning to remove backdoors. However, complex trigger patterns undermine their robustness, and fine-tuning entails high costs, limiting practical utility. Therefore, we shift defense concerns to trigger-agnostic backdoor output behaviors and propose BehaviorGuard, an online behavior-based backdoor detection and mitigation framework for DRL. Specifically, we find that regardless of attacks, backdoored policies induce consistent shifts in action distributions to ensure reliable activation, leaving detectable traces in high-quantile regions and distribution tails, even in the absence of triggers. Based on this, we design a novel metric that captures behavioral drift in action distributions to identify and suppress backdoor actions at runtime. To our knowledge, this is the first online backdoor defense that counters attacks both in single- and multi-agent DRL. Evaluated across diverse benchmarks with different backdoor attacks, BehaviorGuard consistently surpasses prior methods in both efficacy and efficiency.

AI.tldr: BehaviorGuard is an online behavior-based framework that detects and mitigates backdoor attacks in deep reinforcement learning by analyzing action distribution shifts, without needing to identify specific triggers.
AI.motivation: Current backdoor defenses for DRL rely on reward anomalies to reverse-engineer triggers and model fine-tuning, but these approaches are limited by complex trigger patterns and high computational costs, reducing their practical utility.
AI.method: BehaviorGuard shifts focus to trigger-agnostic backdoor output behaviors by analyzing action distribution shifts. The framework identifies that backdoored policies induce consistent shifts in action distributions, detectable in high-quantile regions and distribution tails. It uses a novel metric to capture behavioral drift in action distributions to identify and suppress backdoor actions at runtime.
AI.result: Evaluated across diverse benchmarks with different backdoor attacks, BehaviorGuard consistently surpasses prior methods in both efficacy and efficiency. It is the first online backdoor defense that works for both single- and multi-agent DRL.
AI.conclusion: BehaviorGuard provides a practical, trigger-agnostic defense against backdoor attacks in DRL by focusing on behavioral analysis rather than trigger identification, offering superior performance and efficiency compared to existing methods.

title: Offline Reinforcement Learning for Rotation Profile Control in Tokamaks
summary: Tokamaks remain leading candidates for achieving practical fusion energy, yet many important control problems inside these devices are still difficult or unsolved. One such challenge is controlling the plasma rotation profile, which strongly influences stability, confinement, and transport. While the average rotation can be controlled, controlling the full profile is challenging due to high dimensionality, response to multiple actuators and dependence on plasma condition. Learning-based control methods, such as reinforcement learning (RL), provide a potential solution to this challenging problem with ability to model complex interactions leading to effective multi-input multi-output control. However, learning such policies is challenging due to the lack of accurate simulators that can model the rotation profile dynamics. In this work, we investigate the use of offline RL and offline model-based RL algorithms for rotation profile control, training them solely on historical data from the DIII-D tokamak. Our final method uses probabilistic models of plasma dynamics to generate rollouts for RL training. We deploy this policy on the DIII-D Tokamak and observe promising real-world results. We conclude by highlighting key challenges and insights from training and deploying an RL policy on a complex physical device while using only limited past data.

AI.tldr: Researchers successfully deployed offline reinforcement learning and model-based RL for plasma rotation profile control on DIII-D tokamak using only historical data, achieving promising real-world results.
AI.motivation: Tokamak plasma rotation profile control is challenging due to high dimensionality, multiple actuators, and plasma condition dependence. Traditional methods struggle with full profile control, and learning-based approaches are needed but hindered by lack of accurate simulators.
AI.method: Used offline RL and offline model-based RL algorithms trained solely on historical data from DIII-D tokamak. Specifically employed probabilistic models of plasma dynamics to generate rollouts for RL training.
AI.result: Successfully deployed the RL policy on the DIII-D Tokamak and observed promising real-world results, demonstrating effective multi-input multi-output control for plasma rotation profile.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates the feasibility of using offline RL with only historical data for complex physical system control, while highlighting key challenges and insights from training and deploying RL policies on real-world fusion devices.

title: TACT: Mitigating Overthinking and Overacting in Coding Agents via Activation Steering
summary: When language model agents tackle complex software engineering tasks, they often degrade over long trajectories, which we define as *agent drift*. We focus on two recurring failure modes *overthinking* and *overacting*, i.e., where the agent repeatedly reasons over information it already has, and where it issues tool calls without integrating recent observations or acquiring new evidence. In this paper, we introduce TACT (Think-Act Calibration via activation Steering), to detect and mitigate agent drift in the residual stream before it surfaces as a behavioral failure. In specific, we label trajectory steps as overthinking, overacting, or calibrated, and find that their hidden states can separate linearly along two *drift axes*, pointing from calibrated behavior toward each failure mode (AUC $\approx$ 0.9). To mitigate agent drift, we project each step's activation onto these axes at test time and pull drifted ones back toward the calibrated region. Experiments show that TACT outperforms unsteered baselines across SWE-bench Verified, Terminal-Bench 2.0, and CLAW-Eval, lifting average resolve rate by $+5.8$ pp on Qwen3.5-27B and $+4.8$ pp on Gemma-4-26B-A4B-it while cutting steps-to-resolve by up to $26\%$. These gains frame agent drift as a steerable direction in the residual stream, and position TACT as a viable handle for reliable long-horizon agents.

AI.tldr: TACT (Think-Act Calibration via activation Steering) detects and mitigates agent drift in language model agents by identifying steerable directions in the residual stream to prevent overthinking and overacting failures.
AI.motivation: Language model agents degrade over long trajectories when tackling complex software engineering tasks, exhibiting two recurring failure modes: overthinking (repeatedly reasoning over existing information) and overacting (issuing tool calls without integrating recent observations). This degradation is called "agent drift."
AI.method: The authors label trajectory steps as overthinking, overacting, or calibrated, and find hidden states separate linearly along two "drift axes" pointing from calibrated behavior toward each failure mode. They then project each step's activation onto these axes at test time and pull drifted ones back toward the calibrated region to mitigate drift.
AI.result: Experiments show TACT outperforms unsteered baselines across SWE-bench Verified, Terminal-Bench 2.0, and CLAW-Eval, lifting average resolve rate by +5.8 percentage points on Qwen3.5-27B and +4.8 pp on Gemma-4-26B-A4B-it while cutting steps-to-resolve by up to 26%.
AI.conclusion: Agent drift represents a steerable direction in the residual stream, and TACT serves as a viable method for building reliable long-horizon agents by proactively detecting and correcting drift before it surfaces as behavioral failure.

title: Do Neural Operators Forget Geometry? The Forgetting Hypothesis in Deep Operator Learning
summary:
Neural operators perform well on structured domains, yet their behaviour on irregular geometries remains poorly understood. We show that this limitation is not merely an encoding issue, but a depth-wise failure mode inherent to deep operator architectures.
  We formalise the Geometric Forgetting Hypothesis: due to the Markovian structure of operator layers and their reliance on global mixing mechanisms, neural operators progressively lose access to domain geometry as depth increases. Using layer-wise geometric probing, we demonstrate that both spectral and attention-based operators systematically lose geometric fidelity.
  We show that this geometric forgetting degrades accuracy, stability, and generalisation. To counteract it, we introduce a lightweight geometry memory injection mechanism that restores geometric constraints at intermediate depths with minimal architectural overhead. This simple intervention consistently mitigates forgetting and exposes a geometric shortcut instability in transformer-based operators, revealing that geometric retention is a structural requirement rather than a design choice.

AI.tldr: Neural operators progressively lose geometric information as depth increases (Geometric Forgetting Hypothesis), degrading performance on irregular geometries. The paper introduces geometry memory injection to counteract this issue.
AI.motivation: Neural operators perform well on structured domains but struggle with irregular geometries. The authors investigate whether this limitation is merely an encoding issue or a deeper architectural problem inherent to deep operator networks.
AI.method: The paper formalizes the Geometric Forgetting Hypothesis, uses layer-wise geometric probing to analyze spectral and attention-based operators, and introduces a lightweight geometry memory injection mechanism to restore geometric constraints at intermediate depths with minimal architectural overhead.
AI.result: Demonstrates that neural operators systematically lose geometric fidelity as depth increases, which degrades accuracy, stability, and generalization. The geometry memory injection consistently mitigates forgetting and reveals geometric shortcut instability in transformer-based operators.
AI.conclusion: Geometric forgetting is a fundamental failure mode in deep operator architectures, not just an encoding issue. Geometric retention is a structural requirement rather than a design choice, and the proposed intervention effectively counters this limitation.

title: SOPE: Stabilizing Off-Policy Evaluation for Online RL with Prior Data
summary: Incorporating prior data into online reinforcement learning accelerates training but typically forces a difficult trade-off between high computational costs and long, multi-stage training pipelines. While fixed-length stabilization phases are significantly more computationally efficient than static update schedules, they require task-dependent manual tuning, risking either the waste of prior knowledge or severe overfitting. To address this, we propose SOPE, an algorithm that uses an actor-aligned Off-Policy Policy Evaluation (OPE) signal as an automated early-stopping mechanism to dynamically control the length of offline training phases. By evaluating the critic on a held-out validation split under the current policy's action distribution, SOPE halts gradient updates exactly when out-of-distribution benefits saturate, eliminating the need for manual schedule tuning. Evaluated on 25 continuous control tasks from the Minari benchmark suite, SOPE improves baseline performance by up to 45.6% while reducing the required TFLOPs by up to 22x, thus balancing the tradeoff between sample and computational efficiency. These findings demonstrate that adaptive, evaluation-driven update schedules are more effective than relying on static, exhaustive update schedules.

AI.tldr: SOPE is an algorithm that uses actor-aligned off-policy policy evaluation as an automated early-stopping mechanism to dynamically control offline training phases, achieving better performance with significantly less computation than static methods.
AI.motivation: Current methods for incorporating prior data into online RL face a difficult trade-off: either accept high computational costs from static update schedules or rely on manually-tuned fixed-length stabilization phases that risk wasting prior knowledge or causing severe overfitting.
AI.method: SOPE uses actor-aligned Off-Policy Policy Evaluation (OPE) as an automated early-stopping mechanism. It evaluates the critic on a held-out validation split under the current policy's action distribution, dynamically halting gradient updates when out-of-distribution benefits saturate.
AI.result: On 25 continuous control tasks from the Minari benchmark suite, SOPE improves baseline performance by up to 45.6% while reducing required computational resources by up to 22x (measured in TFLOPs).
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, evaluation-driven update schedules are more effective than static, exhaustive update schedules for balancing the trade-off between sample and computational efficiency in offline-to-online reinforcement learning.

title: Strat-LLM: Stratified Strategy Alignment for LLM-based Stock Trading with Real-time Multi-Source Signals
comment: Accepted by the 2026 International Joint Conference on Neural Networks (IJCNN)
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are evolving into autonomous trading agents, yet existing benchmarks often overlook the interplay between architectural reasoning and strategy consistency. We propose Strat-LLM, a framework grounded in Stratified Strategy Alignment. Operating in a live-forward setting throughout 2025, it integrates heterogeneous data including sequential prices, real-time news, and annual reports to eliminate look-ahead bias. Extensive stress tests on A-share and U.S. markets reveal: (1) reasoning-heavy models achieve peak utility in Free Mode via internal logic, whereas standard models require Strict Mode as a vital risk anchor; (2) alignment utility is regime-dependent, with Free and Guided modes capturing momentum in uptrending markets, while Strict Mode mitigates drawdowns in downtrends; (3) mid-scale models (35B) show optimal fidelity under strict constraints, whereas ultra-large models (122B) suffer an alignment tax under rigid rules but gain a performance premium in Guided Mode; (4) standard LLMs often fall into a high win-rate trap, optimizing for small gains at the expense of total returns, which can only be mitigated through deep reasoning or strict external guardrails. Project details are available at https://Strat-LLM.github.io.

AI.tldr: Strat-LLM proposes a stratified strategy alignment framework for LLM-based trading agents, tested in 2025 live-forward settings to eliminate look-ahead bias, revealing how different model architectures and alignment modes perform across market regimes.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based trading agent benchmarks often overlook the interplay between architectural reasoning capabilities and strategy consistency, creating gaps in understanding how different model types should be aligned for optimal trading performance.
AI.method: Stratified Strategy Alignment framework (Strat-LLM) operating in live-forward setting throughout 2025, integrating heterogeneous data including sequential prices, real-time news, and annual reports. Tested on A-share and U.S. markets with extensive stress tests across three alignment modes: Free Mode (reasoning-heavy models), Strict Mode (standard models with external constraints), and Guided Mode (balanced approach).
AI.result: Four key findings: 1) Reasoning-heavy models excel in Free Mode using internal logic, while standard models need Strict Mode for risk control; 2) Alignment utility is regime-dependent with different modes optimal for uptrending vs downtrending markets; 3) Mid-scale models (35B) perform best under strict constraints, while ultra-large models (122B) suffer under rigid rules but gain in Guided Mode; 4) Standard LLMs fall into high win-rate traps optimizing small gains at expense of total returns.
AI.conclusion: The optimal alignment strategy for LLM trading agents depends on model architecture, market regime, and model scale. A stratified approach matching reasoning capabilities with appropriate constraints is essential for robust trading performance, with different model types requiring different alignment strategies to avoid pitfalls like high win-rate traps.

title: QuadraSHAP: Stable and Scalable Shapley Values for Product Games via Gauss-Legendre Quadrature
summary: We study the efficient computation of Shapley values for \emph{product games} -- cooperative games in which the coalition value factorizes as a product of per-player terms. Such games arise in machine learning explainability whenever the value function inherits a multiplicative structure from the underlying model, as in kernel methods with product kernels and tree-based models. Our key result is that the Shapley value of each player in a product game admits an exact one-dimensional integral representation: the weighted sum over exponentially many feature coalitions collapses to the integral of a degree-$(d-1)$ polynomial over $[0,1]$, where $d$ is the total number of features. This yields a Gauss--Legendre quadrature scheme that is \emph{provably exact} whenever the number of nodes satisfies $m_q \geq \lceil d/2 \rceil$, and otherwise provides a \emph{near-exact} approximation with error provably decaying geometrically in $m_q$. In practice, a few hundred nodes can achieve highly precise estimates even with thousands of features. Building on this formulation, we derive a numerically stable implementation via log-space evaluation, together with an efficient parallel implementation based on associative scan primitives that achieves $O(d\,m_q)$ total work and $O(\log d)$ parallel time. Experiments show that \textsc{QuadraSHAP} is the fastest numerically stable method across all tested configurations.

AI.tldr: Exact one-dimensional integral representation for Shapley values in product games with provably exact Gauss-Legendre quadrature requiring only O(⌈d/2⌉) nodes, enabling fast computation even for thousands of features.
AI.motivation: Product games arise in ML explainability when value functions inherit multiplicative structure from models like kernel methods with product kernels and tree-based models. Current Shapley value computation requires summing over exponentially many feature coalitions, which is computationally prohibitive for high-dimensional settings.
AI.method: Derived an exact 1D integral representation for Shapley values in product games, showing they collapse to integral of degree-(d-1) polynomial over [0,1]. Used Gauss-Legendre quadrature that is provably exact with m_q ≥ ⌈d/2⌉ nodes. Implemented numerically stable log-space evaluation and efficient parallel implementation using associative scan primitives (O(d m_q) work, O(log d) parallel time).
AI.result: The method (QuadraSHAP) achieves highly precise estimates with few hundred nodes even for thousands of features. It's provably exact with sufficient nodes and otherwise provides geometrically decaying error. Experimental results show QuadraSHAP is the fastest numerically stable method across all tested configurations.
AI.conclusion: The integral representation enables efficient computation of Shapley values for product games, providing a theoretically grounded and practically efficient solution for ML explainability tasks where multiplicative structure exists, overcoming exponential complexity barriers.

title: Pathways to AGI
summary: Our focus are five related questions that stem from a critical software studies perspective. Underpinning this view is the acknowledged need to avoid assumptions regarding the inevitability of the current situation relating to AI. What we need to see is the closeness of the linkage between current commercial AI development and our prevailing social, political and economic circumstances. This does mean that the perspectives presented here are done so critically and conditionally. Most importantly, Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) is seen as being problematic both conceptually and definitionally. This conditioning of any view regarding AGI does lead the discussion in specific directions and to certain conclusions regarding the future. However, adopting this perspective enables the work to offer some final recommendations. We set out to ask the following questions, 1. What are the critical pathways that produced the current dominant generative AI tools (capabilities, product forms, adoption patterns)? 2. Which decision points acted as leverage nodes (small changes that had large downstream effects), and which dead ends reveal alternative possibilities that did not become dominant? 3. How do pathways differ across three foundational-model trajectories such as the frontier proprietary models, open-weight models or specific domain and sovereign models? 4. Which alternative projects branched from key leverage nodes, what is their current state, and why did some succeed, stall, fail or become absorbed? 5. Based on this analysis, what socio-technical development programmes could plausibly move toward AGI-adjacent capability while meeting requirements for transparency, moderation, wellbeing and sustainable business models?

AI.tldr: A critical software studies perspective examining five key questions about AI development pathways, decision points, and alternative trajectories, with emphasis on the conceptual problems of AGI and recommendations for socio-technical development.
AI.motivation: The paper adopts a critical software studies perspective to challenge assumptions about AI's inevitability and examine the close linkage between commercial AI development and current social, political, and economic circumstances. It aims to avoid accepting the current AI situation as predetermined and instead critically analyze how we arrived at the present state.
AI.method: The research employs a critical software studies approach, examining five related questions: (1) critical pathways producing current generative AI tools, (2) leverage nodes and dead ends in decision points, (3) differences across foundational-model trajectories, (4) analysis of alternative projects from key nodes, and (5) development of socio-technical programs. The perspective treats AGI as conceptually problematic.
AI.result: The paper develops critical and conditional perspectives on AI development, leading to specific directions regarding the future of AI. It analyzes different development pathways (frontier proprietary, open-weight, domain-specific models) and identifies leverage nodes and alternative possibilities. Based on this analysis, the work offers final recommendations for socio-technical development programs.
AI.conclusion: Adopting a critical perspective enables recommendations for alternative development pathways toward AGI-adjacent capabilities that meet requirements for transparency, moderation, wellbeing, and sustainable business models. The paper emphasizes the need to move beyond current commercial AI trajectories and develop socio-technical approaches that align with broader societal values.

title: Retain-Neutral Surrogates for Min-Max Unlearning
summary: Machine unlearning seeks to remove the influence of designated training data while preserving performance on the remaining data. Approximate unlearning can be viewed as a local editing problem; in min-max unlearning, the key local object is the surrogate point at which the retain objective is evaluated. When forget and retain gradients are strongly aligned, an unconstrained forget-maximizing perturbation can move to a surrogate point that increases retain loss. We propose Retain-Orthogonal Surrogate Unlearning (ROSU), which constrains the inner surrogate construction by maximizing first-order forget gain subject to zero first-order retain change under a fixed perturbation budget. This yields a closed-form retain-orthogonal perturbation, a lightweight transported outer update, and amplification along the retain-neutral direction. Our analysis establishes (i) a curvature-controlled second-order bound on retain damage, (ii) a positive-alignment regime in which ROSU strictly reduces surrogate retain loss relative to standard min-max perturbations, and (iii) near-equivalence when the two gradients are nearly orthogonal. Across vision and language benchmarks (CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, TOFU, WMDP), the empirical pattern follows this geometry: ROSU gives its clearest gains in high-coupling regimes while remaining competitive elsewhere.

AI.tldr: ROSU is a new machine unlearning method that constrains surrogate point updates to be orthogonal to retain gradients, preventing damage to retained data performance while effectively removing forgotten data influence.
AI.motivation: Existing approximate unlearning methods, particularly min-max approaches, can inadvertently increase retain loss when forget and retain gradients are strongly aligned, potentially harming performance on the remaining data while trying to forget specific samples.
AI.method: Retain-Orthogonal Surrogate Unlearning (ROSU) introduces a constrained surrogate construction: maximize first-order forget gain while maintaining zero first-order retain change under a fixed perturbation budget. This yields a closed-form retain-orthogonal perturbation, lightweight transported outer update, and amplification along retain-neutral directions.
AI.result: ROSU provides theoretical guarantees including curvature-controlled second-order bounds on retain damage, strict reduction of surrogate retain loss in positive-alignment regimes, and near-equivalence to standard methods when gradients are orthogonal. Empirically, ROSU shows clearest gains in high-coupling regimes (where gradients align strongly) while remaining competitive elsewhere across vision and language benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: ROSU offers a geometrically-grounded solution to machine unlearning that effectively prevents retain loss damage during forgetting operations, particularly valuable in practical scenarios where forget and retain gradients often align strongly.

title: Novelty-based Tree-of-Thought Search for LLM Reasoning and Planning
summary: Although advances such as chain-of-thought, tree-of-thought or reinforcement learning have improved the performance of LLMs in reasoning and planning tasks, they are still brittle and have not achieved human-level performance in many domains, and often suffer from high time and token costs. Inspired by the success of width-based search in planning, we explore how the concept of novelty can be transferred to language domains and how it can improve tree-of-thought reasoning. A tree of thoughts relies on building possible "paths" of consecutive ideas or thoughts. These are generated by repeatedly prompting an LLM. In our paper, a measurable concept of novelty is proposed that describes the uniqueness of a new node (thought) in comparison to nodes previously seen in the search tree. Novelty is estimated by prompting an LLM and making use of embedded general knowledge from pre-training. This metric can then be used to prune branches and reduce the scope of the search. Although this method introduces more prompts per state, the overall token cost can be reduced by pruning and reducing the overall tree size. This procedure is tested and compared using several benchmarks in language-based planning and general reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novelty-based pruning method for tree-of-thought reasoning in LLMs, using LLM-prompted novelty estimation to reduce search space and token costs while improving performance.
AI.motivation: Current LLM reasoning methods (chain-of-thought, tree-of-thought, reinforcement learning) are brittle, haven't achieved human-level performance in many domains, and suffer from high time and token costs. There's a need to improve tree-of-thought reasoning efficiency.
AI.method: Proposes a measurable concept of novelty that assesses uniqueness of new nodes (thoughts) compared to previously seen nodes in the search tree. Novelty is estimated by prompting an LLM and leveraging embedded general knowledge from pre-training. This metric is used to prune branches and reduce search scope.
AI.result: Although the method introduces more prompts per state, overall token cost can be reduced by pruning and reducing overall tree size. The procedure was tested and compared using several benchmarks in language-based planning and general reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Transferring width-based search concepts from planning to language domains through novelty-based pruning can improve tree-of-thought reasoning efficiency by reducing search space and token costs while maintaining or improving performance.

title: RepFlow: Representation Enhanced Flow Matching for Causal Effect Estimation
summary:
Estimating causal effects from observational data has become increasingly critical in diverse fields including healthcare, economics, and social policy. The fundamental challenge in causal inference arises from the missing counterfactuals and the selection bias. Existing methods are largely limited to point estimates and lack the capacity for distribution modeling. In this work, we propose RepFlow, a novel framework that formulates causal effect estimation as a joint optimization problem integrating representation learning with Conditional Flow Matching (CFM).
  RepFlow mitigates selection bias by minimizing the entropically regularized Wasserstein distance between treated and control representations.
  To enhance numerical stability, we further introduce an $L_2$ normalization constraint on latent representations.
  This balanced representation enables the flow model to accurately capture the distribution of potential outcomes. Extensive experiments across a wide range of benchmarks demonstrate that RepFlow consistently outperforms existing methods in both point and distributional causal effect estimation.

AI.tldr: RepFlow is a novel framework for causal effect estimation that combines representation learning with Conditional Flow Matching to address selection bias and enable distribution modeling of potential outcomes.
AI.motivation: Causal effect estimation from observational data is critical but faces fundamental challenges: missing counterfactuals and selection bias. Existing methods are limited to point estimates and lack distribution modeling capabilities.
AI.method: RepFlow formulates causal effect estimation as joint optimization integrating representation learning with Conditional Flow Matching. It mitigates selection bias by minimizing entropically regularized Wasserstein distance between treated and control representations, with L2 normalization for stability.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across benchmarks show RepFlow consistently outperforms existing methods in both point and distributional causal effect estimation.
AI.conclusion: RepFlow provides an effective framework for causal inference that addresses key limitations of existing methods by enabling distribution modeling while handling selection bias through integrated representation learning and flow matching.

title: Visual Fingerprints for LLM Generation Comparison
comment: Submitted to the Short Paper track at IEEE VIS 2026
summary: Large language model (LLM) outputs arise from complex interactions among prompts, system instructions, model parameters, and architecture. We refer to specific configurations of these factors as generation conditions, each of which can bias outputs in various ways. Understanding how different generation conditions shape model behaviors is essential for tasks such as prompt design and model evaluation, yet it remains challenging due to the stochastic and open-ended nature of text generation. We present an approach to visually compare LLM outputs across generation conditions by modeling responses as collections of linguistic choices, including content, expression, and structure. We extract these choices using natural language processing pipelines and represent their distributions across repeated samples. We then visualize these distributions as visual fingerprints, enabling direct, distribution-level comparison of condition-specific tendencies. Through four usage scenarios, we demonstrate how visual fingerprints reveal consistent patterns in LLM behavior that are difficult to observe through individual responses or aggregate metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "visual fingerprints" - a method to compare LLM outputs across different generation conditions by modeling responses as linguistic choices and visualizing their distributions, revealing consistent behavioral patterns.
AI.motivation: Understanding how different generation conditions (prompts, instructions, parameters, architecture) shape LLM behaviors is essential for prompt design and model evaluation, but challenging due to the stochastic and open-ended nature of text generation.
AI.method: The approach models LLM responses as collections of linguistic choices (content, expression, structure), extracts these choices using NLP pipelines, represents their distributions across repeated samples, and visualizes them as "visual fingerprints" for direct comparison of condition-specific tendencies.
AI.result: Through four usage scenarios, the visual fingerprints reveal consistent patterns in LLM behavior that are difficult to observe through individual responses or aggregate metrics, enabling distribution-level comparison of condition-specific tendencies.
AI.conclusion: Visual fingerprints provide an effective method for understanding how generation conditions shape LLM outputs, offering insights that go beyond traditional individual response analysis and aggregate metrics, with applications in prompt engineering and model evaluation.

title: VARS-FL: Validation-Aligned Client Selection for Non-IID Federated Learning in IoT Systems
summary: Federated learning (FL) systems typically employ stateless client selection, treating each communication round independently and ignoring accumulated evidence of client contribution quality. Under non-IID data, this leads to slow convergence and unstable training, particularly when selection relies on local proxies (e.g., training loss) that are misaligned with the global optimization objective. These challenges are especially pronounced in Internet of Things (IoT) and Industrial IoT (IIoT) environments, where data is highly heterogeneous and distributed across devices observing different traffic patterns. In this paper, we propose VARS-FL (Validation-Aligned Reputation Scoring for Federated Learning), a client selection framework that quantifies each client's contribution using the reduction in server-side validation loss induced by its update. These per-round signals are aggregated into a Reputation score that combines a sliding-window average of recent contributions with a logarithmically scaled participation term, enabling robust exploration-exploitation selection. VARS-FL requires no changes to local training or aggregation and remains fully compatible with standard FedAvg. We evaluate VARS-FL on a 15-class non-IID IoT intrusion detection task using the Edge-IIoTset dataset, with 100 clients across multiple seeds, and compare it against FedAvg, Oort, and Power-of-Choice. VARS-FL consistently improves accuracy, F1-Macro, and loss, while accelerating convergence (up to 36% fewer rounds to reach 80% accuracy). These results demonstrate that validation-aligned, history-aware client selection provides a more reliable and efficient training process for federated learning in heterogeneous IoT environments.

AI.tldr: VARS-FL introduces a reputation-based client selection method for federated learning that tracks historical contributions via validation loss reduction, improving convergence and performance in non-IID IoT environments.
AI.motivation: Current FL systems use stateless client selection that ignores accumulated evidence of client contributions, leading to slow convergence and unstable training under non-IID data, especially in IoT/IIoT environments with highly heterogeneous data and different traffic patterns.
AI.method: Proposes VARS-FL framework that quantifies each client's contribution using reduction in server-side validation loss induced by their update, aggregated into a Reputation score combining sliding-window average of recent contributions with logarithmically scaled participation term for exploration-exploitation selection. Requires no changes to local training or aggregation and is fully compatible with standard FedAvg.
AI.result: Evaluated on 15-class non-IID IoT intrusion detection task using Edge-IIoTset dataset with 100 clients across multiple seeds. VARS-FL consistently improves accuracy, F1-Macro, and loss while accelerating convergence (up to 36% fewer rounds to reach 80% accuracy) compared to FedAvg, Oort, and Power-of-Choice.
AI.conclusion: Validation-aligned, history-aware client selection provides more reliable and efficient training process for federated learning in heterogeneous IoT environments, demonstrating superior performance over existing client selection methods.

title: VibeServe: Can AI Agents Build Bespoke LLM Serving Systems?
summary: For years, we have built LLM serving systems like any other critical infrastructure: a single general-purpose stack, hand-tuned over many engineer-years, meant to support every model and workload. In this paper, we take the opposite bet: a multi-agent loop that automatically synthesizes bespoke serving systems for different usage scenarios. We propose VibeServe, the first agentic loop that generates entire LLM serving stacks end-to-end. VibeServe uses an outer loop to plan and track the search over system designs, and an inner loop to implement candidates, check correctness, and measure performance on the target benchmark. In the standard deployment setting, where existing stacks are highly optimized, VibeServe remains competitive with vLLM, showing that generation-time specialization need not come at the cost of performance. More interestingly, in non-standard scenarios, VibeServe outperforms existing systems by exploiting opportunities that generic systems miss in six scenarios involving non-standard model architectures, workload knowledge, and hardware-specific optimizations. Together, these results suggest a different point in the design space for infrastructure software: generation-time specialization rather than runtime generality. Code is available at https://github.com/uw-syfi/vibe-serve.

AI.tldr: VibeServe introduces an agentic loop that automatically generates specialized LLM serving systems tailored to specific usage scenarios, moving from generic hand-tuned infrastructure to automatic generation-time specialization.
AI.motivation: Traditional LLM serving systems are built as single general-purpose stacks that require extensive manual tuning and optimization. This approach doesn't adapt well to different models, workloads, and hardware scenarios, creating inefficiencies when dealing with non-standard deployment scenarios.
AI.method: VibeServe uses a multi-agent loop with two components: an outer loop to plan and track system design search, and an inner loop to implement candidate systems, verify correctness, and measure performance on target benchmarks. It automatically synthesizes bespoke serving systems end-to-end.
AI.result: In standard deployment scenarios, VibeServe remains competitive with highly optimized existing systems like vLLM. More significantly, in six non-standard scenarios involving unusual model architectures, workload patterns, and hardware-specific requirements, VibeServe outperforms existing systems by exploiting optimization opportunities that generic systems miss.
AI.conclusion: The results suggest a paradigm shift in infrastructure software design: moving from runtime generality to generation-time specialization, where systems are automatically synthesized for specific scenarios rather than manually optimized for broad applicability.

title: VisMMOE: Exploiting Visual-Expert Affinity for Efficient Visual-Language MoE Offloading
summary: Large-scale vision-language mixture-of-experts (VL-MoE) models provide strong multimodal capability, but efficient deployment on memory-constrained platforms remains difficult. Existing MoE offloading systems are largely designed for text-centric workloads and become much less effective for visual-heavy inputs, where large numbers of visual tokens induce broader and less predictable expert accesses. We present VisMMoE, a VL-MoE offloading system built on a single systems insight: pruning redundant visual tokens can improve offloading not only by reducing computation, but also by reshaping expert demand. We refer to this effect as \textit{visual-expert affinity}: token pruning makes expert accesses more concentrated within layers and more stable across layers, producing a smaller and more predictable expert working set. Guided by this insight, VisMMoE combines affinity-aware token compression, lookahead expert prediction, and cache/pipeline orchestration to improve expert locality and prefetch effectiveness under tight memory budgets. We implement VisMMoE on multiple frameworks and evaluate it on representative VL-MoE models and benchmarks. VisMMoE improves end-to-end inference performance by up to 2.68x and 1.61x, respectively, over strong baselines for today's VL-MoE deployments while maintaining competitive accuracy.

AI.tldr: VisMMoE is a system that improves deployment efficiency of large vision-language MoE models by pruning redundant visual tokens to enhance expert locality and prefetching, achieving significant speedups under memory constraints.
AI.motivation: Large-scale VL-MoE models have strong multimodal capabilities but face deployment challenges on memory-constrained platforms. Existing MoE offloading systems designed for text workloads are ineffective for visual-heavy inputs, where numerous visual tokens cause broad and unpredictable expert accesses.
AI.method: VisMMoE leverages the insight that pruning redundant visual tokens improves offloading by reducing computation and reshaping expert demand (visual-expert affinity). It combines affinity-aware token compression, lookahead expert prediction, and cache/pipeline orchestration to improve expert locality and prefetch effectiveness.
AI.result: VisMMoE improves end-to-end inference performance by up to 2.68x and 1.61x over strong baselines for current VL-MoE deployments while maintaining competitive accuracy. The system was implemented on multiple frameworks and evaluated on representative VL-MoE models and benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Token pruning not only reduces computation but also fundamentally reshapes expert demand patterns in VL-MoE models, enabling efficient deployment through improved expert locality and predictable working sets. The visual-expert affinity insight provides a foundation for optimizing multimodal MoE systems on memory-constrained platforms.

title: Safety Certification is Classification
summary: The goal of this paper is certifying safety of dynamical systems subject to uncertainty. Existing approaches use trajectory data to estimate transition probabilities, and compute safety probabilities recursively via dynamic programming (DP). This recursion may lead to compounding errors in the certified safety probability, thus collapsing to a vacuous lower bound for growing horizons $T$. We propose a kernel embedding framework that treats safety certification as a classification problem on trajectory data, directly estimating the $T$-step safety probability without recursion. We show that the framework subsumes well-established approaches from the literature (e.g., barrier certificates, robust Markov models) as special cases, and allows us to go beyond their limitations. As the main consequence, it bypasses compounding error across the horizon and enables certification for systems with non-Markovian dynamics. We demonstrate that direct estimators remain stable independent of the certification horizon and in the non-Markovian setting, whilst DP-based certificates silently go unsound -- confirmed in simulation on a neural-controlled quadrotor.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a kernel embedding framework that directly estimates T-step safety probabilities from trajectory data without recursive dynamic programming, avoiding compounding errors and working with non-Markovian systems.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches for safety certification of dynamical systems use recursive dynamic programming (DP) on estimated transition probabilities, which leads to compounding errors that collapse safety probability bounds to vacuous values for long horizons. These methods also require Markovian assumptions.
AI.method: The authors propose a kernel embedding framework that treats safety certification as a classification problem on trajectory data. Instead of recursive DP, they directly estimate T-step safety probabilities, bypassing the recursion step entirely.
AI.result: The framework subsumes existing approaches (barrier certificates, robust Markov models) as special cases, goes beyond their limitations, avoids compounding errors, works with non-Markovian dynamics, and remains stable independent of certification horizon. Simulation on a neural-controlled quadrotor confirms DP-based certificates become unsound while direct estimators remain reliable.
AI.conclusion: Direct safety certification via kernel embedding avoids compounding errors inherent in DP-based approaches, enables certification for non-Markovian systems, and provides stable safety guarantees independent of horizon length.

title: Quadratic Objective Perturbation: Curvature-Based Differential Privacy
summary: Objective perturbation is a standard mechanism in differentially private empirical risk minimization. In particular, Linear Objective Perturbation (LOP) enforces privacy by adding a random linear term, while strong convexity and stability are ensured by an additional deterministic quadratic term. However, this approach requires the strong assumption of bounded gradients of the loss function, which excludes many modern machine learning models. In this work, we introduce Quadratic Objective Perturbation (QOP), which perturbs the objective with a random quadratic form. This perturbation induces strong convexity and enforces stability of the problem through curvature, thereby enabling privacy and allowing sensitivity to be controlled through spectral properties of the perturbation rather than assumptions on the gradients. As a result, we obtain $(\varepsilon, δ)$-differential privacy under weaker assumptions, in the interpolation regime. Furthermore, we extend the analysis to account for approximate solutions, showing that privacy guarantees are preserved under inexact solves. Additionally, we derive utility guarantees in terms of empirical excess risk, and provide a theoretical and numerical comparison to LOP, highlighting the advantages of curvature-based perturbations. Finally, we discuss algorithmic aspects and show that the resulting problems can be solved efficiently using modern splitting schemes.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Quadratic Objective Perturbation (QOP) for differentially private empirical risk minimization, replacing traditional linear perturbation with random quadratic forms to achieve stronger privacy guarantees with weaker assumptions.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome limitations of Linear Objective Perturbation (LOP) which requires the strong assumption of bounded gradients of the loss function. This assumption excludes many modern machine learning models, so the authors seek a more general privacy mechanism with weaker requirements.
AI.method: The method introduces Quadratic Objective Perturbation (QOP) which perturbs the objective function with a random quadratic form instead of a linear term. This perturbation induces strong convexity through curvature, enabling privacy through spectral properties of the perturbation rather than gradient bounds. The analysis extends to account for approximate solutions and inexact solves.
AI.result: The paper achieves (ε,δ)-differential privacy under weaker assumptions in the interpolation regime, shows that privacy guarantees are preserved under inexact solves, derives utility guarantees in terms of empirical excess risk, provides theoretical and numerical comparisons to LOP showing advantages of curvature-based perturbations, and demonstrates that resulting problems can be solved efficiently using modern splitting schemes.
AI.conclusion: Quadratic Objective Perturbation provides a more general and effective differentially private mechanism for empirical risk minimization that works under weaker assumptions than traditional linear perturbation methods, making it applicable to a broader range of modern machine learning models.

title: Shallow Prefill, Deep Decoding: Efficient Long-Context Inference via Layer-Asymmetric KV Visibility
summary: Long-context inference in decoder-only language models is costly because long prompts are processed during Prefill, cached at every layer, and repeatedly attended to during autoregressive Decode. We introduce \emph{Shallow Prefill, dEEp Decode} (SPEED), a phase-asymmetric KV-visibility policy that materializes non-anchor prompt-token KV states only in lower layers while keeping Decode-phase tokens full-depth. Unlike previous approaches that make upper-layer prompt KV states cheaper to store or construct, SPEED removes prefill tokens from the upper-layer Decode visibility set altogether. With a minimal BoS anchor, this simple change preserves broad benchmark quality while reducing long-context cost. In a controlled Llama-3.1-8B instruction-tuning study, SPEED using only 75\% of layers for prefill tokens reaches 51.2 average score on OLMES-style benchmarks, compared with 51.4 for the full-depth baseline, while improving TTFT by 33\%, TPOT by 22\%, and reducing active KV memory by 25.0\% at 128K context. Layer-wise diagnostics suggest that this cutoff retains the main prompt-selection and representation-stabilization regions of the full-depth model. These results show that long-context prompt tokens need not always persist as full-depth KV-cache objects when Decode-phase tokens remain full-depth.

AI.tldr: SPEED is a novel KV-cache optimization method that processes long prompts only in lower layers during prefill, reducing computational cost and memory while maintaining quality.
AI.motivation: Long-context inference in decoder-only LLMs is expensive because long prompts are fully processed during prefill, cached at every layer, and repeatedly attended to during decode, leading to high computational cost and memory usage.
AI.method: SPEED (Shallow Prefill, dEEp Decode) uses a phase-asymmetric KV-visibility policy that materializes non-anchor prompt-token KV states only in lower layers while keeping decode-phase tokens full-depth. It removes prefill tokens from upper-layer decode visibility altogether, using only a minimal beginning-of-sequence (BoS) anchor.
AI.result: In Llama-3.1-8B instruction-tuning study, SPEED using only 75% of layers for prefill tokens achieved 51.2 average score on OLMES-style benchmarks vs 51.4 for full-depth baseline, while improving TTFT by 33%, TPOT by 22%, and reducing active KV memory by 25.0% at 128K context. Layer-wise diagnostics show this cutoff retains main prompt-selection and representation-stabilization regions.
AI.conclusion: Long-context prompt tokens don't need to persist as full-depth KV-cache objects when decode-phase tokens remain full-depth. SPEED offers significant efficiency gains with minimal quality loss by leveraging the insight that prompt processing can be shallower than generation.

title: From Drops to Grid: Noise-Aware Spatio-Temporal Neural Process for Rainfall Estimation
summary: High-resolution rainfall observations are crucial for weather forecasting, water management, and hazard mitigation. Traditional operational measurements are often biased and low-resolution, limiting their ability to capture local rainfall. Accurate high-resolution rainfall maps require integrating sparse surface observations, yet existing deep learning densification methods are hindered by rainfall's skewed, localized nature, noise, and limited spatio-temporal fusion. We present DropsToGrid, a Neural Process-based method that generates dense rainfall fields by fusing temporal sequences from noisy, irregularly distributed private weather stations with spatial context from radar. Leveraging multi-scale feature extraction, temporal attention, and multi-modal fusion, the model produces stochastic, continuous rainfall estimates and explicitly quantifies uncertainty. Evaluations on real-world datasets demonstrate that DropsToGrid outperforms both operational and deep learning baselines, generating accurate high-resolution rainfall maps with well-calibrated uncertainty, even when only few stations are available and in cross-regional scenarios.

AI.tldr: DropsToGrid: Neural Process-based method for generating high-resolution rainfall maps by fusing sparse station data with radar, handling noise and uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Traditional rainfall measurements are biased and low-resolution, limiting local rainfall capture. Existing deep learning methods struggle with rainfall's skewed, localized nature, noise, and limited spatio-temporal fusion.
AI.method: Neural Process-based approach that fuses temporal sequences from noisy, irregular weather stations with spatial context from radar. Uses multi-scale feature extraction, temporal attention, and multi-modal fusion to produce stochastic, continuous rainfall estimates with explicit uncertainty quantification.
AI.result: Outperforms both operational and deep learning baselines. Generates accurate high-resolution rainfall maps with well-calibrated uncertainty, even with few stations and in cross-regional scenarios.
AI.conclusion: DropsToGrid provides an effective solution for high-resolution rainfall mapping by addressing key challenges in spatio-temporal fusion, noise handling, and uncertainty quantification in rainfall data densification.

title: On Time, Within Budget: Constraint-Driven Online Resource Allocation for Agentic Workflows
summary: Agentic systems increasingly solve complex user requests by executing orchestrated workflows, where subtasks are assigned to specialized models or tools and coordinated according to their dependencies. While recent work improves agent efficiency by optimizing the performance--cost--latency frontier, real deployments often impose concrete requirements: a workflow must be completed within a specified budget and before a specified deadline. This shifts the goal from average efficiency optimization to maximizing the probability that the entire workflow completes successfully under explicit budget and deadline constraints. We study \emph{constraint-driven online resource allocation for agentic workflows}. Given a dependency-structured workflow and estimates of success rates and generation lengths for each subtask--model pair, the executor allocates models and parallel samples across simultaneously executable subtasks while managing the remaining budget and time. We formulate this setting as a finite-horizon stochastic online allocation problem and propose \emph{Monte Carlo Portfolio Planning} (MCPP), a lightweight closed-loop planner that directly estimates constrained completion probability through simulated workflow executions and replans after observed outcomes. Experiments on CodeFlow and ProofFlow demonstrate that MCPP consistently improves constrained completion probability over strong baselines across a wide range of budget--deadline constraints.

AI.tldr: MCPP is a Monte Carlo planning method that maximizes the probability of completing agentic workflows within given budget and deadline constraints by simulating executions and dynamically allocating resources.
AI.motivation: Real-world agentic system deployments often have concrete budget and deadline requirements, shifting the optimization goal from average efficiency to maximizing the probability of successful completion under explicit constraints.
AI.method: Monte Carlo Portfolio Planning (MCPP) - a lightweight closed-loop planner that formulates the problem as finite-horizon stochastic online allocation, estimates constrained completion probability through simulated workflow executions, and replans after observed outcomes.
AI.result: Experiments on CodeFlow and ProofFlow show that MCPP consistently improves constrained completion probability over strong baselines across a wide range of budget-deadline constraints.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MCPP approach effectively addresses the constraint-driven resource allocation problem for agentic workflows, providing practical improvements for real-world deployments with explicit budget and deadline requirements.

title: Near-Policy: Accelerating On-Policy Distillation via Asynchronous Generation and Selective Packing
summary: Standard knowledge distillation for autoregressive models often suffers from distribution mismatch. While on-policy methods mitigate this by leveraging student-generated outputs, they rely on computationally expensive Reinforcement Learning (RL) frameworks. To improve efficiency, we propose Near-Policy Distillation (NPD), an asynchronous approach that decouples student generation from training. This reformulation enables Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with sequence packing. However, asynchronous updates inevitably introduce policy lag and sample noise, which can cause the behavior to drift from near-policy toward off-policy. To counteract this without sacrificing efficiency, NPD integrates sparse student updates and the $Δ$-IFD filtering mechanism, a heuristic sample selection mechanism that empirically stabilizes the optimization trajectory. By filtering extreme out-of-distribution samples, $Δ$-IFD prevents noise from dominating the gradients, ensuring updates remain within a safe proximal learning zone. Empirically, the NPD framework achieves a 8.1x speedup over on-policy baselines and outperforms SFT by 8.09%. Crucially, by effectively narrowing the exploration space for subsequent RL, our method enables openPangu-Embedded-1B to reach a state-of-the-art score of 68.73%, outperforming the substantially larger Qwen3-1.7B. Codes will be released soon.

AI.tldr: NPD (Near-Policy Distillation) is an efficient asynchronous knowledge distillation method for autoregressive models that avoids computationally expensive RL frameworks while maintaining training stability through sparse updates and Δ-IFD filtering.
AI.motivation: Standard knowledge distillation suffers from distribution mismatch, while on-policy methods that mitigate this issue are computationally expensive due to their reliance on Reinforcement Learning frameworks. There's a need for more efficient distillation methods that maintain training stability.
AI.method: Proposes Near-Policy Distillation (NPD) - an asynchronous approach that decouples student generation from training, enabling Supervised Fine-Tuning with sequence packing. To address policy lag and sample noise from asynchronous updates, NPD integrates sparse student updates and the Δ-IFD filtering mechanism, which filters extreme out-of-distribution samples to keep updates within a safe proximal learning zone.
AI.result: Achieves 8.1x speedup over on-policy baselines and outperforms SFT by 8.09%. Enables openPangu-Embedded-1B to reach a state-of-the-art score of 68.73%, outperforming the larger Qwen3-1.7B model. By narrowing exploration space for subsequent RL, the method demonstrates superior efficiency and effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: NPD provides an efficient alternative to RL-based on-policy distillation methods, maintaining training stability through asynchronous updates with filtering mechanisms, and demonstrates significant performance improvements and speedups while enabling smaller models to outperform larger ones.

title: CrossCult-KIBench: A Benchmark for Cross-Cultural Knowledge Insertion in MLLMs
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), trained primarily on English-centric data, frequently generate culturally inappropriate or misaligned responses in cross-cultural settings. To mitigate this, we introduce the task of cross-cultural knowledge insertion, which focuses on adapting models to specific cultural contexts while preserving their original behavior in other cultures. To facilitate research in this area, we introduce CrossCult-KIBench, a comprehensive evaluation benchmark for assessing both the effectiveness of knowledge insertion and its unintended side effects on non-target cultures. The benchmark includes 9,800 image-grounded cases covering 49 culturally relevant visual scenarios across English, Chinese, and Arabic language-culture groups. It supports evaluation in both single-insert and sequential-insert settings. We also propose Memory-Conditioned Knowledge Insertion (MCKI) as a baseline method. MCKI retrieves relevant cultural knowledge from an external memory using frozen MLLM representations, prepending matched entries as conditional prompts when applicable. Extensive experiments on CrossCult-KIBench reveal that current approaches struggle to balance effective cultural adaptation with behavioral preservation, highlighting a key challenge in developing culturally-aware MLLMs. Our work thus underscores an important research direction for developing more culturally adaptive and responsible MLLMs.

AI.tldr: A method for making multimodal LLMs culturally aware by inserting cross-cultural knowledge while preserving behavior in other cultures, with a new benchmark and baseline approach.
AI.motivation: Multimodal LLMs trained on English-centric data often generate culturally inappropriate responses in cross-cultural settings, creating a need for culturally-aware adaptation.
AI.method: Introduce cross-cultural knowledge insertion task, propose MCKI (Memory-Conditioned Knowledge Insertion) baseline that retrieves cultural knowledge from external memory using frozen MLLM representations, and prepends matched entries as conditional prompts.
AI.result: Current approaches struggle to balance effective cultural adaptation with behavioral preservation in other cultures, highlighting a key challenge in developing culturally-aware MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: The work underscores the importance of developing more culturally adaptive and responsible MLLMs and provides a foundation for future research in this direction.

title: Knowing but Not Correcting: Routine Task Requests Suppress Factual Correction in LLMs
summary: LLMs reliably correct false claims when presented in isolation, yet when the same claims are embedded in task-oriented requests, they often comply rather than correct. We term this failure mode \emph{correction suppression} and construct a benchmark of 300 false premises to systematically evaluate it across eight models. Suppression rates range from 19\% to 90\%, with four models exceeding 80\%, establishing correction suppression as a prevalent and severe phenomenon. Mechanistic analysis reveals that suppression is not a knowledge failure: the model registers the error internally but task context diverts early-layer attention from the false claim as output intent crystallizes toward compliance at middle layers. We characterize this as \emph{knowing but not correcting} -- suppression occurs at response selection rather than knowledge encoding. Guided by this mechanism, we propose two training-free interventions. Correction Direction Steering (CDS) estimates a correction-compliance direction from matched pairs and injects it at middle layers before output intent crystallizes. Dynamic Payload Amplification (DPA) localizes payload tokens via attention divergence between early and late layers and amplifies their representation at the final layer, requiring no calibration data.
