Experiments on Qwen3.5-9B and LLaMA3.1-8B show both methods substantially improve factual strictness. CDS achieves the highest correction rate on Qwen3.5-9B (0\%$\to$58.2\%). DPA is the only method that preserves or improves reasoning capability on both models. These findings introduce \emph{factual strictness} -- the willingness to uphold accuracy against contextual pressures -- as a new dimension of model reliability.

AI.tldr: LLMs often comply with false claims in task-oriented requests instead of correcting them, a phenomenon called "correction suppression." The paper introduces interventions that improve factual strictness without harming reasoning.
AI.motivation: LLMs reliably correct false claims when presented in isolation, but they often comply rather than correct when the same claims are embedded in task-oriented requests. This failure mode is called "correction suppression," but its prevalence and mechanisms are not well understood.
AI.method: Created a benchmark of 300 false premises to systematically evaluate correction suppression across eight models. Performed mechanistic analysis of attention patterns and developed two training-free interventions: Correction Direction Steering (CDS) which injects correction signals at middle layers, and Dynamic Payload Amplification (DPA) which amplifies payload tokens at the final layer using attention divergence analysis.
AI.result: Suppression rates ranged from 19% to 90%, with four models exceeding 80%, establishing correction suppression as prevalent. CDS improved correction rate on Qwen3.5-9B from 0% to 58.2%. DPA preserved or improved reasoning capability on both tested models. The mechanistic analysis showed suppression occurs at response selection rather than knowledge encoding.
AI.conclusion: The paper introduces "factual strictness" - the willingness to uphold accuracy against contextual pressures - as a new dimension of model reliability. The findings reveal LLMs often "know but don't correct" false claims in task contexts, and demonstrate effective interventions to improve this capability.

title: Policy-Guided Stepwise Model Routing for Cost-Effective Reasoning
summary: Inference-time computation has greatly enhanced the performance of large language models (LLMs) on challenging reasoning tasks, but this strategy can incur high inference costs. One solution is to route intermediate chain-of-thought (CoT) states to language models of different sizes; however, existing approaches rely on handcrafted routing strategies that limit performance, or on training large process reward models that may be infeasible in many applications. We formulate stepwise model routing as a constrained decision-making problem, which we solve by training a small control policy using reinforcement learning in conjunction with threshold calibration to tune the performance-efficiency tradeoff. We validate our method on three math benchmarks (GSM8K, MATH500, and OmniMath) on both open and closed models. Our method consistently improves the accuracy-cost tradeoff compared to handcrafted approaches, while achieving a comparable tradeoff to methods that require training large process reward models.

AI.tldr: Small RL-trained control policy for stepwise model routing improves accuracy-cost tradeoff in LLM reasoning tasks without needing large process reward models.
AI.motivation: Inference-time computation like chain-of-thought improves LLM reasoning but incurs high costs. Existing routing solutions are limited - handcrafted strategies underperform, while process reward models require large training that may be infeasible.
AI.method: Formulate stepwise model routing as constrained decision-making problem, train small control policy using reinforcement learning, and apply threshold calibration to tune performance-efficiency tradeoff.
AI.result: Validated on three math benchmarks (GSM8K, MATH500, OmniMath) with open and closed models. Method consistently improves accuracy-cost tradeoff over handcrafted approaches, and achieves comparable tradeoff to methods requiring large process reward models.
AI.conclusion: RL-based control policy provides an effective solution for efficient model routing in reasoning tasks, balancing performance and cost without requiring extensive training resources.

title: Uncertainty Estimation via Hyperspherical Confidence Mapping
comment: Accepted at ICLR 2026. 24 pages, 7 figures, including appendix
summary: Quantifying uncertainty in neural network predictions is essential for high-stakes domains such as autonomous driving, healthcare, and manufacturing. While existing approaches often depend on costly sampling or restrictive distributional assumptions, we propose Hyperspherical Confidence Mapping (HCM), a simple yet principled framework for sampling-free and distribution-free uncertainty estimation. HCM decomposes outputs into a magnitude and a normalized direction vector constrained to lie on the unit hypersphere, enabling a novel interpretation of uncertainty as the degree of violation of this geometric constraint. This yields deterministic and interpretable estimates applicable to both regression and classification. Experiments across diverse benchmarks and real-world industrial tasks demonstrate that HCM matches or surpasses ensemble and evidential approaches, with far lower inference cost and stronger confidence-error alignment. Our results highlight the power of geometric structure in uncertainty estimation and position HCM as a versatile alternative to conventional techniques.

AI.tldr: HCM (Hyperspherical Confidence Mapping) is a sampling-free, distribution-free uncertainty estimation method that uses geometric constraints on a hypersphere to get deterministic, interpretable uncertainty estimates for neural networks.
AI.motivation: Existing uncertainty estimation approaches often require costly sampling or make restrictive distributional assumptions, which limits their practical application in high-stakes domains like autonomous driving and healthcare where efficient and reliable uncertainty quantification is crucial.
AI.method: HCM decomposes neural network outputs into magnitude and normalized direction vectors constrained to lie on a unit hypersphere. It interprets uncertainty as the degree of violation of this geometric constraint, providing a deterministic, sampling-free approach without distributional assumptions.
AI.result: Experiments across diverse benchmarks and real-world industrial tasks show HCM matches or surpasses ensemble and evidential approaches, with significantly lower inference cost and stronger confidence-error alignment.
AI.conclusion: HCM demonstrates the power of geometric structure in uncertainty estimation and serves as a versatile, efficient alternative to conventional uncertainty estimation techniques for neural networks.

title: Back to the Beginning of Heuristic Design: Bridging Code and Knowledge with LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have recently advanced automatic heuristic design (AHD) for combinatorial optimization (CO), where candidate heuristics are iteratively proposed, evaluated, and refined. Most existing approaches search over executable programs and distill insights from execution feedback to guide later iterations. Because this process moves from low-level implementations to high-level principles, we refer to it as a bottom-up paradigm. We argue that this view is incomplete and introduce a complementary top-down perspective: knowledge becomes the primary search object and code merely instantiates and tests it, making what is learned explicit and reusable across problems and trajectories. We formalize this shift through a statistical-learning view that exposes a distortion--compression trade-off, and instantiate it in both population-based and tree-based AHD frameworks. Across CO and tasks beyond it, knowledge-first search improves discovery efficiency, transfer, and generalization, often outperforming code-centric pipelines, while combining both strategies yields further gains. Our results suggest that progress in AHD depends on iteratively constructing and evolving interpretable hypotheses that retain value beyond a single search trajectory.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a top-down "knowledge-first" paradigm for automatic heuristic design with LLMs, treating knowledge as the primary search object rather than code, improving efficiency and generalization across combinatorial optimization tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing bottom-up approaches in automatic heuristic design (AHD) search over executable programs and distill insights from execution feedback, moving from low-level implementations to high-level principles. The authors argue this view is incomplete and propose a complementary top-down perspective where knowledge becomes the primary search object and code merely instantiates and tests it, making what is learned explicit and reusable.
AI.method: The authors formalize the shift through a statistical-learning view that exposes a distortion-compression trade-off, and instantiate it in both population-based and tree-based AHD frameworks. They treat knowledge as the primary search object rather than code, allowing for explicit knowledge representation and reuse across problems and search trajectories.
AI.result: Across combinatorial optimization and tasks beyond it, knowledge-first search improves discovery efficiency, transfer, and generalization, often outperforming code-centric pipelines. Combining both bottom-up and top-down strategies yields further gains.
AI.conclusion: Progress in automatic heuristic design depends on iteratively constructing and evolving interpretable hypotheses that retain value beyond a single search trajectory. The knowledge-first approach makes learned principles explicit and reusable, offering complementary benefits to traditional code-centric methods.

title: Beyond Uniform Credit Assignment: Selective Eligibility Traces for RLVR
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become a key approach for improving the reasoning abilities of large language models. However, widely used critic-free algorithms such as Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) necessitate a ``uniform credit assignment'' assumption that indiscriminately broadcast trajectory-level advantages, hindering learning efficiency by failing to distinguish critical reasoning steps. To address this limitation, we propose Selective Eligibility Traces (S-trace). Grounded in the intuition of partial trust region preservation, we initially introduce P-trace as a sample-efficient, critic-free eligibility traces method, upon which we build S-trace, implementing a sparse eligibility traces mechanism to further mitigate variance and achieve fine-grained credit assignment by selectively masking low-entropy tokens. Theoretically, we contextualize the recent Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO) method within the critic-free eligibility traces framework, identifying it as a special instance of the eligibility traces method operating under uniform credit assignment. Experiments demonstrate that S-trace not only outperforms GRPO, showing gains of 0.49\% on Qwen3-1.7B and 3.16\% on Qwen3-4B, and maintaining a robust 2.98\% improvement when scaled further to Qwen3-8B in average pass@16, but notably achieves this with simultaneously higher sample and token efficiency.

AI.tldr: S-trace is a novel critic-free eligibility traces method that improves reasoning in LLMs by enabling selective credit assignment to critical tokens, outperforming GRPO with better efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current critic-free RLVR algorithms like GRPO suffer from uniform credit assignment that broadcasts trajectory-level advantages indiscriminately, failing to distinguish critical reasoning steps and hindering learning efficiency.
AI.method: Proposes Selective Eligibility Traces (S-trace) built on P-trace (partial trust region preservation). S-trace implements sparse eligibility traces to mitigate variance and achieve fine-grained credit assignment by selectively masking low-entropy tokens, enabling better identification of critical reasoning steps.
AI.result: S-trace outperforms GRPO with gains of 0.49% on Qwen3-1.7B, 3.16% on Qwen3-4B, and maintains 2.98% improvement on Qwen3-8B in average pass@16, while achieving higher sample and token efficiency simultaneously.
AI.conclusion: S-trace effectively addresses the limitations of uniform credit assignment in critic-free RLVR, demonstrating superior performance and efficiency through selective eligibility traces that better capture critical reasoning components in LLMs.

title: P-Guide: Parameter-Efficient Prior Steering for Single-Pass CFG Inference
summary: Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) is essential for high-fidelity conditional generation in flow matching, yet it imposes significant computational overhead by requiring dual forward passes at each sampling step. In this work, we address this bottleneck by introducing \textbf{P-Guide}, a framework that achieves high-quality guidance through a single inference pass by modulating only the initial latent state. We further show that, under a first-order approximation, P-Guide is equivalent to CFG in the sense that it steers generation from the prior space, without requiring explicit velocity field extrapolation during sampling. We consider both homoscedastic and \textbf{heteroscedastic} priors, and find that jointly modeling the mean and variance enables adaptive loss attenuation and improved robustness to data uncertainty. Extensive experiments demonstrate that P-Guide reduces inference latency by approximately 50\% while maintaining fidelity and prompt alignment competitive with standard dual-pass CFG baselines.

AI.tldr: P-Guide enables efficient classifier-free guidance for flow matching by modulating only the initial latent state with a single inference pass, achieving ~50% latency reduction while maintaining competitive quality.
AI.motivation: Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) imposes significant computational overhead by requiring dual forward passes at each sampling step for high-fidelity conditional generation in flow matching.
AI.method: P-Guide modulates only the initial latent state to steer generation from the prior space, eliminating explicit velocity field extrapolation during sampling. It considers both homoscedastic and heteroscedastic priors, with joint modeling of mean and variance for adaptive loss attenuation.
AI.result: P-Guide reduces inference latency by approximately 50% while maintaining fidelity and prompt alignment competitive with standard dual-pass CFG baselines.
AI.conclusion: P-Guide provides an efficient alternative to CFG for flow matching that maintains quality while significantly reducing computational overhead through single-pass inference and initial latent state modulation.

title: Sharper Guarantees for Misspecified Kernelized Bandit Optimization
summary:
Existing guarantees for misspecified kernelized bandit optimization pay for misspecification through kernel complexity: in generic offline bounds, the misspecification level $\varepsilon$ is multiplied by $\sqrt{d_\mathrm{eff}}$, where $d_\mathrm{eff}$ is the kernel effective dimension, while in online regret bounds, the corresponding penalty is $\sqrt{γ_n}\,n\varepsilon$, where $γ_n$ is the maximum information gain after $n$ rounds of interaction.
  In this work, we show that, for a large class of kernels, the misspecification amplification can be reduced to logarithmic or polylogarithmic growth. In the offline setting, we first prove high-probability simple-regret bounds whose misspecification term is governed by a spectral Lebesgue constant. This yields logarithmic amplification for one-dimensional monotone spectra and polylogarithmic amplification for multivariate Fourier-diagonal product kernels. In the online setting, we modify a domain-splitting algorithm and prove a cumulative regret bound of $\widetilde{\mathcal O}(\sqrt{γ_n n}+n\varepsilon)$ under mild localized eigendecay assumptions, removing the extra $\sqrt{γ_n}$ factor from the misspecification term. The common principle is localization: spectral localization controls the Lebesgue constant of the offline approximation operator, while domain splitting implements the spatial analogue of this mechanism in the online setting, preventing local misspecification errors from being amplified globally.

AI.tldr: This paper reduces misspecification penalties in kernelized bandit optimization from polynomial to logarithmic/polylogarithmic growth for certain kernels by leveraging localization principles.
AI.motivation: Existing kernelized bandit optimization approaches suffer from significant penalties when models are misspecified (i.e., when the true function doesn't lie in the reproducing kernel Hilbert space). Current methods multiply misspecification error by kernel complexity measures like effective dimension or maximum information gain, leading to large performance degradation.
AI.method: The authors introduce localization principles to control misspecification amplification. In offline settings, they use spectral Lebesgue constants to bound simple regret. In online settings, they modify a domain-splitting algorithm to prevent local misspecification errors from propagating globally.
AI.result: For offline optimization, they achieve logarithmic amplification for one-dimensional monotone spectra and polylogarithmic amplification for multivariate Fourier-diagonal product kernels. For online optimization, they prove cumulative regret bounds of $\widetilde{\mathcal O}(\sqrt{γ_n n}+n\varepsilon)$, removing the extra $\sqrt{γ_n}$ factor from misspecification term compared to prior work.
AI.conclusion: Localization techniques significantly improve misspecification tolerance in kernelized bandits by controlling error amplification, transforming polynomial growth penalties to logarithmic/polylogarithmic ones for important kernel classes, making these methods more robust to model mismatch.

title: Skill1: Unified Evolution of Skill-Augmented Agents via Reinforcement Learning
summary: A persistent skill library allows language model agents to reuse successful strategies across tasks. Maintaining such a library requires three coupled capabilities. The agent selects a relevant skill, utilizes it during execution, and distills new skills from experience. Existing methods optimize these capabilities in isolation or with separate reward sources, resulting in partial and conflicting evolution. We propose Skill1, a framework that trains a single policy to co-evolve skill selection, utilization, and distillation toward a shared task-outcome objective. The policy generates a query to search the skill library, re-ranks candidates to select one, solves the task conditioned on it, and distills a new skill from the trajectory. All learning derives from a single task-outcome signal. Its low-frequency trend credits selection and its high-frequency variation credits distillation. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop show that Skill1 outperforms prior skill-based and reinforcement learning baselines. Training dynamics confirm the co-evolution of the three capabilities, and ablations show that removing any credit signal degrades the evolution.

AI.tldr: Skill1 is a framework that trains a single policy to co-evolve skill selection, utilization, and distillation using a shared task-outcome objective, outperforming prior methods on ALFWorld and WebShop benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for maintaining persistent skill libraries optimize skill selection, utilization, and distillation in isolation or with separate reward sources, leading to partial and conflicting evolution rather than coordinated improvement.
AI.method: Skill1 trains a single policy that performs four key operations: generates a query to search the skill library, re-ranks candidates to select a skill, solves tasks conditioned on the selected skill, and distills new skills from the trajectory. All learning derives from a single task-outcome signal, with low-frequency trends crediting selection and high-frequency variation crediting distillation.
AI.result: Skill1 outperforms prior skill-based and reinforcement learning baselines on ALFWorld and WebShop benchmarks. Training dynamics confirm the co-evolution of the three capabilities, and ablation studies show that removing any credit signal degrades evolution.
AI.conclusion: The Skill1 framework successfully demonstrates that coordinated co-evolution of skill selection, utilization, and distillation through a unified learning approach leads to superior performance compared to isolated optimization methods, with the integrated credit assignment mechanism being essential for effective skill library maintenance.

title: Training Transformers for KV Cache Compressibility
summary: Long-context language modeling is increasingly constrained by the Key-Value (KV) cache, whose memory and decode-time access costs scale linearly with the prefix length. This bottleneck has motivated a range of context-compression methods, from token-level summarization to recent optimization-based KV compression methods. These post-hoc methods operate on the KV cache of a fixed pretrained model, so their effectiveness is fundamentally limited by how well the model's internal representations can be compressed. In this work, we formalize the notion of KV compressibility and show that it is a property of the learned representations, rather than of the context alone. We prove that almost any sequence-to-vector function admits both highly compressible and inherently non-compressible transformer implementations, highlighting the need to guide transformers toward compressible representations during training. Motivated by this, we propose KV-Compression Aware Training (KV-CAT), a continued pretraining procedure that incentivizes the emergence of compressible representations. We introduce a train-time KV sparsification policy that masks KV slots during training. This forces the model to use fewer KV slots and encourages it to learn representations amenable to post-hoc compression. Empirically, we show that KV-CAT improves the quality-budget tradeoff of downstream compression methods across retrieval, long-context question answering, and perplexity-based evaluation of compressed-prefix continuation.

AI.tldr: KV-CAT: A training method that makes language models inherently more compressible by teaching them to use KV cache more efficiently during continued pretraining.
AI.motivation: Long-context language models suffer from KV cache bottlenecks - memory and decode-time access costs scale linearly with context length. Current post-hoc compression methods are limited by the inherent compressibility of the model's learned representations.
AI.method: KV-CAT: A continued pretraining procedure that uses train-time KV sparsification to mask KV slots during training, forcing the model to use fewer slots and learn more compressible representations.
AI.result: KV-CAT improves the quality-budget tradeoff of downstream compression methods across retrieval, long-context QA, and perplexity-based evaluation of compressed-prefix continuation.
AI.conclusion: KV compressibility is a property of learned representations, not just the context. Training transformers to be inherently more compressible (via KV-CAT) enables better practical compression for long-context modeling.

title: Graphlets as Building Blocks for Structural Vocabulary in Knowledge Graph Foundation Models
summary: Foundation models excel at language, where sentences become tokens, and vision, where images become pixels, because both reduce to discrete symbols on a shared, fixed grid. Knowledge Graphs share the discreteness, but not the geometry. Their entities and relations are discrete symbols, yet their arrangement is relational and lacks a common, fixed grid. Knowledge Graphs (KGs) share the discreteness, but not the geometry. They form irregular, non-Euclidean topologies whose local neighborhoods differ from graph to graph. Therefore, Knowledge Graph Foundation Models (KGFMs) rely on identifying structural invariances to produce transferable representations. Without a universal token set, KGFMs are limited in their ability to transfer representations across unseen KGs. We close this gap by treating graphlets, small connected graphs, as structural tokens that recur in heterogeneous KGs. In this paper, We introduce a model-agnostic framework based on a vocabulary of graphlets that mines a KG between relations via pattern matching. In particular, we considered closed and open 2- and 3-path, and star graphlets, to obtain robust invariances. The framework is evaluated on 51 KGs from a wide range of domains, for zero-shot inductive and transductive link prediction. Experiments show that adding simple graphlets to the vocabulary yields models that outperform prior KGFMs.

AI.tldr: A framework for Knowledge Graph Foundation Models that uses graphlets as structural tokens to achieve transferable representations across heterogeneous knowledge graphs, enabling better zero-shot link prediction.
AI.motivation: Knowledge Graphs lack the geometric regularity of language and vision tasks - while they have discrete symbols (entities/relations), they lack a common fixed grid and form irregular, non-Euclidean topologies. This makes transfer learning difficult for KGFMs since they can't rely on universal tokens like in NLP/vision.
AI.method: Treat graphlets (small connected graphs) as structural tokens that recur across KGs. Develop a model-agnostic framework with a vocabulary of graphlets (closed/open 2-/3-paths and star graphlets) mined via pattern matching between relations. This captures robust structural invariances.
AI.result: Evaluation on 51 KGs across various domains shows that adding simple graphlets to vocabulary yields models outperforming prior KGFMs in zero-shot inductive and transductive link prediction tasks.
AI.conclusion: Graphlets serve as effective structural tokens for KGFMs, bridging the gap caused by KG heterogeneity and enabling better transfer learning across diverse knowledge graphs without requiring a universal token set.

title: Physical Fidelity Reconstruction via Improved Consistency-Distilled Flow Matching for Dynamical Systems
summary: Reconstructing high-fidelity flow fields from low-fidelity observations is a central problem in scientific machine learning, yet recent diffusion and flow-matching models typically rely on iterative sampling, making them costly for latency-sensitive workflows such as ensemble forecasting, real-time visualization, and simulation-in-the-loop inference. We study whether a high-fidelity flow-matching generative model can be compressed into a compact one-step model for fast scientific flow reconstruction. Our approach distills an optimal-transport flow-matching teacher into a one-step consistency model. Low-fidelity observations are incorporated at inference by initializing the generative trajectory from a noised observation along the transport path, allowing an unconditional high-fidelity flow model to perform conditional reconstruction without retraining the teacher. We evaluate this distillation strategy on three fluid benchmarks, Smoke Buoyancy, Turbulent Channel Flow, and Kolmogorov Flow, using coarse-to-fine reconstruction as a controlled testbed at field sizes up to $256 \times 256$. Across these settings, the distilled student retains similar performance of the teacher's model on spectrum metrics, while using roughly half as many parameters and achieving a $12\times$ inference speedup over the flow-matching teacher. Under the same training budget, the distilled student also outperforms a one-step consistency model trained directly from scratch by $23.1\%$ in SSIM, showing that teacher distillation improves training efficiency rather than merely accelerating sampling. These results suggest a promising route for turning future high-capacity scientific generative models into compact reconstruction models that are faster to train, cheaper to run, and easier to deploy.

AI.tldr: One-step consistency model distillation from flow-matching teachers enables fast, efficient scientific flow reconstruction with 12× speedup and 23.1% better SSIM than scratch training.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion and flow-matching models for scientific flow reconstruction rely on iterative sampling, making them too slow for latency-sensitive applications like ensemble forecasting, real-time visualization, and simulation-in-the-loop inference. There's a need for faster, more compact models without sacrificing reconstruction quality.
AI.method: Distills an optimal-transport flow-matching teacher model into a compact one-step consistency model. Low-fidelity observations are incorporated at inference by initializing the generative trajectory from a noised observation along the transport path, enabling conditional reconstruction without retraining the teacher.
AI.result: The distilled student achieves 12× inference speedup while retaining similar spectral performance to the teacher, using half the parameters. Under same training budget, it outperforms one-step consistency models trained from scratch by 23.1% in SSIM across three fluid benchmarks (Smoke Buoyancy, Turbulent Channel Flow, Kolmogorov Flow) up to 256×256 resolution.
AI.conclusion: Teacher distillation provides a promising path for converting high-capacity scientific generative models into compact, fast, deployable reconstruction models that improve both training efficiency and inference speed, rather than just accelerating sampling.

title: Beyond Accuracy: Policy Invariance as a Reliability Test for LLM Safety Judges
summary: LLM-as-a-Judge pipelines have become the de facto evaluator for agent safety, yet existing benchmarks treat their verdicts as ground-truth proxies without checking whether the verdicts depend on the agent's behavior or merely on how the evaluation policy happens to be worded. We argue that any trustworthy safety judge must satisfy a basic property we call policy invariance, and we operationalize it as three testable principles: rubric-semantics invariance under certified-equivalent rewrites, rubric-threshold invariance under intentional strict-to-lenient shifts, and ambiguity-aware calibration so that verdict instability concentrates on genuinely ambiguous cases. Instantiating these principles as a stress-test protocol with four agent-class judges on trajectories drawn from ASSEBench and R-Judge, we surface a previously unmeasured failure mode: today's judges respond to meaningful normative shifts and to meaningless structural rewrites with comparable strength, and cannot tell the two apart. Content-preserving policy rewrites flip up to 9.1% of verdicts above baseline jitter, and 18-43% of all observed flips occur on unambiguous cases under such rewrites, so existing safety scores conflate what the agent did with how the evaluator was prompted. Beyond the diagnosis, we contribute the Policy Invariance Score and the Judge Card reporting protocol, which expose an order-of-magnitude spread in judge reliability that is invisible to accuracy-only leaderboards. We release the protocol and code so that future agent-safety benchmarks can audit their own evaluators rather than trust them by default.

AI.tldr: Current LLM-as-a-Judge safety evaluation systems lack robustness to policy wording changes, with up to 9.1% verdict flips from content-preserving rewrites. The paper introduces policy invariance principles and testing protocol to measure judge reliability.
AI.motivation: Existing agent safety benchmarks treat LLM judge verdicts as ground truth without verifying whether verdicts depend on actual agent behavior or just on how evaluation policies are worded. There's a need to ensure trustworthy safety judges that are invariant to policy wording changes.
AI.method: The authors operationalize policy invariance as three testable principles: rubric-semantics invariance under certified-equivalent rewrites, rubric-threshold invariance under strict-to-lenient shifts, and ambiguity-aware calibration. They instantiate these principles as a stress-test protocol tested on four agent-class judges using trajectories from ASSEBench and R-Judge.
AI.result: Current judges respond to meaningful normative shifts and meaningless structural rewrites with comparable strength, unable to distinguish between them. Content-preserving policy rewrites flip up to 9.1% of verdicts above baseline jitter, with 18-43% of flips occurring on unambiguous cases. The study reveals an order-of-magnitude spread in judge reliability invisible to accuracy-only metrics.
AI.conclusion: Existing safety scores conflate agent behavior with evaluator prompting. The paper contributes the Policy Invariance Score and Judge Card reporting protocol to audit evaluator reliability. Future benchmarks should audit their evaluators rather than trusting them by default.

title: Towards Steering without Sacrifice: Principled Training of Steering Vectors for Prompt-only Interventions
summary: Recently, steering vectors (SVs) have emerged as an effective and lightweight approach to steer behaviors of large language models (LLMs), among which fine-tuned SVs are more effective than optimization-free ones. However, current approaches to fine-tuned SVs suffer from two limitations. First, they require careful selection of steering factors on a per-SV basis to balance steering effectiveness and generation quality at inference time. Second, they operate as full-sequence SVs (FSSVs), which can sacrifice generation quality regardless of factor selection due to excessive intervention on the model generation process. To address the first limitation, we propose joint training of steering factors and directions, such that post-hoc factor selection is no longer required. Using neural network scaling theory, we find that moderately large initialization sizes and learning rates for steering factors are essential for stability and efficiency of joint training. To tackle the second limitation, we draw inspiration from representation fine-tuning and introduce Prompt-only SV (PrOSV), an SV that intervenes only on a few prompt tokens. Our empirical results show that PrOSV outperforms traditional FSSVs on AxBench when using our joint training scheme. We also find that PrOSV achieves a better tradeoff between general model utility and adversarial robustness than FSSV.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Prompt-only Steering Vectors (PrOSV) with joint training of steering factors and directions, which only intervene on a few prompt tokens instead of the full sequence, achieving better steering effectiveness without compromising generation quality.
AI.motivation: Current steering vector approaches have two main limitations: (1) they require careful per-SV selection of steering factors to balance effectiveness and quality, and (2) full-sequence SVs sacrifice generation quality due to excessive intervention throughout the generation process.
AI.method: Proposes joint training of steering factors and directions to eliminate post-hoc factor selection, with insights from neural network scaling theory about initialization sizes and learning rates. Introduces Prompt-only SV (PrOSV) that intervenes only on a few prompt tokens instead of the full sequence.
AI.result: PrOSV outperforms traditional full-sequence SVs on AxBench when using the joint training scheme. It achieves a better tradeoff between general model utility and adversarial robustness compared to full-sequence SVs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PrOSV with joint training addresses both limitations of current steering vector approaches by eliminating the need for factor selection and reducing excessive intervention, resulting in improved steering effectiveness without sacrificing generation quality.

title: Post Reasoning: Improving the Performance of Non-Thinking Models at No Cost
summary: As the widespread adoption of Large Language Models (LLMs) accelerates, token consumption from intermediate reasoning traces increasingly contributes to inference latency and operational cost. Recent studies suggest that many real-world tasks require little to no explicit reasoning, with additional reasoning sometimes even degrading performance. In this work, we propose \textbf{Post-Reasoning}, a simple yet effective approach that improves instruction-tuned models by conditioning them to justify their answers after generating the final response. By design, it enables the final answer to be obtained without additional latency or token cost, while still improving performance through simple instruction augmentation. We evaluate Post-Reasoning across \(117\) model--benchmark settings spanning \(13\) open and proprietary models, \(4\) model families, and \(9\) diverse reasoning and knowledge-intensive benchmarks, including AMC, HMMT, GSM8K, GPQA, MMLU-Pro, and BIG-Bench Hard. Post-Reasoning improves performance in over \(88.19\%\) of evaluated settings, achieving a mean relative improvements of \(17.37\%\). Furthermore, we propose supervised post-reason tuning, which further improves performance in over \(91.11\%\) of evaluated settings, and exceeds the prompt-based post-reasoning baseline by an average of \(8.01\%\), demonstrating that post-reasoning can be effectively internalized through training. Ultimately, Post-Reasoning establishes a new performance ceiling for direct-answer capabilities.

AI.tldr: Post-Reasoning improves LLM performance by generating answers first and reasoning afterward, eliminating extra latency and cost while enhancing accuracy through simple instruction augmentation.
AI.motivation: Current LLMs generate intermediate reasoning traces before answers, which increases inference latency and operational costs. Many real-world tasks don't need explicit reasoning, and sometimes additional reasoning even degrades performance. There's a need to reduce token consumption while maintaining or improving performance.
AI.method: Post-Reasoning conditions instruction-tuned models to justify their answers AFTER generating the final response. This allows answers to be obtained without additional latency or token cost. The approach includes simple instruction augmentation and also proposes supervised post-reason tuning to internalize this behavior through training.
AI.result: Evaluated across 117 model-benchmark settings (13 models, 4 families, 9 benchmarks). Post-Reasoning improved performance in 88.19% of settings with 17.37% mean relative improvement. Supervised post-reason tuning improved 91.11% of settings, exceeding prompt-based baseline by 8.01% average improvement.
AI.conclusion: Post-Reasoning establishes a new performance ceiling for direct-answer capabilities by decoupling reasoning from answer generation, reducing latency and costs while improving performance. It's a simple yet effective approach that can be implemented through both prompting and supervised tuning.

title: DiBA: Diagonal and Binary Matrix Approximation for Neural Network Weight Compression
summary: In this paper, we propose DiBA (Diagonal and Binary Matrix Approximation), a compact matrix factorization for neural network weight compression. Many components of modern networks, including linear layers, $1\times1$ convolutions, attention projections, and embedding layers, have dense matrix weights. DiBA approximates $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ by $\widehat A=D_1B_1D_2B_2D_3$, where $D_1,D_2,D_3$ are diagonal matrices and $B_1,B_2$ are $0/1$ binary matrices. The intermediate dimension $k$ controls the trade-off between theoretical storage and approximation accuracy. For matrix-vector products, DiBA decomposes dense multiplication into three element-wise scaling operations and two binary mixing operations, reducing the floating-point multiplication count from $mn$ to $m+k+n$. For optimization, we introduce DiBA-Greedy, an alternating solver that combines closed-form least-squares updates for the diagonal factors with exact one-bit improvement tests for the binary factors. We also introduce DiBARD (DiBA with Retuning only Diagonal factors), which replaces dense-matrix layers by DiBA factors, freezes the binary matrices, and retunes only the diagonal entries on downstream data. This preserves compact binary mixing without discrete search during adaptation. On 40 dense weight matrices extracted from public pretrained models, DiBA-Greedy yields consistent SNR improvements as the theoretical storage ratio increases. After DiBA replacement in two component-replacement studies, DiBARD improves DistilBERT/WikiText masked-token accuracy from 0.4447 to 0.5210 and Speech Commands test accuracy for an Audio Spectrogram Transformer from 0.7684 to 0.9781 without reoptimizing the binary factors.

AI.tldr: DiBA proposes a compact matrix factorization using diagonal and binary matrices for neural network weight compression, reducing multiplication operations while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Many modern neural network components (linear layers, convolutions, attention projections, embedding layers) have dense matrix weights that require significant computational resources and storage. There's a need for efficient compression techniques that reduce both storage and computation while maintaining performance.
AI.method: DiBA approximates dense matrices A as D₁B₁D₂B₂D₃, where Dᵢ are diagonal matrices and Bᵢ are 0/1 binary matrices. The method includes DiBA-Greedy optimization algorithm with alternating solver using closed-form least-squares for diagonals and exact one-bit improvement tests for binary factors, plus DiBARD for adaptation that freezes binary matrices and retunes only diagonal entries on downstream data.
AI.result: DiBA reduces floating-point multiplication count from mn to m+k+n for matrix-vector products. On 40 dense weight matrices from pretrained models, DiBA-Greedy shows consistent SNR improvements with increasing storage ratio. DiBARD improves DistilBERT/WikiText masked-token accuracy from 0.4447 to 0.5210 and Speech Commands test accuracy for Audio Spectrogram Transformer from 0.7684 to 0.9781 without reoptimizing binary factors.
AI.conclusion: DiBA provides an effective weight compression technique that decomposes dense matrix multiplication into more efficient operations while maintaining accuracy, with DiBARD offering practical adaptation benefits by only retuning diagonal factors on downstream tasks.

title: BioMedArena: An Open-source Toolkit for Building and Evaluating Biomedical Deep Research Agents
summary: Building a deep research agent today is an exercise in glue code: the same backbone evaluated on the same benchmark can report different accuracies in different papers because harness and tool registry all differ, and integrating a new foundation model into a comparable evaluation surface costs weeks of model-specific engineering. We call this the per-paper engineering tax and release BioMedArena, an open-source toolkit that not only alleviates it but also provides an arena for fair comparison of different foundation models when evaluating them as deep-research agents. BioMedArena decouples six layers of biomedical agent evaluation -- benchmark loading, tool exposure, tool selection, execution mode, context management, and scoring -- and exposes 147 biomedical benchmarks and 75 biomedical tools across 9 functional families. Adding a new model, benchmark, or tool reduces to registering a few-line provider adapter. We further provide 6 agent harnesses with 6 context-management strategies, which provide 12 backbones with competitive research capabilities and significantly improved performance, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) results on 8 representative biomedical benchmarks, with an average lift of +15.03 percentage points over prior SOTA. The toolkit, configurations, and per-task traces are available at https://github.com/AI-in-Health/BioMedArena

AI.tldr: BioMedArena is an open-source toolkit that standardizes evaluation of biomedical deep research agents, eliminating per-paper engineering inconsistencies and enabling fair model comparisons with state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Current deep research agent evaluation suffers from inconsistent "glue code" implementations where the same backbone model reports different accuracies in different papers due to varying tool registries, harnesses, and evaluation surfaces. This creates a "per-paper engineering tax" where integrating new models requires weeks of model-specific engineering work.
AI.method: BioMedArena decouples six layers of biomedical agent evaluation: benchmark loading, tool exposure, tool selection, execution mode, context management, and scoring. It exposes 147 biomedical benchmarks and 75 tools across 9 functional families. The system uses provider adapters that allow adding new models, benchmarks, or tools with just a few lines of code, plus 6 agent harnesses with 6 context-management strategies.
AI.result: The toolkit enables 12 backbones to achieve competitive research capabilities with significantly improved performance, obtaining state-of-the-art results on 8 representative biomedical benchmarks with an average improvement of +15.03 percentage points over previous SOTA methods.
AI.conclusion: BioMedArena provides a standardized framework that eliminates evaluation inconsistencies in biomedical agent research, enables fair comparisons across models, and dramatically reduces integration overhead while delivering superior performance on key benchmarks.

title: A Fine-Grained Understanding of Uniform Convergence for Halfspaces
summary: We study the fine-grained uniform convergence behavior of halfspaces beyond worst-case VC bounds. For inhomogeneous halfspaces in $\mathbb{R}^d$ with $d\ge 2$, we show that standard first-order VC bounds are essentially tight: even consistent hypotheses can incur population error $Θ(d\ln(n/d)/n)$, and in the agnostic setting the deviation scales as $\sqrt{τ\ln(1/τ)}$ at true error $τ$. In contrast, homogeneous halfspaces in $\mathbb{R}^2$ exhibit a markedly different behavior. In the realizable case, every hypothesis consistent with the sample has error $O(1/n)$. In the agnostic case, we prove a bandwise, log-free deviation bound on each dyadic risk band via a critical-wedge localization argument. Unioning over bands incurs only a $\ln\ln n$ overhead, and we establish a matching lower bound showing this overhead is unavoidable. Together, these results give a fine-grained and nearly complete picture of uniform convergence for halfspaces, revealing sharp dimensional and structural thresholds.

AI.tldr: Halfspaces exhibit sharp threshold behavior: inhomogeneous halfspaces match worst-case VC bounds, while 2D homogeneous halfspaces show significantly better convergence with O(1/n) error in realizable case and near-log-free deviation bounds in agnostic case.
AI.motivation: To understand fine-grained uniform convergence behavior of halfspaces beyond worst-case VC dimension bounds, and to reveal dimensional and structural thresholds that differentiate homogeneous vs. inhomogeneous halfspaces in different dimensions.
AI.method: Analyze inhomogeneous and homogeneous halfspaces separately. For homogeneous halfspaces in ℝ², use critical-wedge localization argument to prove bandwise, log-free deviation bounds on dyadic risk bands, then union over bands with lnlnn overhead.
AI.result: Inhomogeneous halfspaces achieve worst-case bounds (Θ(dln(n/d)/n) in realizable case, √(τln(1/τ)) deviation in agnostic case). 2D homogeneous halfspaces: O(1/n) error in realizable case, lnlnn overhead unavoidable in agnostic case via bandwise analysis.
AI.conclusion: Halfspaces exhibit sharp dimensional and structural thresholds: inhomogeneous halfspaces match VC bounds, while 2D homogeneous halfspaces show dramatically better convergence with near-log-free behavior, completing fine-grained picture of uniform convergence.

title: Rethinking Adapter Placement: A Dominant Adaptation Module Perspective
summary: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) is a widely used parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that places trainable low-rank adapters into frozen pre-trained models. Recent studies show that using fewer LoRA adapters may still maintain or even improve performance, but existing methods still distribute adapters broadly, leaving where to place a limited number of adapters to maximize performance largely open. To investigate this, we introduce PAGE (Projected Adapter Gradient Energy), a gradient-based sensitivity probe that estimates the initial trainable gradient energy available to each candidate LoRA adapter. Surprisingly, we find that PAGE is highly concentrated on a single shallow FFN down-projection across two model families and four downstream tasks. We term this module the dominant adaptation module and show that its layer index is architecture-dependent but task-stable. Motivated by this finding, we propose DomLoRA, a placement method that places a single adapter at the dominant adaptation module. With only ~0.7% of vanilla LoRA's trainable parameters, DomLoRA outperforms it on average across various downstream tasks, including instruction following, mathematical reasoning, code generation, and multi-turn conversation. This method also improves other LoRA variants, supporting the dominant adaptation module perspective as a practical placement guideline.

AI.tldr: DomLoRA places a single LoRA adapter at a dominant shallow FFN down-projection module, achieving superior performance with only ~0.7% of vanilla LoRA's parameters across multiple tasks.
AI.motivation: While recent studies show fewer LoRA adapters can maintain or improve performance, existing methods still distribute adapters broadly, leaving optimal placement of limited adapters as an open question. The paper aims to determine where to place a limited number of adapters to maximize performance.
AI.method:
1. Introduces PAGE (Projected Adapter Gradient Energy) - a gradient-based sensitivity probe that estimates initial trainable gradient energy for each candidate LoRA adapter. 
2. Discovers that PAGE is highly concentrated on a single shallow FFN down-projection across multiple models and tasks.
3. Identifies this as the "dominant adaptation module" with layer index that is architecture-dependent but task-stable.
4. Proposes DomLoRA - a placement method that places only a single adapter at this dominant adaptation module.
AI.result: DomLoRA with only ~0.7% of vanilla LoRA's trainable parameters outperforms vanilla LoRA on average across various downstream tasks including instruction following, mathematical reasoning, code generation, and multi-turn conversation. The method also improves other LoRA variants.
AI.conclusion: The dominant adaptation module perspective provides a practical placement guideline for LoRA adapters, showing that optimal performance can be achieved with minimal parameter overhead by focusing adaptation on a single key module rather than distributing adapters broadly.

title: Quantizing With Randomized Hadamard Transforms: Efficient Heuristic Now Proven
summary:
Uniform random rotations (URRs) are a common preprocessing step in modern quantization approaches used for gradient compression, inference acceleration, KV-cache compression, model weight quantization, and approximate nearest-neighbor search in vector databases. In practice, URRs are often replaced by randomized Hadamard transforms (RHTs), which preserve orthogonality while admitting fast implementations. The remaining issue is the performance for worst-case inputs. With a URR, each coordinate is individually distributed as a shifted beta distribution, which converges to a Gaussian distribution in high dimensions. Generally, one RHT is not suitable in the worst case, as individual coordinates can be far from these distributions. We show that after composing two RHTs on any $d$-sized input vector, the marginal distribution of every fixed coordinate of the normalized rotated vector is within $O(d^{-1/2})$ of a standard Gaussian both in Kolmogorov distance and in $1$-Wasserstein distance. We then plug these bounds into the analyses of modern compression schemes, namely DRIVE and QUIC-FL, and show that two RHTs achieve performance that asymptotically matches URRs.
  However, we show that two RHTs may not be sufficient for Vector Quantization (VQ), which often requires weak correlation across fixed-size blocks of coordinates (as opposed to only marginal distribution convergence for single coordinates). We prove that a composition of three RHTs leads to decaying coordinate covariance. This ensures that any fixed, bounded, multi-dimensional VQ codebook optimized for URRs has the same expected error when using three RHTs, up to an additive term that vanishes with the dimension.
  Finally, because practical inputs are rarely adversarial, we propose a linear-time ${O}(d)$ check on the input's moments to dynamically adapt the number of RHTs used at runtime to improve performance.

AI.tldr: Two randomized Hadamard transforms (RHTs) make individual coordinates Gaussian-like, matching URR performance for most compression schemes, but three RHTs are needed for vector quantization to ensure coordinate independence, with a runtime check to adapt the number of RHTs.
AI.motivation: Uniform random rotations (URRs) are widely used in quantization but often replaced by faster randomized Hadamard transforms (RHTs). However, RHTs may not perform well for worst-case inputs, creating a need to understand how many RHTs are needed to match URR performance for different compression schemes.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of coordinate distributions after RHT compositions: 1) Show two RHTs make individual coordinates near-Gaussian in Kolmogorov and Wasserstein distances, 2) Prove three RHTs ensure coordinate covariance decay for vector quantization, 3) Propose linear-time moment check to adapt number of RHTs at runtime.
AI.result: Two RHTs asymptotically match URR performance for DRIVE and QUIC-FL compression schemes, while three RHTs are required for vector quantization to achieve the same expected error. A practical runtime check adapts RHT count based on input moments.
AI.conclusion: The number of RHTs needed depends on the application: two suffice for schemes relying on coordinate-wise Gaussianity, but three are necessary for vector quantization requiring coordinate independence, with adaptive runtime selection providing practical benefits.

title: Event-Causal RAG: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework for Long Video Reasoning in Complex Scenarios
summary: Recent large vision-language models have achieved strong performance on short- and medium-length video understanding, yet they remain inadequate for ultra-long or even infinite video reasoning, where models must preserve coherent memory over extended durations and infer causal dependencies across temporally distant events. Existing end-to-end video understanding methods are fundamentally limited by the $O(n^2)$ complexity of self-attention, while recent retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approaches still suffer from fragmented clip-level memory, weak modeling of temporal and causal structure, and high storage and online inference costs. We present Event-Causal RAG, a lightweight retrieval-augmented framework for infinite long-video reasoning. Instead of indexing fixed-length clips, our method segments streaming videos into semantically coherent events and represents each event as a structured State-Event-State (SES) graph, capturing the event together with its surrounding state transitions. These graphs are merged into a global Event Knowledge Graph and stored in a dual-store memory that supports both semantic matching and causal-topological retrieval. On top of this memory, we design a bidirectional retrieval strategy to efficiently identify the most relevant event causal chains and provide them, together with the associated video evidence, to a backbone video foundation model for answer generation. Experiments on long-video understanding benchmarks demonstrate that Event-Causal RAG consistently outperforms strong clip-based retrieval baselines and long-context video models, particularly on questions requiring multi-event integration and causal inference across long temporal gaps, while also achieving improved memory efficiency and robust streaming performance.

AI.tldr: Event-Causal RAG: A lightweight retrieval-augmented framework for infinite video reasoning that segments videos into coherent events as State-Event-State graphs, enabling efficient causal-temporal reasoning across extended durations.
AI.motivation: Current vision-language models struggle with ultra-long video reasoning due to O(n²) complexity of self-attention and fragmented clip-level memory in existing RAG approaches, which fail to model temporal and causal structure effectively across extended time periods.
AI.method: Segments streaming videos into semantically coherent events, representing each as structured State-Event-State (SES) graphs capturing event states and transitions. These merge into a global Event Knowledge Graph stored in dual-store memory supporting semantic matching and causal-topological retrieval. Uses bidirectional retrieval to identify relevant event causal chains for video foundation models.
AI.result: Outperforms clip-based retrieval baselines and long-context video models on long-video benchmarks, especially on questions requiring multi-event integration and causal inference across temporal gaps, while achieving improved memory efficiency and robust streaming performance.
AI.conclusion: Event-Causal RAG provides an effective framework for infinite video reasoning by addressing key limitations of existing approaches through structured event representation, dual-store memory, and bidirectional retrieval that preserves temporal and causal relationships across extended video sequences.

title: Matrix-Decoupled Concentration for Autoregressive Sequences: Dimension-Free Guarantees for Sparse Long-Context Rewards
summary: Sequence-level evaluations in autoregressive Large Language Models (LLMs) rely on highly dependent token generation. Establishing tight concentration bounds for these processes remains a challenge due to two fundamental bottlenecks in existing frameworks: (i) classical inequalities typically separate dependency structures from target sensitivities, leading to a scalar collapse that inflates the variance proxy to a suboptimal $\mathcal{O}(N)$ for sparse terminal rewards; (ii) conversely, while certain spatial methods achieve tighter bounds, they lack the strictly causal filtration required by sequential generation, rendering them inapplicable to the autoregressive setting. To resolve both bottlenecks, we establish a sharp McDiarmid-type inequality for dependent sequences, governed strictly by the exact matrix-vector multiplication of the causal dependency resolvent and the target sensitivity vector. This Matrix-Decoupled Concentration (MDC) framework natively recovers optimal constants for Markov chains and exploits directed $d$-separation to yield order-optimal bounds for causal trees. Crucially, by exactly preserving the coordinate-wise sparsity of rewards within a strictly causal framework, MDC mathematically prevents scalar collapse, guaranteeing a dimension-free $\mathcal{O}(1)$ variance proxy and providing a rigorous mathematical justification for the stability of long-context reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Matrix-Decoupled Concentration (MDC) framework to establish sharp concentration bounds for dependent sequences in autoregressive LLMs, overcoming limitations of existing methods by preserving sparsity and causal structure.
AI.motivation: Current concentration bounds for autoregressive LLMs face two bottlenecks: (1) classical inequalities separate dependency structures from sensitivities, leading to suboptimal O(N) variance for sparse rewards, and (2) spatial methods achieve tighter bounds but lack the causal filtration required for sequential generation.
AI.method: Proposes a sharp McDiarmid-type inequality for dependent sequences governed by matrix-vector multiplication of causal dependency resolvent and target sensitivity vector. The MDC framework preserves coordinate-wise sparsity within a strictly causal framework, preventing "scalar collapse."
AI.result: MDC achieves optimal constants for Markov chains and order-optimal bounds for causal trees. Crucially, it guarantees a dimension-free O(1) variance proxy for sparse terminal rewards, providing rigorous justification for stability of long-context reasoning in LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The MDC framework resolves fundamental bottlenecks in concentration bounds for autoregressive LLMs by combining mathematical rigor with practical applicability, offering a theoretically sound foundation for stable long-context reasoning in language models.

title: OPSD Compresses What RLVR Teaches: A Post-RL Compaction Stage for Reasoning Models
summary: On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) has recently emerged as an alternative to Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), promising higher accuracy and shorter responses through token-level credit assignment from a self-teacher conditioned on privileged context. However, this promise does not carry over to thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning, where reported accuracy gains shrink and sometimes turn negative. We hypothesize that hindsight supervision can specify better token-level alternatives in short thinking-disabled outputs, but in long thinking-enabled traces it more readily identifies redundancy than supplies better replacements. To test this, we applied OPSD separately to correct and incorrect rollout groups, so that compression and correction can be observed in isolation. Our results show that in thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning, OPSD behaves most reliably as a compression mechanism rather than a correction mechanism: training only on correct rollouts preserves accuracy while substantially shortening responses, whereas training only on incorrect rollouts damages accuracy. In light of these findings, we propose a revised post-training pipeline for thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning: SFT then RLVR then OPSD.

AI.tldr: OPSD works as a compression mechanism in thinking-enabled math reasoning, shortening correct responses but not improving accuracy; SFT→RLVR→OPSD pipeline is proposed.
AI.motivation: OPSD has shown promise in RLVR settings but doesn't work well for thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning - accuracy gains shrink or become negative, suggesting OPSD may not be effectively correcting errors in long reasoning traces.
AI.method: Applied OPSD separately to correct and incorrect rollout groups to isolate compression and correction effects. Analyzed OPSD behavior specifically in thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning tasks.
AI.result: OPSD behaves as a compression mechanism rather than correction mechanism: training on correct rollouts preserves accuracy while shortening responses; training on incorrect rollouts damages accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Proposes revised pipeline for thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), then Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), then On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) as a final compression step.

title: Multi-agent decision making: A Blackwell's informativeness approach
summary: The rapid development of large language models (LLMs) has motivated research on decision-making in multi-agent systems, where multiple agents collaborate to achieve shared objectives. Existing aggregation approaches, such as voting and debate, are largely ad-hoc and lack formal guarantees regarding the informativeness of the resulting decisions. In this paper, we provide a principled approach to analyse decisions made in the multi-LLM setting using Blackwell's informativeness framework. Within the Blackwell information-structure abstraction, we show that voting and debate induce information structures that are no more informative than the pooled private information of all agents. This result identifies Bayesian pooled posterior maximisation as an information-theoretic upper-bound decision rule under the Blackwell ordering. Motivated by this theoretical analysis, we introduce a practical method for LLM-based question-answering (QA) tasks that estimates each agent's posterior and approximates the pooled posterior using a product-of-posteriors estimator. Extensive experiments on six QA benchmarks demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art multi-LLM debate and voting methods.

AI.tldr: A principled framework using Blackwell's informativeness theory shows voting and debate in multi-LLM systems are no more informative than pooled private information, leading to a practical posterior estimation method that outperforms existing approaches.
AI.motivation: The rapid development of large language models has driven research on multi-agent decision-making, but existing aggregation methods like voting and debate lack formal guarantees about the informativeness of resulting decisions.
AI.method: Using Blackwell's informativeness framework to analyze multi-LLM decisions theoretically, then developing a practical method that estimates each agent's posterior and approximates the pooled posterior using a product-of-posteriors estimator.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows voting and debate induce information structures no more informative than pooled private information. Experimental results on six QA benchmarks demonstrate the proposed approach outperforms state-of-the-art multi-LLM debate and voting methods.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a principled information-theoretic foundation for multi-LLM decision-making, establishing Bayesian pooled posterior maximization as an upper-bound decision rule and offering a practical implementation that improves performance over existing methods.

title: Systematic Evaluation of Large Language Models for Post-Discharge Clinical Action Extraction
summary: The work in this paper evaluates zero-shot and few-shot large language models (LLMs) for safety-critical clinical action extraction using the CLIP discharge-note dataset, with particular emphasis on transitions of care and post-discharge patient safety. To manage the complexity of clinical documentation, we introduce a two-stage extraction framework that decomposes discharge notes, that are written in narrative form, into fine-grained, explicitly actionable clinical tasks through a staged prompting strategy. Our contributions include a systematic assessment of generative LLMs for clinical action extraction, a detailed comparison between general-purpose LLMs and task-specific supervised BERT-based models, and an analysis of annotation inconsistencies across different action categories. We show that contemporary LLMs achieve performance comparable to or exceeding supervised models on binary actionability detection, while supervised baselines retain a meaningful advantage on fine-grained multi-label category classification, despite the absence of task-specific fine-tuning and under strict data-privacy constraints. Qualitative error analysis reveals that many failures stem from misalignment between model reasoning and dataset annotation conventions, particularly in cases involving implicit clinical actions and rigid structural labeling rules. These results indicate that reported performance reflects model limitations due to lack of clinical reasoning, that is not captured by plain annotations. Labels without rationales make it impossible to distinguish clinical reasoning failures from annotation convention mismatches. Advancing clinical NLP requires reasoning-annotated datasets that document why specific spans are actionable, not merely which spans were labeled, enabling proper evaluation of model clinical understanding.

AI.tldr: LLMs show competitive performance on binary clinical action detection but supervised models still outperform on fine-grained classification, revealing that current clinical NLP evaluation suffers from annotation convention mismasks that obscure true clinical reasoning failures.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to evaluate LLMs for safety-critical clinical action extraction from discharge notes, focusing on transitions of care and post-discharge patient safety. There's a need to assess how well modern LLMs can handle complex clinical documentation compared to traditional supervised methods, while also addressing the challenge of annotation inconsistencies in clinical NLP.
AI.method: The authors introduce a two-stage extraction framework that decomposes narrative discharge notes into fine-grained actionable clinical tasks using staged prompting. They conduct a systematic evaluation comparing zero-shot/few-shot LLMs with task-specific supervised BERT-based models on the CLIP discharge-note dataset, while also analyzing annotation inconsistencies across different action categories.
AI.result: Contemporary LLMs achieve performance comparable to or exceeding supervised models on binary actionability detection. However, supervised baselines maintain a meaningful advantage on fine-grained multi-label category classification, even without task-specific fine-tuning and under data-privacy constraints. Error analysis reveals many failures stem from misalignment between model reasoning and dataset annotation conventions, particularly with implicit clinical actions and rigid labeling rules.
AI.conclusion: The reported performance reflects limitations due to lack of clinical reasoning not captured by plain annotations. The key insight is that labels without rationales make it impossible to distinguish clinical reasoning failures from annotation convention mismatches. Advancing clinical NLP requires reasoning-annotated datasets that document why specific spans are actionable, enabling proper evaluation of model clinical understanding beyond mere labeling.

title: Does Synthetic Data Help? Empirical Evidence from Deep Learning Time Series Forecasters
summary: Synthetic data has transformed language model training, yet its role in time series forecasting remains poorly understood. We present a large-scale empirical study: nine experiment groups, 4,218 runs systematically evaluating synthetic time series augmentation across five architectures, four synthetic signals and seven datasets. The effect is sharply architecture-conditional: channel-mixing models (TimesNet, iTransformer) benefit in the majority of trials, while channel-independent models (DLinear, PatchTST) are consistently degraded. In selected low-resource settings the gains are striking: TimesNet trained on only 10\% of Weather data with synthetic augmentation surpasses the full-data baseline (4 of 16 sparsity-dataset combinations). Averaged across all architectures, augmentation hurts in 67\% of trials. We further find that only the Seasonal-Trend generator reliably helps across the tested benchmarks, and that hard curriculum switching is actively harmful (+24\% MSE degradation). These results provide concrete, actionable guidelines on how to use synthetic data: use synthetic augmentation with channel-mixing architectures, use gradual annealing schedules, and treat low-resource augmentation as architecture- and dataset-dependent. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/hugoiscracked/synthetic-ts/tree/main}

AI.tldr: Synthetic data in time series forecasting helps channel-mixing architectures but harms channel-independent ones, with best gains in low-resource settings when using seasonal-trend generators and gradual schedules.
AI.motivation: Synthetic data has transformed language model training but its role in time series forecasting remains poorly understood, necessitating systematic evaluation to provide actionable guidelines.
AI.method: Large-scale empirical study with 4,218 runs across 9 experiment groups, evaluating synthetic time series augmentation across 5 architectures, 4 synthetic signal generators, and 7 datasets with systematic testing.
AI.result: Effect is sharply architecture-conditional: channel-mixing models (TimesNet, iTransformer) benefit in majority of trials while channel-independent models (DLinear, PatchTST) are consistently degraded. Low-resource gains are striking (TimesNet trained on 10% Weather data with augmentation surpasses full-data baseline). Averaged across architectures, augmentation hurts in 67% of trials. Only Seasonal-Trend generator reliably helps across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Provide concrete guidelines: use synthetic augmentation with channel-mixing architectures, employ gradual annealing schedules, and treat low-resource augmentation as architecture- and dataset-dependent.

title: The Granularity Axis: A Micro-to-Macro Latent Direction for Social Roles in Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are routinely prompted to take on social roles ranging from individuals to institutions, yet it remains unclear whether their internal representations encode the granularity of such roles, from micro-level individual experience to macro-level organizational, institutional, or national reasoning. We show that they do. We define a contrast-based Granularity Axis as the difference between mean macro- and micro-role hidden states. In Qwen3-8B, this axis aligns with the principal axis (PC1) of the role representation space at cosine 0.972 and accounts for 52.6% of its variance, indicating that granularity is the dominant geometric axis organizing prompted social roles. We construct 75 social roles across five granularity levels and collect 91,200 role-conditioned responses over shared questions and prompt variants, then extract role-level hidden states and project them onto the axis. Role projections increase monotonically across all five levels, remain stable across layers, prompt variants, endpoint definitions, held-out splits, and score-filtered subsets, and transfer to Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. The axis is also causally relevant: activation steering along it shifts response granularity in the predicted direction, with Llama moving from 2.00 to 3.17 on a five-point macro scale under positive steering on prompts that admit local responses. The two models differ in controllability, suggesting that steering depends on each model's default operating regime. Overall, our findings suggest that social role granularity is not merely a stylistic surface feature, but a structured, ordered, and causally manipulable latent direction in role-conditioned language model behavior.

AI.tldr: LLMs internally represent social roles along a granularity axis from individual to institutional levels, which is the dominant geometric organization in role representation space and can be causally manipulated to shift response granularity.
AI.motivation: While LLMs are commonly prompted to adopt various social roles (from individuals to institutions), it's unclear whether their internal representations actually encode the granularity differences between micro-level individual experiences and macro-level organizational/institutional reasoning.
AI.method: Defined a contrast-based Granularity Axis as the difference between mean macro- and micro-role hidden states. Created 75 social roles across five granularity levels, collected 91,200 role-conditioned responses over shared questions, extracted hidden states, and projected them onto the axis. Tested across layers, prompt variants, endpoint definitions, held-out splits, score-filtered subsets, and different models (Qwen3-8B and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct). Used activation steering to causally manipulate the axis.
AI.result: In Qwen3-8B, the Granularity Axis aligns with the principal axis (PC1) of role representation space at cosine 0.972, accounting for 52.6% of variance. Role projections increase monotonically across all five granularity levels. The axis remains stable across various conditions and transfers to Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. Activation steering along the axis causally shifts response granularity (Llama moving from 2.00 to 3.17 on a five-point macro scale with positive steering). Models differ in controllability based on their default operating regimes.
AI.conclusion: Social role granularity is not just a stylistic surface feature in LLMs, but a structured, ordered, and causally manipulable latent direction in role-conditioned language model behavior that organizes role representations at a fundamental geometric level.

title: Optimal Transport for LLM Reward Modeling from Noisy Preference
summary: Reward models are fundamental to Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), yet real-world datasets are inevitably corrupted by noisy preference. Conventional training objectives tend to overfit these errors, while existing denoising approaches often rely on homogeneous noise assumptions that fail to capture the complexity of linguistic preferences. To handle these challenges, we propose SelectiveRM, a framework grounded in optimal transport. We first devise a Joint Consistency Discrepancy to align the distribution of model predictions with preference data. Furthermore, to address the limitation of strict mass conservation which compels the model to fit outliers, we incorporate a Mass Relaxation mechanism via partial transport. This enables the autonomous exclusion of samples with noisy preference that contradict semantic consistency. Theoretically, we demonstrate that SelectiveRM optimizes a tighter upper bound on the unobserved clean risk. Extensive experiments validate that our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks.

AI.tldr: SelectiveRM is an optimal transport-based framework for robust reward model training in RLHF that handles noisy preference labels by aligning model predictions with data distribution and excluding contradictory samples via partial transport.
AI.motivation: Real-world datasets for RLHF contain inevitable noisy preferences, and conventional training objectives overfit these errors. Existing denoising approaches rely on homogeneous noise assumptions that fail to capture the complexity of linguistic preferences, creating a need for more robust methods.
AI.method: Proposes SelectiveRM framework grounded in optimal transport: (1) Devises Joint Consistency Discrepancy to align distribution of model predictions with preference data, (2) Incorporates Mass Relaxation mechanism via partial transport to enable autonomous exclusion of samples with noisy preferences that contradict semantic consistency.
AI.result: Extensive experiments validate that SelectiveRM significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks. Theoretically, the approach optimizes a tighter upper bound on the unobserved clean risk.
AI.conclusion: SelectiveRM provides an effective framework for robust reward model training in RLHF by addressing noisy preference labels through optimal transport theory, enabling better handling of complex linguistic preferences while avoiding overfitting to corrupted data.

title: Towards Annotation-Free Validation of MLLMs: A Vision-Language Logical Consistency Metric
summary: Dominant accuracy evaluation might reward unwarranted guessing of Large Language Models, and it might not be applicable to novel tasks for model validation without ground-truth (gt) annotation. Based on basic logic principle, we propose a novel framework to evaluate the vision-language logical consistency of MLLMs on both sufficient and necessary cause-effect relations. We define Vision-Language Logical Consistency Metric (VL-LCM) on traditional MC-VQA tests, and recent NaturalBench tests without the need for gt annotation.
