While token-histogram baselines achieve higher purity, the graph representation offers valuable structural insights with stable cluster assignments across hyperparameters and random seeds.

title: Contrastive Identification and Generation in the Limit
summary: In the classical identification in the limit model of Gold [1967], a stream of positive examples is presented round by round, and the learner must eventually recover the target hypothesis. Recently, Kleinberg and Mullainathan [2024] introduced generation in the limit, where the learner instead must eventually output novel elements of the target's support. Both lines of work focus on positive-only or fully labeled data. Yet many natural supervision signals are inherently relational rather than singleton, which encode relationships between examples rather than labels of individual ones. We initiate the study of contrastive identification and generation in the limit, where the learner observes a contrastive presentation of data: a stream of unordered pairs $\{x,y\}$ satisfying $h(x)\ne h(y)$ for an unknown target binary hypothesis $h$, but which element is positive is hidden from the learner. We first present three results in the noiseless setting: an exact characterization of contrastive identifiable classes (a one-line geometric refinement of Angluin [1980]'s tell-tale condition), a combinatorial dimension called contrastive closure dimension (a contrasitive analogue of the closure dimension in Raman et al. [2025]) and exactly characterizing uniform contrastive generation with tight sample complexity, and a strict hierarchy in which contrastive generation and text identification are mutually incomparable. We then prove a sharp reversal under finite adversarial corruption: there exist classes identifiable from contrastive pairs under any finite corruption budget by a single budget-independent algorithm, yet not identifiable from positive examples under even one corrupted observation. The unifying technical object is the common crossing graph, which encodes pairwise ambiguity, family-level generation obstructions, and corruption defects in a single coverage-and-incidence language.

AI.tldr: Contrastive learning in limit models: studying binary hypothesis learning from unordered pairs where labels differ but which is positive is hidden, characterizing identifiability and generation, with surprising results under corruption.
AI.motivation: Current learning in the limit models focus on positive-only or fully labeled data, but many real-world supervision signals are relational (contrastive pairs) rather than singleton labels. The authors want to study learning from contrastive pairs where only the relationship (difference) between examples is known, not which specific example is positive.
AI.method: Introduce contrastive identification and generation in the limit framework, analyze using: 1) geometric refinement of Angluin's tell-tale condition for contrastive identifiability, 2) contrastive closure dimension for uniform generation, 3) common crossing graph as unifying technical tool encoding pairwise ambiguity and generation obstructions, 4) study both noiseless and adversarial corruption settings.
AI.result: 1) Exact characterization of contrastively identifiable classes. 2) Combinatorial dimension called contrastive closure dimension characterizes uniform contrastive generation with tight sample complexity. 3) Contrastive generation and text identification are mutually incomparable. 4) Sharp reversal under corruption: classes identifiable from contrastive pairs under any finite corruption budget by single algorithm, yet not identifiable from positive examples under even one corruption.
AI.conclusion: Contrastive learning in the limit introduces new phenomena not present in classical settings, with surprising power under corruption. The common crossing graph provides unifying framework for analyzing pairwise ambiguity, generation obstructions, and corruption defects.

title: Process Matters more than Output for Distinguishing Humans from Machines
summary: Reliable human-machine discrimination is becoming increasingly important as large language models and autonomous agents are deployed in online settings. Existing approaches evaluate whether a system can produce behavior or responses indistinguishable from those of a human, following the emphasis on outputs as a criterion for intelligence proposed by Alan Turing. Cognitive science offers an alternative perspective: evaluating the process by which behavior is produced. To test whether cognitive processes can reliably distinguish humans from machines, we introduce CogCAPTCHA30, a battery of 30 cognitive tasks designed to elicit diagnostic process-level features even when task performance is matched. Across the battery, process-level features provide stronger discriminative signal than performance metrics alone, reliably distinguishing humans from agents even under output matching (mean process-feature classifier AUC = 0.88). To evaluate agentic process differences, we compare off-the-shelf frontier agents (Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro), Centaur (a language model fine-tuned on 10.7M human decisions), and two task-specific fine-tuning approaches applied to Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct: action-level supervised fine-tuning (A-SFT) and process-level fine-tuning (P-SFT), which directly optimizes process features. Broad fine-tuning on human decisions improves human-like task processes relative to off-the-shelf agents, while task-specific process-level supervision further improves behavioral mimicry. However, this advantage diminishes under cross-task transfer when supervised process targets do not naturally generalize across tasks. Explicit process-level supervision can improve human behavioral mimicry, but only if appropriate task-specific process representations are available, highlighting process specification as a bottleneck for achieving human-like cognitive processes in machines.

AI.tldr: CogCAPTCHA30 uses 30 cognitive tasks with process-level features to distinguish humans from AI agents, even when task performance is matched, achieving mean AUC of 0.88.
AI.motivation: Reliable human-machine discrimination is crucial as AI systems become more prevalent online. Traditional approaches focus on output similarity, but cognitive science suggests evaluating the underlying cognitive processes could provide better discrimination.
AI.method: Developed CogCAPTCHA30 battery with 30 cognitive tasks designed to elicit diagnostic process-level features. Tested various AI systems: frontier agents (Claude, GPT-5, Gemini), a Centaur model fine-tuned on human decisions, and task-specific fine-tuned Qwen2.5 models with action-level (A-SFT) and process-level (P-SFT) supervision.
AI.result: Process-level features provided stronger discriminative signal than performance metrics alone (mean AUC = 0.88). Broad fine-tuning on human decisions improved human-like processes, and process-level supervision further enhanced behavioral mimicry, but advantages diminished under cross-task transfer.
AI.conclusion: Explicit process-level supervision can improve human behavioral mimicry, but only with appropriate task-specific process representations, highlighting process specification as the key bottleneck for achieving human-like cognitive processes in machines.

title: Playing the network backward: A Game Theoretic Attribution Framework
summary: Attribution methods explain which input features drive a model's prediction, making them central to model debugging and mechanistic interpretability. Yet backward attribution methods, including gradients, LRP, and transformer-specific rules, lack a shared framework in which to compare the underlying backward calculations. We introduce such a framework by recasting backward attribution as a two-player game on an extended network graph, building on Gaubert and Vlassopoulos' ReLU Net Game. Gradients and the full alpha-beta-LRP family arise as integrals over game trajectories under specific equilibria, so attribution maps become projections of trajectory distributions rather than the primary object. Desired explanation properties, such as localisation focus, robustness to input noise, or stable attention routing, can be specified as game-theoretic concepts, including policy regularization, risk aversion, and extended action sets, and translate directly into novel adaptations of the well-known backward rules. On ViT-B/16, one such selected adaptation of alpha-beta-LRP outperforms prior transformer-specific backward methods across all considered localisation metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a game-theoretic framework for backward attribution methods, unifying gradients, LRP, and transformer-specific rules as integrals over game trajectories, enabling systematic adaptation of attribution methods through game-theoretic concepts.
AI.motivation: Backward attribution methods lack a shared framework for comparison, making it difficult to systematically understand, compare, and adapt different attribution techniques like gradients, LRP, and transformer-specific rules.
AI.method: The authors recast backward attribution as a two-player game on an extended network graph, building on Gaubert and Vlassopoulos' ReLU Net Game. They show how gradients and alpha-beta-LRP family arise as integrals over game trajectories under specific equilibria, enabling desired explanation properties to be specified as game-theoretic concepts.
AI.result: One adaptation of alpha-beta-LRP derived from this framework outperforms prior transformer-specific backward methods across all considered localisation metrics on ViT-B/16, demonstrating the practical utility of the game-theoretic perspective.
AI.conclusion: The game-theoretic framework provides a unified foundation for understanding, comparing, and systematically adapting backward attribution methods, transforming attribution maps from primary objects to projections of trajectory distributions and enabling principled design of attribution methods with desired properties.

title: Market-Alignment Risk in Pricing Agents: Trace Diagnostics and Trace-Prior RL under Hidden Competitor State
summary: Outcome metrics can certify the wrong behavior. We study this failure in a two-hotel revenue-management simulator where Hotel A trains an agent against a fixed rule-based revenue-management competitor, Hotel B. A standard learning agent can obtain near-reference revenue per available room (RevPAR) while failing to learn market-like yield management: it sells too aggressively, undercuts, or collapses to modal price buckets. We diagnose this as a Goodhart-style failure under partial observability. Hotel A cannot observe the competitor's remaining inventory, booking curve, or pricing rule, so the same Hotel A-visible state maps to multiple plausible Hotel B prices. Deterministic value-based RL and deterministic copying collapse this unresolved uncertainty into shortcut behavior. We introduce a trace-level diagnostic protocol using RevPAR, occupancy, ADR, full price-bucket distributions, L1/JS distances, and seed-level confidence intervals. The verified repair is Trace-Prior RL: learn a distributional market prior from lagged market traces, then train a stochastic pricing policy with a RevPAR reward and a KL penalty to the learned prior. The final policy matches Hotel B's RevPAR, occupancy, ADR, and price distribution within seed-level uncertainty, while still optimizing Hotel A's own reward. We argue that the contribution is not a new optimizer and not a hotel-pricing leaderboard, but a reproducible failure-and-repair recipe for agentic systems where scalar rewards are easy to game and the intended behavior is only visible in traces. A key finding is that higher exact action accuracy can worsen aggregate trace alignment when the target is distributional.

AI.tldr: Standard RL agents can achieve good scalar metrics (RevPAR) while learning wrong behavior (poor yield management) - a Goodhart failure under partial observability. Fix with Trace-Prior RL: learn market distribution from traces, then train with RevPAR reward plus KL penalty to prior.
AI.motivation: Scalar outcome metrics like RevPAR can be gamed by agents, certifying wrong behavior. In revenue management with partial observability (Hotel A can't see competitor's inventory/pricing rules), agents can achieve near-reference RevPAR while exhibiting market-damaging behaviors like aggressive discounting or price collapse.
AI.method: Diagnose using trace-level protocol with multiple metrics (RevPAR, occupancy, ADR, price distributions, distances, confidence intervals). Repair with Trace-Prior RL: 1) Learn distributional market prior from lagged market traces, 2) Train stochastic pricing policy with RevPAR reward + KL penalty to learned prior.
AI.result: Trace-Prior RL policies match Hotel B's key metrics (RevPAR, occupancy, ADR, price distribution) within statistical uncertainty while optimizing Hotel A's own reward. Higher action accuracy can worsen trace alignment when target is distributional.
AI.conclusion: Main contribution is not a new optimizer or benchmark, but a reproducible failure-and-repair recipe for agentic systems where scalar rewards are easy to game and intended behavior is only visible in traces. Distributional regularization via learned priors from market traces prevents Goodhart failures under partial observability.

title: AffineLens: Capturing the Continuous Piecewise Affine Functions of Neural Networks
summary: Piecewise affine neural networks (PANNs) provide a principled geometric perspective on neural network expressivity by characterizing the input--output map as a continuous piecewise affine (CPA) function whose complexity is governed by the number, arrangement, and shapes of its affine regions. However, existing interpretability and expressivity analyses often rely on indirect proxies (e.g., activation statistics or theoretical upper bounds) and rarely offer practical, accurate tools for enumerating and visualizing the induced region partition under realistic architectures and bounded input domains. In this work, we present AffineLens, a unified framework for computing the hyperplane arrangements and polyhedral structures underlying PANNs. Given a calibrated (bounded) input polytope, AffineLens identifies the subset of neuron-induced hyperplanes that intersect the domain, enumerates the resulting affine sub-regions in a layer-wise manner, and returns provably non-empty maximal CPA regions together with interior representatives. The framework further provides visualizations of region partitioning and decision boundaries, enabling qualitative inspection alongside quantitative region counts. By exploiting the affine restriction property of CPA networks under fixed activation patterns, AffineLens supports a broad class of modern components, including batch normalization, pooling, residual connections, multilayer perceptrons, and convolutional layers. Finally, we use AffineLens to perform a systematic empirical study of architectural expressivity, comparing networks through region complexity metrics and revealing how design choices influence the geometry of learned functions.

AI.tldr: AffineLens is a practical framework that directly computes and visualizes the piecewise affine regions in neural networks, enabling systematic analysis of their geometric expressivity.
AI.motivation: Existing analyses of piecewise affine neural networks (PANNs) rely on indirect proxies like activation statistics or theoretical bounds, lacking practical tools to actually compute, enumerate, and visualize the affine region partitions in realistic architectures with bounded input domains.
AI.method: AffineLens framework identifies neuron-induced hyperplanes intersecting bounded input domains, enumerates affine regions layer-wise using the CPA networks' affine restriction property, computes provably non-empty maximal regions with interior representatives, and provides visualization tools.
AI.result: AffineLens works with modern components (batch normalization, pooling, residual connections, CNNs), enables systematic empirical studies of architectural expressivity through region complexity metrics, and reveals how design choices influence learned function geometry.
AI.conclusion: AffineLens provides a unified, practical framework for directly analyzing the geometric structure of piecewise affine neural networks, moving beyond theoretical proxies to enable concrete visualization and quantitative analysis of neural network expressivity.

title: SpatialEpiBench: Benchmarking Spatial Information and Epidemic Priors in Forecasting
summary: Accurate epidemic forecasting is crucial for public health response, resource allocation, and outbreak intervention, but remains difficult with sparse, noisy, and highly non-stationary data. Because epidemics unfold across interacting regions, spatiotemporal methods are natural candidates for improving forecasts. Despite growing interest in spatial information, no standardized benchmark exists, and current evaluations often use simple chronological train-test splits that do not reflect real-time forecasting practice. We address this gap with SpatialEpiBench, a challenging benchmark for spatiotemporal epidemic forecasting in realistic public-health settings. SpatialEpiBench includes 11 epidemic datasets with standardized rolling evaluations and outbreak-specific metrics. We evaluate adjacency-informed forecasting models with widely used epidemic priors that adapt general models to epidemiology, but find that most methods underperform a simple last-value baseline from 1 day to 1 month ahead, even during outbreaks and with these priors. We identify three major failure modes: (1) poor outbreak anticipation, (2) difficulty handling sparsity and noise, and (3) limited utility of common geographic adjacency for epidemiological spatial information. We release benchmark data, code, and instructions at https://github.com/Rachel-Lyu/SpatialEpiBench to support development of operationally useful epidemic forecasting models.

AI.tldr: SpatialEpiBench is a new challenging benchmark for spatiotemporal epidemic forecasting that reveals current methods underperform simple baselines and identifies three key failure modes in real-world public health scenarios.
AI.motivation: Epidemic forecasting is crucial for public health but difficult due to sparse, noisy, non-stationary data. While spatiotemporal methods seem promising, there's no standardized benchmark, and current evaluations don't reflect real-time forecasting practice, creating a gap in assessing model utility for operational public health.
AI.method: The authors developed SpatialEpiBench - a benchmark with 11 epidemic datasets, standardized rolling evaluations (instead of simple chronological splits), and outbreak-specific metrics. They evaluated adjacency-informed forecasting models with epidemiological priors against a simple last-value baseline across different forecast horizons (1 day to 1 month).
AI.result: Most spatiotemporal forecasting methods underperformed a simple last-value baseline across all forecast horizons, even during outbreaks and despite using epidemiological priors. The benchmark revealed three major failure modes: poor outbreak anticipation, difficulty handling sparsity and noise, and limited utility of geographic adjacency for capturing epidemiological spatial relationships.
AI.conclusion: Current spatiotemporal epidemic forecasting methods have significant limitations in practical public health settings. The SpatialEpiBench provides a realistic evaluation framework to support development of operationally useful models, highlighting that fundamental methodological improvements are needed rather than just applying general spatiotemporal models with epidemiological priors.

title: Memory Inception: Latent-Space KV Cache Manipulation for Steering LLMs
summary: Steering large language models (LLMs) is usually done by either instruction prompting or activation steering. Prompting often gives strong control, but caches guidance tokens at every layer and can clutter long interactions; activation steering is compact but typically weaker and does not support large structured reminders. We introduce memory inception (MI), a training-free method that steers in latent attention space by inserting text-derived key-value (KV) banks only at selected layers. Rather than materializing reminder content throughout the prompt cache, MI treats steering as selective KV allocation, injecting latent slots only where the model routes to them. On matched personality-steering tasks, MI gives the best overall control--drift trade-off, remaining competitive with prompting while consistently outperforming CAA. On updateable guidance, MI supports mid-conversation behavior shifts without rewriting the visible transcript, achieving the highest post-shift alignment on Qwen3. On structured reasoning, MI outperforms visible prompting on HARDMath and PHYSICS (10/12 subject$\times$mode cells), serving as proxies for structured reasoning in verifiable domains, while cutting content-matched KV storage by up to 118$\times$. These results position MI as a powerful steering method when guidance is persistent, structured, or expensive to keep in the visible transcript.

AI.tldr: Memory Inception (MI) is a training-free steering method that inserts text-derived key-value banks selectively in latent attention space, achieving better control-drift trade-off than prompting or activation steering while using far less KV storage.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for steering large language models have limitations: prompting provides strong control but clutters interactions and caches guidance tokens at every layer, while activation steering is compact but weaker and doesn't support large structured reminders.
AI.method: Memory Inception steers LLMs in latent attention space by inserting text-derived key-value banks only at selected layers, treating steering as selective KV allocation. It injects latent slots only where the model routes to them, avoiding materializing reminder content throughout the prompt cache.
AI.result: On personality-steering tasks: best overall control-drift trade-off, competitive with prompting while consistently outperforming CAA. On updateable guidance: supports mid-conversation behavior shifts without rewriting visible transcript, achieving highest post-shift alignment on Qwen3. On structured reasoning: outperforms visible prompting on HARDMath and PHYSICS (10/12 subject×mode cells) while cutting content-matched KV storage by up to 118×.
AI.conclusion: Memory Inception positions itself as a powerful steering method when guidance needs to be persistent, structured, or expensive to keep in the visible transcript, offering an effective alternative to both prompting and activation steering approaches.

title: Ex Ante Evaluation of AI-Induced Idea Diversity Collapse
summary: Creative AI systems are typically evaluated at the level of individual utility, yet creative outputs are consumed in populations: an idea loses value when many others produce similar ones. This creates an evaluation blind spot, as AI can improve individual outputs while increasing population-level crowding. We introduce a human-relative framework for benchmarking AI-induced human diversity collapse without requiring human-AI interaction data, providing an ex ante protocol to estimate crowding risk from model-only generations and matched unaided human baselines. By modeling ideas as congestible resources, we show that source-level crowding is identifiable from within-distribution comparisons, yielding an excess-crowding coefficient $Δ$ and a human-relative diversity ratio $ρ$. We show that $ρ\ge1$ is the no-excess-crowding parity condition and connect $Δ$ to an adoption game with exposure-dependent redundancy costs. Across short stories, marketing slogans, and alternative-uses tasks, three frontier LLMs fall below parity across crowding kernels. Estimates stabilize with feasible model-only sample sizes. Importantly, generation-protocol variants show that crowding can be reduced through targeted design, making diversity collapse an actionable, development-time evaluation target for population-aware creative AI.

AI.tldr: AI can improve individual creative outputs but may reduce population-level diversity through excessive similarity, creating a crowding problem that needs specific evaluation metrics and targeted design solutions.
AI.motivation: Current AI evaluation focuses on individual utility, but creative outputs lose value when many are similar. There's a blind spot where AI can improve individual outputs while increasing population-level crowding, potentially causing diversity collapse.
AI.method: Introduced a human-relative framework for benchmarking AI-induced diversity collapse without human-AI interaction data. Modeled ideas as congestible resources, showing source-level crowding is identifiable from within-distribution comparisons using excess-crowding coefficient Δ and human-relative diversity ratio ρ. Used an adoption game with exposure-dependent redundancy costs and tested across short stories, marketing slogans, and alternative-uses tasks with three frontier LLMs.
AI.result: Three frontier LLMs fell below the no-excess-crowding parity condition (ρ≥1) across different crowding kernels. Estimates stabilized with feasible model-only sample sizes. Generation-protocol variants showed that crowding can be reduced through targeted design, making diversity collapse actionable at development time.
AI.conclusion: AI-induced human diversity collapse is a measurable risk that can be evaluated ex ante using model-only generations. While current LLMs increase population-level crowding, targeted design interventions can mitigate this effect, making diversity an actionable evaluation target for population-aware creative AI systems.

title: Soft Deterministic Policy Gradient with Gaussian Smoothing
summary: Deterministic policy gradient (DPG) is widely utilized for continuous control; however, it inherently relies on the differentiability of the critic with respect to the action during policy updates. This assumption is violated in practical control problems involving sparse or discrete rewards, leading to ill-defined policy gradients and unstable learning. To address these challenges, we propose a principled alternative based on a smoothed Bellman equation formulated via Gaussian smoothing. Specifically, we define a novel action-value function based on a smoothed Bellman equation and derive the soft deterministic policy gradient (Soft-DPG). Our formulation eliminates explicit dependence on critic action-gradients and ensures that the gradient remains well-defined even for non-smooth Q-functions. We instantiate this framework into a deep reinforcement learning algorithm, which we call soft deep deterministic policy gradient (Soft DDPG). Empirical evaluations on standard continuous control benchmarks and their discretized-reward variants show that Soft DDPG remains competitive in dense-reward settings and provides clear gains in most discretized-reward environments, where standard DDPG is more sensitive to irregular critic landscapes.

AI.tldr: Soft DDPG: A smoothed Bellman equation approach using Gaussian smoothing to handle non-differentiable Q-functions in deterministic policy gradient methods, enabling stable learning with sparse/discrete rewards.
AI.motivation: Standard deterministic policy gradient (DPG) requires differentiability of the critic w.r.t. actions, which is violated in practical control problems with sparse/discrete rewards, causing ill-defined gradients and unstable learning.
AI.method: Proposes a smoothed Bellman equation via Gaussian smoothing, defining a novel action-value function and deriving soft deterministic policy gradient (Soft-DPG) that eliminates explicit dependence on critic action-gradients.
AI.result: Soft DDPG remains competitive in dense-reward settings and provides clear gains in most discretized-reward environments where standard DDPG struggles with irregular critic landscapes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework offers a principled alternative to DPG that handles non-smooth Q-functions, enabling stable learning in practical control problems with sparse or discrete rewards.

title: Improved techniques for fine-tuning flow models via adjoint matching: a deterministic control pipeline
summary: We propose a deterministic adjoint matching framework that formulates human preference alignment for flow-based generative models as an optimal control problem over velocity fields. One can directly regress the control toward a value-gradient-induced target under the current policy, leading to a simple and stable training objective. Building on this perspective, we introduce a truncated adjoint scheme that focuses computation on the terminal portion of the trajectory, where reward-relevant signals concentrate, which yields substantial computational savings while preserving alignment quality. We further generalize the framework beyond standard KL-based regularization, allowing more flexible trade-offs between alignment strength and distributional preservation. Experiments on SiT-XL/2 and FLUX.2-Klein-4B demonstrate consistent gains across multiple alignment metrics, along with substantially improved diversity and mode preservation.

AI.tldr: A deterministic adjoint matching framework for flow-based generative model alignment via optimal control over velocity fields with computational efficiency and flexible regularization.
AI.motivation: Traditional human preference alignment methods for flow-based generative models need improvement in stability, computational efficiency, and flexibility of regularization.
AI.method: Formulates alignment as optimal control over velocity fields using deterministic adjoint matching; introduces truncated adjoint scheme focusing computation on terminal trajectory portions; allows flexible trade-offs beyond standard KL regularization.
AI.result: Experiments on SiT-XL/2 and FLUX.2-Klein-4B show consistent gains across alignment metrics, with substantially improved diversity and mode preservation, while achieving computational savings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed deterministic adjoint matching framework provides a stable, computationally efficient, and flexible approach for human preference alignment in flow-based generative models.

title: Band Together: Untargeted Adversarial Training with Multimodal Coordination against Evasion-based Promotion Attacks
summary: Multimodal recommender systems exploit visual and textual signals to alleviate data sparsity, but this also makes them more vulnerable to evasion-based promotion attacks. Existing defenses are largely limited to single-modal settings and mainly focus on poisoning-based threats, leaving evasion-based threats underexplored. In this work, we first identify a cross-modal gradient mismatch under the multi-user promotion setting, where visual and textual perturbations are optimized in inconsistent directions due to the dominance of distinct user groups. This phenomenon dilutes the attack effectiveness and leads robust training to underestimate worst-case risks. To address this issue, we propose Untargeted Adversarial Training with Multimodal Coordination (UAT-MC). UAT-MC tackles the challenge of unknown targeted items in evasion-based attacks (as opposed to poisoning-based attacks) by treating all items as potential targets, and introduces a gradient alignment mechanism to explicitly correct this mismatch. This design ensures synchronized perturbations across modalities, thereby maximizing adversarial strength for robust training. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UAT-MC significantly improves robustness against promotion attacks while maintaining acceptable recommendation performance under the defense-accuracy trade-off. Code is available at https://github.com/gmXian/UAT-MC.

AI.tldr: UAT-MC is a defense method for multimodal recommender systems that addresses evasion-based promotion attacks through untargeted adversarial training with gradient alignment across modalities.
AI.motivation: Multimodal recommenders using visual/textual signals are vulnerable to evasion-based promotion attacks, but existing defenses focus on single-modal settings and poisoning-based threats, leaving evasion-based threats underexplored.
AI.method: Proposes Untargeted Adversarial Training with Multimodal Coordination (UAT-MC), which treats all items as potential targets (addressing unknown targets in evasion attacks) and introduces gradient alignment to synchronize perturbations across modalities.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show UAT-MC significantly improves robustness against promotion attacks while maintaining acceptable recommendation performance under the defense-accuracy trade-off.
AI.conclusion: UAT-MC effectively addresses cross-modal gradient mismatch in evasion attacks and provides a robust defense mechanism for multimodal recommender systems.

title: NeuroAgent: LLM Agents for Multimodal Neuroimaging Analysis and Research
summary: Multimodal neuroimaging analysis often involves complex, modality-specific preprocessing workflows that require careful configuration, quality control, and coordination across heterogeneous toolchains. Beyond preprocessing, downstream statistical analysis and disease classification commonly require task-specific code, evaluation protocols, and data-format conventions, creating additional barriers between raw acquisitions and reproducible scientific analysis. We present NeuroAgent, an LLM-driven agentic framework that automates key preprocessing and analysis steps for heterogeneous neuroimaging data, including sMRI, fMRI, dMRI, and PET, and supports interactive downstream analysis through natural-language queries. NeuroAgent employs a hierarchical multi-agent architecture with a feedback-driven Generate-Execute-Validate engine: agents autonomously generate executable preprocessing code, detect and recover from runtime errors, and validate output integrity. We evaluate the system on 1,470 subjects pooled across all ADNI phases (CN=1,000, AD=470), where all subjects have sMRI and tabular data, with subsets also having Tau-PET (n=469), fMRI (n=278), and DTI ($n=620$). Pipeline ablation studies across multiple LLM backends show that capable models reach up to 100% intent-parsing accuracy, with the strongest backend (Qwen3.5-27B) reaching 84.8% end-to-end preprocessing step correctness. Automated recovery limits manual intervention to edge cases where human review is required via the Human-In-The-Loop interface. For Alzheimer's Disease classification using automatically preprocessed multimodal data, our agent ensemble achieves an AUC of 0.9518 with four modalities, outperforming all single-modality baselines. These results show that NeuroAgent can reduce the manual effort required for neuroimaging preprocessing and enable end-to-end automated analysis pipelines for neuroimaging research.

AI.tldr: NeuroAgent is an LLM-driven framework that automates preprocessing and analysis of multimodal neuroimaging data (sMRI, fMRI, dMRI, PET) using a hierarchical multi-agent architecture with Generate-Execute-Validate engine, achieving high preprocessing accuracy and strong disease classification performance.
AI.motivation: Multimodal neuroimaging analysis involves complex, modality-specific preprocessing workflows requiring careful configuration, quality control, and coordination across heterogeneous toolchains. Downstream analysis also requires task-specific code and conventions, creating barriers between raw data and reproducible scientific analysis.
AI.method: Hierarchical multi-agent architecture with feedback-driven Generate-Execute-Validate engine. Agents autonomously generate executable preprocessing code, detect/recover from runtime errors, and validate output integrity. Human-In-The-Loop interface for edge cases. Evaluated on 1,470 ADNI subjects across modalities.
AI.result: Qwen3.5-27B backend achieves 84.8% end-to-end preprocessing step correctness and 100% intent-parsing accuracy. For Alzheimer's Disease classification, agent ensemble achieves AUC of 0.9518 with four modalities, outperforming all single-modality baselines. Automated recovery minimizes manual intervention.
AI.conclusion: NeuroAgent can significantly reduce manual effort for neuroimaging preprocessing and enable end-to-end automated analysis pipelines for neuroimaging research, demonstrating the potential of LLM-driven agents for complex scientific workflows.

title: When Graph Language Models Go Beyond Memorization
summary: It remains unclear whether graph language models learn structural regularities or merely memorize training graphs; this cannot be resolved by current aggregate fidelity metrics alone. We develop a calibrated diagnostic protocol that combines frequent subgraph mining, a graph-level bootstrap baseline, and three-level frequency stratification to disentangle memorization from structural alignment. Using this framework, we show that graph language models can acquire structural regularities beyond memorization at scale, primarily in the high-frequency regime. This is supported by the following empirical evidence: On five TU benchmarks, LLaMA-style graph language models reach high subgraph-rank correlation, yet their alignment is matched or exceeded by the memorization bootstrap in most cases. At small scale, under our bootstrap diagnostic, fidelity is largely indistinguishable from verbatim recall. In contrast, at large scale with 3.75M graphs, verbatim memorization drops sharply while rank correlation remains near ceiling. Crucially, in a separate fixed-subsample analysis, frequent subgraph mining restricted to the novel-only subset closely tracks the corresponding all-generation Spearman correlation, providing evidence that the alignment is not driven solely by verbatim recall. Across all scales, high-frequency patterns are well reproduced, while rare patterns remain poorly covered, and this deficit narrows only marginally as capacity increases. We observe the same scale-dependent crossover under two distinct graph serializations (canonical DFS code and action sequences), providing evidence of robustness in our analysis.

AI.tldr: Graph language models can learn structural regularities beyond memorization at large scale, primarily reproducing high-frequency patterns while struggling with rare patterns.
AI.motivation: It's unclear whether graph language models truly learn structural patterns or just memorize training graphs, as current aggregate fidelity metrics cannot distinguish between the two.
AI.method: Developed a calibrated diagnostic protocol combining frequent subgraph mining, graph-level bootstrap baseline, and three-level frequency stratification to disentangle memorization from structural alignment.
AI.result: At small scale, model performance is indistinguishable from memorization, but at large scale (3.75M graphs), verbatim memorization drops sharply while structural alignment remains high. High-frequency patterns are well reproduced across scales, while rare patterns remain poorly covered and improve only marginally with increased capacity.
AI.conclusion: Graph language models can acquire genuine structural regularities beyond mere memorization, particularly at large scale and for high-frequency patterns, though their ability to capture rare patterns remains limited.

title: SkillOS: Learning Skill Curation for Self-Evolving Agents
summary: LLM-based agents are increasingly deployed to handle streaming tasks, yet they often remain one-off problem solvers that fail to learn from past interactions. Reusable skills distilled from experience provide a natural substrate for self-evolution, where high-quality skill curation serves as the key bottleneck. Existing approaches either rely on manual skill curation, prescribe heuristic skill operations, or train for short-horizon skill operations. However, they still struggle to learn complex long-term curation policies from indirect and delayed feedback. To tackle this challenge, we propose SkillOS, an experience-driven RL training recipe for learning skill curation in self-evolving agents. SkillOS pairs a frozen agent executor that retrieves and applies skills with a trainable skill curator that updates an external SkillRepo from accumulated experience. To provide learning signals for curation, we design composite rewards and train on grouped task streams based on skill-relevant task dependencies, where earlier trajectories update the SkillRepo, and later related tasks evaluate these updates. Across multi-turn agentic tasks and single-turn reasoning tasks, SkillOS consistently outperforms memory-free and strong memory-based baselines in both effectiveness and efficiency, with the learned skill curator generalizing across different executor backbones and task domains. Further analyses show that the learned curator produces more targeted skill use, while the skills in SkillRepo evolve into more richly structured Markdown files that encode higher-level meta-skills over time.

AI.tldr: SkillOS is an RL-based training framework that enables LLM agents to learn long-term skill curation policies from experience, allowing them to self-evolve by building and improving reusable skills over time.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based agents deployed for streaming tasks fail to learn from past interactions and remain one-off problem solvers. While reusable skills offer a path to self-evolution, existing approaches struggle with complex long-term curation policies due to manual curation, heuristic operations, or short-horizon training that can't handle delayed feedback.
AI.method: SkillOS pairs a frozen agent executor (retrieves/applies skills) with a trainable skill curator that updates an external SkillRepo from experience. It uses composite rewards and trains on grouped task streams based on skill-relevant dependencies. Early trajectories update the SkillRepo, and later related tasks evaluate these updates.
AI.result: SkillOS outperforms memory-free and strong memory-based baselines across multi-turn agentic and single-turn reasoning tasks in both effectiveness and efficiency. The learned curator generalizes across different executor backbones and task domains. Skills evolve into richer Markdown files encoding higher-level meta-skills over time.
AI.conclusion: Experience-driven RL training enables effective learning of complex skill curation policies, leading to self-evolving agents that build reusable, structured skills and show improved task performance across diverse domains through targeted skill use.

title: Cumulative-Goodness Free-Riding in Forward-Forward Networks: Real, Repairable, but Not Accuracy-Dominant
summary: Forward-Forward (FF) training allows each layer to learn from a local goodness criterion. In cumulative-goodness variants, however, later layers can inherit a task that earlier layers have already partially separated. We formalize this phenomenon as layer free-riding: under the softplus FF criterion, the class-discrimination gradient reaching block $d$ decays exponentially with the positive margin accumulated by preceding blocks. We then study three local remedies -- per-block, hardness-gated, and depth-scaled -- that recover current-layer separation measures without relying on backpropagated gradients. On CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, these remedies dramatically improve layer-separation statistics, with $4\times$--$45\times$ gains in deeper layers, while changing accuracy by less than one percentage point for non-degenerate training procedures. Tiny ImageNet provides a tougher cross-dataset check for our selected block-wise configuration and reveals the same qualitative gap between layer-health diagnostics and final accuracy. Calibration experiments further show that architecture and augmentation choices have a larger effect on final accuracy than the training-rule modifications studied here. Cumulative free-riding is therefore a real and repairable optimization pathology. Nonetheless, for the FF training rules, architectures, and datasets we study, it is not the dominant factor limiting achievable accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes "layer free-riding" in Forward-Forward training, where later layers inherit tasks already solved by earlier layers due to cumulative goodness metrics, and proposes local remedies that dramatically improve layer separation statistics without significant accuracy impact.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the phenomenon of "layer free-riding" in Forward-Forward training where under cumulative-goodness variants, later layers can inherit tasks that earlier layers have already partially solved, leading to exponential decay of class-discrimination gradients reaching deeper layers.
AI.method: The paper formalizes the layer free-riding phenomenon mathematically and studies three local remedies: per-block, hardness-gated, and depth-scaled approaches. These methods recover current-layer separation measures without relying on backpropagated gradients. Experiments are conducted on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny ImageNet datasets.
AI.result: The remedies dramatically improve layer-separation statistics with 4×–45× gains in deeper layers, while changing accuracy by less than one percentage point. Tiny ImageNet reveals the same qualitative gap between layer-health diagnostics and final accuracy. Calibration experiments show architecture and augmentation choices have larger effects on final accuracy than the training-rule modifications.
AI.conclusion: Cumulative free-riding is a real and repairable optimization pathology in Forward-Forward training, but for the studied training rules, architectures, and datasets, it is not the dominant factor limiting achievable accuracy.

title: MASPO: Joint Prompt Optimization for LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based Multi-agent systems (MAS) have shown promise in tackling complex collaborative tasks, where agents are typically orchestrated via role-specific prompts. While the quality of these prompts is pivotal, jointly optimizing them across interacting agents remains a non-trivial challenge, primarily due to the misalignment between local agent objectives and holistic system goals. To address this, we introduce MASPO, a novel framework designed to automatically and iteratively refine prompts across the entire system. A core innovation of MASPO is its joint evaluation mechanism, which assesses prompts not merely by their local validity, but by their capacity to facilitate downstream success for successor agents. This effectively bridges the gap between local interactions and global outcomes without relying on ground-truth labels. Furthermore, MASPO employs a data-driven evolutionary beam search to efficiently navigate the high-dimensional prompt space. Extensive empirical evaluations across 6 diverse tasks demonstrate that MASPO consistently outperforms state-of-the-art prompt optimization methods, achieving an average accuracy improvement of 2.9. We release our code at https://github.com/wangzx1219/MASPO.

AI.tldr: MASPO is a framework for automatically optimizing prompts across multi-agent LLM systems by jointly evaluating prompts based on downstream agent success rather than local validity alone.
AI.motivation: Prompt quality is crucial in LLM-based multi-agent systems, but jointly optimizing prompts across interacting agents is challenging due to misalignment between local agent objectives and holistic system goals.
AI.method: Introduces MASPO framework with joint evaluation mechanism that assesses prompts by their capacity to facilitate downstream success for successor agents, and employs data-driven evolutionary beam search to navigate high-dimensional prompt space.
AI.result: Extensive empirical evaluations across 6 diverse tasks show MASPO consistently outperforms state-of-the-art prompt optimization methods, achieving an average accuracy improvement of 2.9.
AI.conclusion: MASPO effectively bridges the gap between local interactions and global outcomes in multi-agent LLM systems without relying on ground-truth labels, providing a practical solution for prompt optimization in collaborative agent settings.

title: Structure-Preserving Gaussian Processes Via Discrete Euler-Lagrange Equations
summary: In this paper, we propose Lagrangian Gaussian Processes (LGPs) for probabilistic and data-efficient learning of dynamics via discrete forced Euler-Lagrange equations. Importantly, the geometric structure of the Lagrange-d'Alembert principle, which governs the motion of dynamical systems, is preserved by construction in the absence of external forces. This allows learning physically consistent models that overcome erroneous drift in the system's energy, thereby providing stable long-term predictions. At the core of our approach lie linear operators for Gaussian process conditioning, constructed from discrete forced Euler-Lagrange equations and variational discretization schemes. Thereby and unlike prior work, the method enables learning dynamics from discrete position snapshots, i.e., without access to a system's velocities or momenta. This is particularly relevant for a large class of practical scenarios where only position measurements are available, for instance, in motion capture or visual servoing applications. We demonstrate the data-efficiency and generalization capabilities of the LGPs in various synthetic and real-world case studies, including a real-world soft robot with hysteresis. The experimental results underscore that the LGPs learn physically consistent dynamics with uncertainty quantification solely from sparse positional data and enable stable long-term predictions.

AI.tldr: Lagrangian Gaussian Processes (LGPs) enable learning physically consistent dynamics from discrete position data using forced Euler-Lagrange equations with Gaussian process conditioning, preserving geometric structure and preventing energy drift.
AI.motivation: Many practical applications (like motion capture or visual servoing) provide only discrete position measurements without access to velocities or momenta, making existing methods inapplicable. Existing approaches often fail to preserve the geometric structure of dynamics, leading to erroneous energy drift and unstable long-term predictions.
AI.method: LGPs use linear operators for Gaussian process conditioning constructed from discrete forced Euler-Lagrange equations and variational discretization schemes. The approach preserves the geometric structure of the Lagrange-d'Alembert principle by construction when no external forces are present.
AI.result: LGPs demonstrate data-efficiency and generalization capabilities in synthetic and real-world case studies, including a real-world soft robot with hysteresis. They learn physically consistent dynamics with uncertainty quantification solely from sparse positional data and enable stable long-term predictions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LGPs overcome limitations of prior work by enabling learning from discrete position snapshots while preserving physical consistency, preventing energy drift, and providing stable long-term predictions with uncertainty quantification, making them suitable for practical applications where only position measurements are available.

title: Can RL Teach Long-Horizon Reasoning to LLMs? Expressiveness Is Key
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has been applied to improve large language model (LLM) reasoning, yet the systematic study of how training scales with task difficulty has been hampered by the lack of controlled, scalable environments. We introduce ScaleLogic, a synthetic logical reasoning framework that offers independent control over two axes of difficulty: the depth of the required proof planning (i.e., the horizon) and the expressiveness of the underlying logic. Our proposed framework supports a wide range of logics: from simple implication-only logic ("if-then") towards more expressive first-order reasoning with conjunction ("and"), disjunction ("or"), negation ("not"), and universal quantification ("for all"). Using this framework, we show that the RL training compute $T$ follows a power law with respect to reasoning depth $D$ ($T \propto D^γ$, $R^{2} > 0.99$), and that the scaling exponent $γ$ increases monotonically with logical expressiveness, from $1.04$ to $2.60$. On downstream mathematics and general reasoning benchmarks, more expressive training settings yield both larger performance gains (up to $+10.66$ points) and more compute-efficient transfer compared to less expressive settings, demonstrating that what a model is trained on, not just how much it is trained, shapes downstream transfer. We further show that the power-law relationship holds across multiple RL methods, and curriculum-based training substantially improves scaling efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ScaleLogic, a synthetic framework for studying how RL training for LLM reasoning scales with task difficulty, showing power-law scaling with reasoning depth and that training on more expressive logics leads to better downstream transfer.
AI.motivation: There's a lack of controlled, scalable environments to systematically study how reinforcement learning training scales with task difficulty for improving large language model reasoning, which hampers understanding of training efficiency and transfer learning properties.
AI.method: The authors introduce ScaleLogic, a synthetic logical reasoning framework that allows independent control over two axes of difficulty: reasoning depth (horizon) and logical expressiveness. The framework supports various logics from simple implication-only to first-order reasoning with conjunction, disjunction, negation, and universal quantification.
AI.result: 1) RL training compute follows a power law with reasoning depth (T ∝ D^γ, R² > 0.99). 2) The scaling exponent γ increases monotonically with logical expressiveness (from 1.04 to 2.60). 3) More expressive training yields larger performance gains on downstream benchmarks (up to +10.66 points) and more compute-efficient transfer. 4) The power-law relationship holds across multiple RL methods, and curriculum-based training improves scaling efficiency.
AI.conclusion: What a model is trained on (logical expressiveness) shapes downstream transfer, not just how much it is trained, and systematic study reveals power-law scaling relationships that can guide more efficient training for LLM reasoning.

title: The Role of Node Features in Graph Pooling
summary: Graph pooling is commonly applied in graph classification, yet its empirical gains over standard WL-1 expressive GNNs are often marginal or inconsistent. We study this gap by analysing the interaction between node features and graph topology and their effect on pooling objectives. Our analysis reveals that pooling operators require node features that are well-aligned with the graph's topology -- a condition often overlooked and not guaranteed in empirical networks. We formalise fundamental requirements for node features to enable effective pooling, and introduce a quantitative measure of feature quality. Our empirical evaluation shows that, when these requirements are satisfied, pooling can be beneficial and improve performance on appropriate datasets.

AI.tldr: Graph pooling often shows inconsistent benefits because it requires node features to be well-aligned with graph topology, a condition that's often missing in real-world networks.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the empirical observation that graph pooling methods often show marginal or inconsistent performance gains compared to standard WL-1 expressive GNNs in graph classification tasks. There's a need to understand why pooling sometimes works and sometimes doesn't, particularly examining the interaction between node features and graph topology.
AI.method: The authors conduct theoretical analysis to study the interaction between node features and graph topology and their effect on pooling objectives. They formalize fundamental requirements for node features to enable effective pooling and introduce a quantitative measure of feature quality. They then empirically evaluate these requirements on appropriate datasets.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that pooling operators require node features that are well-aligned with the graph's topology - a condition that is often overlooked and not guaranteed in empirical networks. The empirical evaluation shows that when these feature quality requirements are satisfied, pooling can indeed be beneficial and improve performance.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the effectiveness of graph pooling depends critically on having node features that are properly aligned with graph topology. When this alignment condition is met, pooling methods can provide meaningful benefits, explaining the inconsistent empirical performance observed in practice and providing guidance for when to use pooling techniques.

title: GlazyBench: A Benchmark for Ceramic Glaze Property Prediction and Image Generation
summary: Developing ceramic glazes is a costly, time-consuming process of trial and error due to complex chemistry, placing a significant burden on independent artists. While recent advances in multimodal AI offer a modern solution, the field lacks the large-scale datasets required to train these models. We propose GlazyBench, the first dataset for AI-assisted glaze design. Comprising 23,148 real glaze formulations, GlazyBench supports two primary tasks: predicting post-firing surface properties, such as color and transparency, from raw materials, and generating accurate visual representations of the glaze based on these properties. We establish comprehensive baselines for property prediction using traditional machine learning and large language models, alongside image generation benchmarks using deep generative and large multimodal models. Our experiments demonstrate promising yet challenging results. GlazyBench pioneers a new research direction in AI-assisted material design, providing a standardized benchmark for systematic evaluation.

AI.tldr: GlazyBench is the first AI dataset for ceramic glaze design, featuring 23,148 real formulations with tasks for predicting post-firing properties from raw materials and generating visual representations, establishing ML and multimodal baselines.
AI.motivation: Developing ceramic glazes is costly and time-consuming due to complex chemistry, which burdens independent artists. Recent multimodal AI offers solutions but lacks the large-scale datasets needed to train such models.
AI.method: Created GlazyBench dataset with 23,148 real glaze formulations. Established baselines for two tasks: 1) predicting post-firing surface properties (color, transparency) from raw materials using traditional ML and large language models, 2) generating visual representations using deep generative and large multimodal models.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate promising but challenging results. The dataset enables systematic evaluation of AI models for glaze property prediction and visual representation generation.
AI.conclusion: GlazyBench pioneers AI-assisted material design research, providing a standardized benchmark for systematic evaluation and addressing the data gap in ceramic glaze development.

title: The Weight Gram Matrix Captures Sequential Feature Linearization in Deep Networks
summary: Understanding how deep neural networks learn representations remains a central challenge in machine learning theory. In this work, we propose a feature-centric framework for analyzing neural network training by relating weight updates to feature evolution. We introduce a simple identity, the Feature Learning Equation, which identifies the weight Gram matrix as the key object capturing feature dynamics. This enables us to interpret gradient descent as implicitly inducing a hypothetical evolution of features, whose covariance structure - termed the Virtual Covariance - characterizes how representations evolve during training. Building on this perspective, we introduce Target Linearity, a measure quantifying the linear alignment between features and targets. By analyzing the training and layer-wise dynamics, we show that deep networks learn to sequentially transform representations toward target-linear structure. This linearization perspective provides a unified interpretation of several empirical phenomena, including Neural Collapse and linear interpolation in generative models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a feature-centric framework for analyzing neural network training through the Feature Learning Equation and Virtual Covariance, showing that deep networks sequentially transform representations toward linear alignment with targets.
AI.motivation: Understanding how deep neural networks learn representations remains a central challenge in machine learning theory. The paper aims to provide a theoretical framework to analyze feature evolution during training.
AI.method: Proposes a feature-centric framework with a Feature Learning Equation that relates weight updates to feature evolution, identifies weight Gram matrix as key for feature dynamics, introduces Virtual Covariance to characterize representation evolution, and defines Target Linearity measure to quantify linear alignment between features and targets.
AI.result: Shows that gradient descent implicitly induces hypothetical feature evolution, deep networks learn to sequentially transform representations toward target-linear structure, and this linearization perspective provides unified interpretation of empirical phenomena like Neural Collapse and linear interpolation in generative models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed feature-centric framework with Feature Learning Equation and Virtual Covariance provides new theoretical insights into how deep networks learn representations, revealing that networks systematically linearize representations during training and offering unified explanations for several observed empirical phenomena.

title: AI Co-Mathematician: Accelerating Mathematicians with Agentic AI
summary: We introduce the AI co-mathematician, a workbench for mathematicians to interactively leverage AI agents to pursue open-ended research. The AI co-mathematician is optimized to provide holistic support for the exploratory and iterative reality of mathematical workflows, including ideation, literature search, computational exploration, theorem proving and theory building. By providing an asynchronous, stateful workspace that manages uncertainty, refines user intent, tracks failed hypotheses, and outputs native mathematical artifacts, the system mirrors human collaborative workflows. In early tests, the AI co-mathematician helped researchers solve open problems, identify new research directions, and uncover overlooked literature references. Besides demonstrating a highly interactive paradigm for AI-assisted mathematical discovery, the AI co-mathematician also achieves state of the art results on hard problem-solving benchmarks, including scoring 48% on FrontierMath Tier 4, a new high score among all AI systems evaluated.

AI.tldr: AI co-mathematician system provides interactive workspace for mathematicians to leverage AI agents for mathematical research, achieving state-of-the-art results on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To create a system that supports the exploratory and iterative nature of mathematical workflows, addressing the need for holistic AI assistance in mathematical research including ideation, literature search, computational exploration, theorem proving, and theory building.
AI.method: Developed an asynchronous, stateful workspace that manages uncertainty, refines user intent, tracks failed hypotheses, and outputs native mathematical artifacts, mirroring human collaborative workflows with interactive AI agents.
AI.result: In early tests, helped researchers solve open problems, identify new research directions, and uncover overlooked literature references. Achieved state-of-the-art performance with 48% score on FrontierMath Tier 4 benchmark, the highest among all evaluated AI systems.
AI.conclusion: The AI co-mathematician demonstrates a highly interactive paradigm for AI-assisted mathematical discovery that effectively supports real mathematical workflows while achieving top performance on hard problem-solving benchmarks.

title: Trade-off Functions for DP-SGD with Subsampling based on Random Shuffling: Tight Upper and Lower Bounds
summary: We derive a tight analysis of the trade-off function for Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent (DP-SGD) with subsampling based on random shuffling within the $f$-DP framework. Our analysis covers the regime $σ\geq \sqrt{3/\ln M}$, where $σ$ is the noise multiplier and $M$ is the number of rounds within a single epoch. Unlike $f$-DP analyses for Poisson subsampling, which yield non-closed implicit formulas that can be machine computed but are non-transparent, random shuffling admits a tight analysis yielding transparent and interpretable closed-form bounds. Our concrete bounds, derived via the Berry-Esseen theorem, are tight up to constant factors within the proof framework. We demonstrate worked parameter settings for a single epoch ($E=1$) with a corresponding trade-off function $\geq 1-a-δ$, that is, only $δ$ below the ideal random guessing diagonal $1-a$: For $δ= 1/100$ and $σ= 1$, roughly $M \approx 1.14\times 10^6$ rounds and $N \approx 1.14\times 10^7$ training samples suffice to achieve meaningful differential privacy. This is in contrast to recent negative results for the regime $σ\leq 1/\sqrt{2 \ln M}$. Our concrete bounds can be composed over multiple epochs leading to $δ$ having a linear in $E$ dependency, which restricts $E=O(\sqrt{M})$. To go beyond Berry--Esseen, we introduce a new proof technique based on a generalization of the law of large numbers that yields an asymptotic random guessing diagonal-limit result: if $E=c_M^2M$ with $c_M\to 0$, then the $E$-fold composed trade-off function satisfies $f^{\otimes E}(a)\to 1-a$ uniformly in $a\in[0,1]$ with $δ$ having only an $O(\sqrt{E})$ dependency. We compare this asymptotic regime with the corresponding Poisson subsampling asymptotic, and highlight the characterization of explicit convergence rates as an open question.

AI.tldr: The paper provides tight analysis of differential privacy for DP-SGD with random shuffling subsampling within the f-DP framework, showing better privacy bounds than Poisson subsampling and addressing limitations of existing analyses.
AI.motivation: Current f-DP analyses for DP-SGD with Poisson subsampling yield non-transparent, implicit formulas that are hard to interpret. Random shuffling subsampling is a common practical approach that deserves a transparent, interpretable analysis with closed-form bounds.
AI.method: Derives tight analysis of trade-off functions for DP-SGD with random shuffling subsampling using the Berry-Esseen theorem for concrete bounds and introduces a new proof technique based on generalization of law of large numbers for asymptotic results.
AI.result: For random shuffling in regime σ≥√(3/ln M), obtains transparent closed-form bounds tight up to constants; shows M≈1.14×10^6 rounds with N≈1.14×10^7 samples achieves δ=1/100 privacy; asymptotic analysis shows E-fold composition yields δ dependency O(√E) rather than linear.
AI.conclusion: Random shuffling provides superior privacy analysis compared to Poisson subsampling, with interpretable bounds and better composition properties, though explicit convergence rates remain an open question for further research.

title: A Note on TurboQuant and the Earlier DRIVE/EDEN Line of Work
summary:
This note clarifies the relationship between the recent TurboQuant work and the earlier DRIVE (NeurIPS 2021) and EDEN (ICML 2022) schemes. DRIVE is a 1-bit quantizer that EDEN extended to any $b>0$ bits per coordinate; we refer to them collectively as EDEN.
  First, TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$ is a special case of EDEN obtained by fixing EDEN's scalar scale parameter to $S=1$. EDEN supports both biased and unbiased quantization, each optimized by a different $S$ (chosen via methods described in the EDEN works). The fixed choice $S=1$ used by TurboQuant is generally suboptimal, although the optimal $S$ for biased EDEN converges to $1$ as the dimension grows; accordingly TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$ approaches EDEN's behavior for large $d$.
  Second, TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$ combines a biased $(b-1)$-bit EDEN step with an unbiased 1-bit QJL quantization of the residual. It is suboptimal in three ways: (1) its $(b-1)$-bit step uses the suboptimal $S=1$; (2) its 1-bit unbiased residual quantization has worse MSE than (unbiased) 1-bit EDEN; (3) chaining a biased $(b-1)$-bit step with a 1-bit unbiased residual step is inferior to unbiasedly quantizing the input directly with $b$-bit EDEN.
  Third, some of the analysis in the TurboQuant work mirrors that of the EDEN works: both exploit the connection between random rotations and the shifted Beta distribution, use the Lloyd-Max algorithm, and note that Randomized Hadamard Transforms can replace uniform random rotations.
  Experiments support these claims: biased EDEN (with optimized $S$) is more accurate than TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$, and unbiased EDEN is markedly more accurate than TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$, often by more than a bit (e.g., 2-bit EDEN beats 3-bit TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$). We also repeat all accuracy experiments from the TurboQuant paper, showing that EDEN outperforms it in every setup we have tried.

AI.tldr: TurboQuant is a suboptimal special case of the earlier EDEN quantization scheme: TurboQuant_mse uses fixed scaling (S=1) vs EDEN's optimized scaling, and TurboQuant_prod uses inferior residual quantization compared to unbiased EDEN.
AI.motivation: To clarify the relationship between recent TurboQuant quantization work and earlier EDEN/DRIVE schemes, showing that TurboQuant is actually a special case of EDEN with suboptimal parameter choices.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis comparing mathematical formulations: (1) Showing TurboQuant_mse = EDEN with fixed S=1 scaling, (2) Analyzing TurboQuant_prod's composition of biased (b-1)-bit EDEN and 1-bit QJL residual quantization, (3) Identifying shared analysis techniques (random rotations, Beta distributions, Lloyd-Max algorithm, Randomized Hadamard Transforms).
AI.result: Experimental results show: (1) Biased EDEN with optimized S outperforms TurboQuant_mse, (2) Unbiased EDEN substantially outperforms TurboQuant_prod (e.g., 2-bit EDEN beats 3-bit TurboQuant_prod), (3) EDEN outperforms TurboQuant in all tested setups from the TurboQuant paper.
AI.conclusion: TurboQuant is a suboptimal simplification of the more general and powerful EDEN quantization framework, with EDEN consistently providing superior quantization accuracy through optimized scaling parameters and more effective quantization strategies.

title: Beyond Rigid Alignment: Graph Federated Learning via Dual Manifold Calibration
summary: Graph Federated Learning (GFL) enables collaborative representation learning across distributed subgraphs while preserving privacy. However, heterogeneity remains a critical challenge, as subgraphs across clients typically differ significantly in both semantics and structures. Existing methods address heterogeneity by enforcing the rigid alignment of model parameters or prototypes between clients and the server. However, these alignments implicitly rely on a restrictive global linearity assumption that summarizes local data distributions using a single and globally consistent representation space. This severely compresses the personalized representation space of clients and fails to preserve diverse local graph distributions. To overcome these limitations, we propose Federated Graph Manifold Calibration (FedGMC), a novel paradigm that tackles semantic heterogeneity and structural heterogeneity from a unified manifold perspective. Instead of enforcing rigid alignment, FedGMC introduces a dual manifold calibration mechanism that preserves global commonalities while maximizing the personalized representation space of local clients. Specifically, for semantic heterogeneity, the server constructs a geometrically optimal semantic manifold via equidistant semantic anchors, so as to guide the calibration of local semantic manifolds. For structural heterogeneity, the server constructs a global structural manifold by building global structural templates, so as to guide the calibration of local structural manifolds. Finally, the server dynamically refines both global semantic manifolds and structural manifolds by aggregating local manifolds. Extensive experiments on eleven homophilic and heterophilic graphs demonstrate that FedGMC effectively balances global commonality and local personalization, thereby significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baseline methods.

AI.tldr: FedGMC addresses graph heterogeneity in federated learning through a dual manifold calibration approach that preserves global commonalities while maximizing personalized local representation spaces, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing GFL methods rely on rigid alignment of model parameters/prototypes under a restrictive global linearity assumption, which compresses personalized representation spaces and fails to preserve diverse local graph distributions.
AI.method: FedGMC introduces a dual manifold calibration mechanism: (1) For semantic heterogeneity, server constructs geometrically optimal semantic manifold via equidistant semantic anchors to calibrate local semantic manifolds; (2) For structural heterogeneity, server builds global structural manifold via structural templates to calibrate local structural manifolds; (3) Server dynamically refines both global manifolds by aggregating local manifolds.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on eleven homophilic and heterophilic graphs demonstrate FedGMC effectively balances global commonality and local personalization, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: FedGMC provides a novel manifold perspective to tackle graph heterogeneity in federated learning, enabling collaborative representation learning while preserving personalized representation spaces and local graph distributions.

title: Inference-Time Refinement Closes the Synthetic-Real Gap in Tabular Diffusion
summary: Diffusion-based generators set the current state of the art for synthetic tabular data. These methods approach but rarely exceed real-data utility, and closing this synthetic-real gap has so far been pursued exclusively at training time, via architectural advances, scaling, and retraining of monolithic generators. The inference-time alternative, i.e., refining the outputs of a pre-trained backbone with parameters left untouched, has remained largely unexplored for tabular synthesis. We introduce TARDIS (Tabular generation through Refinement, Distillation, and Inference-time Sampling), an inference-time refinement framework that operates on a frozen pre-trained backbone, configured per dataset by a Tree-structured Parzen Estimator search over score-level guidance during reverse diffusion, with each trial's objective set by an inner grid search over post-hoc sample selectors and an optional soft-label distillation step. The search space encodes a single mathematical pattern we name Bidirectional Chamfer Refinement (BCR): the symmetric Chamfer functional between synthetic and real samples is minimized both continuously, via a score-level gradient, and discretely, via batch-ranking post-generation. The per-dataset search recovers BCR-aligned configurations on most datasets, evidence for BCR as the dominant refinement pattern. Across 15 binary, multiclass, and regression benchmarks TARDIS achieves a median +8.6% downstream-task improvement over models trained on real data (95% CI [+3.3, +16.4], Wilcoxon p=0.016, 11/15 strict wins) and improves over the TabDiff backbone on all 15 datasets (mean +12.9%, p<10^-4), matching the backbone on manifold fidelity, diversity, and sample-level privacy. Inference-time refinement of a pre-trained tabular diffusion backbone reaches and exceeds real-data utility in 1 to 80 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU.

AI.tldr: TARDIS is an inference-time refinement framework for tabular diffusion models that improves synthetic data quality by 8.6% over real data using score-level guidance and sample selection, without retraining the backbone model.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion-based tabular generators rarely exceed real-data utility, and improvements have focused on training-time modifications. Inference-time refinement of pre-trained models remains unexplored for tabular synthesis, creating an opportunity to close the synthetic-real gap without retraining.
AI.method: Uses a frozen pre-trained diffusion backbone with Tree-structured Parzen Estimator search over score-level guidance during reverse diffusion. Features Bidirectional Chamfer Refinement (BCR) pattern minimizing synthetic-real distance both continuously via gradient guidance and discretely via batch-ranking selection. Includes optional soft-label distillation and automated configuration search per dataset.
AI.result: Achieves median +8.6% downstream-task improvement over real data across 15 benchmarks (11/15 strict wins), improves over TabDiff backbone on all datasets (mean +12.9%). Matches backbone on manifold fidelity, diversity, and privacy. Runs in 1-80 minutes on consumer GPU.
AI.conclusion: Inference-time refinement of pre-trained diffusion models can exceed real-data utility for tabular synthesis, establishing BCR as a dominant refinement pattern and enabling efficient improvement without architectural changes or retraining.

title: Can Attribution Predict Risk? From Multi-View Attribution to Planning Risk Signals in End-to-End Autonomous Driving
summary: End-to-end autonomous driving models generate future trajectories from multi-view inputs, improving system integration but introducing opaque decisions and hard-to-localize risks. Existing methods either rely on auxiliary monitoring models or generate textual explanations, but are decoupled from the planning process and fail to reveal the visual evidence underlying trajectory generation. While attribution offers a direct alternative, planning differs from image classification by taking six-view camera images as input and predicting continuous multi-step trajectories, requiring attribution to capture both critical views and regions and their influence on outputs. Moreover, whether attribution maps can support risk identification remains underexplored. To address this, we propose a hierarchical attribution framework for end-to-end planning. Specifically, using L2 consistency with the original trajectory as the objective, we design a coarse-to-fine region attribution strategy that searches candidate regions across the full six-view input and refines attribution within them. We further extract three attribution statistics as predictive signals for planning risk, including attribution entropy to measure how concentrated the planner's reliance is over the joint visual space, within-camera spatial variance to characterize how spread out the attribution is within each view, and cross-camera Gini coefficient to quantify how unevenly attribution is distributed across the six cameras.
