They conduct 12 controlled experiments (8 vision, 4 language) totaling over 11,000 training epochs across multiple datasets to validate E's predictive power.
AI.result: E ≥ 0.5 alone guarantees zero dead experts across all experiments, eliminating the need for handcrafted load-balancing losses. The paper reveals six additional findings: dead experts can resuscitate, ortho toxicity is dataset-dependent, task complexity shifts the critical E threshold, model overfitting decouples from expert health, three-tier MoEs collapse into two-tier structures, and ecological structure is temperature-invariant across 50x range.
AI.conclusion: E serves as a unified diagnostic tool for MoE training, analogous to the Reynolds number in fluid dynamics, providing a simple predictive metric for expert ecological health across modalities and architectures.

title: Federated Cross-Client Subgraph Pattern Detection
summary: Subgraph pattern detection aims to uncover complex interaction structures in graphs. However, state-of-the-art graph neural network (GNN)-based solutions assume centralized access to the entire graph. When graphs are instead distributed across multiple parties, client-local GNN computations diverge from those of a centralized model, resulting in a representation-equivalence gap. We formalize this as a structural observability problem, where subgraph patterns crossing partition boundaries become locally unidentifiable. To bridge this gap, we propose a per-step, layer-wise embedding exchange framework in which clients synchronize intermediate node representations at each layer of the forward pass, without exposing raw features or labels. Under an extended-subgraph assumption and shared model parameters across clients, this framework recovers the same node representations as a centralized GNN over the full graph. Experiments on synthetic directed multigraphs with cycles, bicliques, and scatter-gather patterns show that embedding exchange and federated parameter aggregation are complementary rather than interchangeable: their combination recovers most of the representation gap, provided exchanged embeddings are fresh per-step rather than stale per-epoch.

AI.tldr: A framework for distributed GNNs that uses per-step, layer-wise embedding exchange to achieve representation equivalence with centralized GNNs across partitioned graphs.
AI.motivation: Current GNN-based subgraph pattern detection methods assume centralized graph access, but distributed graphs across multiple parties create representation-equivalence gaps where subgraph patterns crossing partition boundaries become locally unidentifiable.
AI.method: Propose a per-step, layer-wise embedding exchange framework where clients synchronize intermediate node representations at each layer without exposing raw features/labels, combined with federated parameter aggregation under extended-subgraph assumption and shared model parameters.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic directed multigraphs with cycles, bicliques, and scatter-gather patterns show embedding exchange and federated aggregation are complementary, not interchangeable; their combination recovers most representation gap when using fresh per-step embeddings rather than stale per-epoch ones.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully bridges the representation-equivalence gap in distributed GNNs through coordinated embedding exchange, enabling subgraph pattern detection across partitioned graphs while maintaining privacy by not exposing raw data.

title: Hyperbolic Concept Bottleneck Models
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) have become a popular approach to enable interpretability in neural networks by constraining classifier inputs to a set of human-understandable concepts. While effective, current models embed concepts in flat Euclidean space, treating them as independent, orthogonal dimensions. Concepts, however, are highly structured and organized in semantic hierarchies. To resolve this mismatch, we propose Hyperbolic Concept Bottleneck Models (HypCBM), a post-hoc framework that grounds the bottleneck in this structure by reformulating concept activation as asymmetric geometric containment in hyperbolic space. Rather than treating entailment cones as a pre-training penalty, we show they encode a natural test-time activation signal: the margin of inclusion within a concept's entailment cone yields sparse, hierarchy-aware activations without any additional supervision or learned modules. We further introduce an adaptive scaling law for hierarchically faithful interventions, propagating user corrections coherently through the concept tree. Empirically, HypCBM rivals post-hoc Euclidean models trained on 20$\times$ more data in sparse regimes required for human interpretability, with stronger hierarchical consistency and improved robustness to input corruptions.

AI.tldr: Hyperbolic Concept Bottleneck Models (HypCBM) improve interpretability by embedding concepts in hyperbolic space instead of flat Euclidean space, allowing for better representation of hierarchical semantic relationships between concepts.
AI.motivation: Current Concept Bottleneck Models embed concepts in flat Euclidean space, treating them as independent dimensions. However, concepts are actually highly structured and organized in semantic hierarchies, creating a mismatch between model representation and real-world concept organization.
AI.method: Proposes HypCBM, a post-hoc framework that grounds the concept bottleneck in hyperbolic space. It reformulates concept activation as asymmetric geometric containment using entailment cones in hyperbolic space. The method uses entailment cones as test-time activation signals rather than pre-training penalties, and introduces adaptive scaling for hierarchically faithful interventions.
AI.result: HypCBM rivals post-hoc Euclidean models trained on 20× more data in sparse interpretability regimes. It shows stronger hierarchical consistency and improved robustness to input corruptions compared to traditional Euclidean approaches.
AI.conclusion: Embedding concepts in hyperbolic space better captures their inherent hierarchical structure, leading to more interpretable, hierarchy-aware models with sparse activations and coherent intervention propagation without additional supervision.

title: FedFrozen: Two-Stage Federated Optimization via Attention Kernel Freezing
summary: Federated learning with heterogeneous clients remains a significant challenge for deep learning, primarily due to client drift arising from inconsistent local updates. Existing federated optimization methods typically address this issue through objective-level regularization or update-correction mechanisms. Recent studies, however, suggest that Transformer-based architectures may be inherently more robust than conventional models under heterogeneous federated training. Motivated by this observation, we investigate how different parameter components within the attention mechanism influence federated optimization. Specifically, we decompose the attention module into a query/key block, which determines the attention kernel, and a value block, which performs semantic transformation under the induced kernel. Based on this perspective, we propose FedFrozen, a two-stage federated optimization framework that first performs full-model warm-up training and then freezes the query/key block while continuing to optimize the value block. Under a linear-attention formulation, we show that the warm-up stage can be interpreted as an inexact descent procedure on a regularized kernel-profile objective, while the frozen stage reduces to a restricted value-block optimization problem under a fixed attention kernel. Our analysis further reveals an explicit trade-off that governs the choice of warm-up length. Simulations validate the predicted bias-drift behavior, and real-data experiments demonstrate that FedFrozen improves both the stability and effectiveness of Transformer models in heterogeneous federated learning.

AI.tldr: FedFrozen is a two-stage federated learning framework for Transformers that first warms up the full model, then freezes the attention kernel-forming query/key block while continuing to optimize the value block, improving stability and effectiveness in heterogeneous settings.
AI.motivation: Transformer architectures show inherent robustness in heterogeneous federated learning compared to conventional models, motivating investigation into how different attention parameter components influence federated optimization. The core problem is client drift from inconsistent local updates in federated learning with heterogeneous clients.
AI.method: FedFrozen decomposes attention into query/key block (determines attention kernel) and value block (performs semantic transformation). The framework has two stages: 1) Full-model warm-up training, 2) Freeze query/key block while continuing to optimize value block. The method uses a linear-attention formulation to analyze optimization dynamics.
AI.result: The warm-up stage is interpreted as inexact descent on a regularized kernel-profile objective, while the frozen stage reduces to restricted value-block optimization under fixed attention kernel. Analysis reveals explicit trade-off governing warm-up length choice. Simulations validate predicted bias-drift behavior, and real-data experiments show FedFrozen improves Transformer stability and effectiveness in heterogeneous federated learning.
AI.conclusion: By strategically freezing attention kernel components while continuing to optimize semantic transformation components, FedFrozen addresses client drift in heterogeneous federated learning for Transformers, offering improved optimization stability and performance compared to existing methods that use objective-level regularization or update-correction mechanisms.

title: Scene-Adaptive Continual Learning for CSI-based Human Activity Recognition with Mixture of Experts
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures, 3 tables, this article was submitted to IEEE for possible publication
summary: Channel state information (CSI)-based human activity recognition (HAR) is vulnerable to performance degradation under domain shifts across varying physical environments. Continual learning (CL) offers a principled way to learn new domains sequentially while preserving past knowledge, but existing CL solutions for CSI-based HAR scale poorly with accumulating domains, rely on a large replay buffer, or incur linearly growing inference cost. In this letter, we propose Scene-Adaptive Mixture of Experts with Clustered Specialists (SAMoE-C), which formulates cross-domain CSI-based HAR as a mixture-of-experts system that enables scene-specific adaptation, via an attention-based semantic router that activates only selected experts for each input. Moreover, we develop a novel training protocol, which requires only a tiny replay buffer for stabilizing domain discrimination of the router. Experimental results on a four-scene CSI dataset demonstrate that SAMoE-C approaches the state-of-the-art accuracy, while maintaining a significantly lower inference cost. By jointly combining modular experts, selective activation with router and a lightweight training protocol, SAMoE-C enables scalable cross-domain CSI-based HAR deployment with low training overhead and high computational efficiency in real-world settings.

AI.tldr: SAMoE-C is a novel continual learning approach for CSI-based human activity recognition that uses a mixture-of-experts system with a semantic router to enable scalable cross-domain adaptation while minimizing computational costs.
AI.motivation: CSI-based human activity recognition suffers from performance degradation when deployed across different physical environments (domain shifts). Existing continual learning solutions for this problem scale poorly, require large replay buffers, or have linearly growing inference costs as domains accumulate.
AI.method: The authors propose SAMoE-C (Scene-Adaptive Mixture of Experts with Clustered Specialists), which formulates cross-domain CSI-based HAR as a mixture-of-experts system. Key components include: 1) An attention-based semantic router that selectively activates only relevant experts for each input, 2) A novel training protocol that requires only a tiny replay buffer to stabilize domain discrimination of the router, 3) Clustered specialists that enable scene-specific adaptation.
AI.result: Experimental results on a four-scene CSI dataset show that SAMoE-C approaches state-of-the-art accuracy while maintaining significantly lower inference costs compared to existing methods. The approach enables scalable cross-domain HAR deployment with low training overhead and high computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: SAMoE-C effectively addresses the scalability challenges of continual learning for CSI-based HAR by combining modular experts, selective activation through a router, and lightweight training. This enables practical deployment in real-world settings where domain adaptation is required across multiple environments.

title: ORTHOBO: Orthogonal Bayesian Hyperparameter Optimization
summary: Bayesian optimization is widely used for hyperparameter optimization when model evaluations are expensive; however, noisy acquisition estimates can lead to unstable decisions. We identify acquisition estimation noise as a failure mode that was previously overlooked: even when the surrogate model and acquisition target are correctly specified, finite-sample Monte Carlo error can perturb acquisition values. This can, in turn, flip candidate rankings and lead to suboptimal BO decisions. As a remedy, we aim at variance reduction and propose an orthogonal acquisition estimator that subtracts an optimally weighted score-function control variate, which yields an acquisition residual orthogonal to posterior score directions and which thus reduces Monte Carlo variance. We further introduce OrthoBO: a Bayesian optimization framework that combines our orthogonal acquisition estimator with ensemble surrogates and an outer log transformation. We show theoretically that our estimator preserves the target, leads to variance reduction, and improves pairwise ranking stability. We further verify the theoretical properties of OrthoBO through numerical experiments where our framework reduces acquisition estimation variance, stabilizes candidate rankings, and achieves strong performance. We also demonstrate the downstream utility of OrthoBO in hyperparameter optimization for neural network training and fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: OrthoBO: Bayesian optimization with orthogonal acquisition estimators reduces Monte Carlo noise in acquisition function estimation, improving ranking stability and performance in expensive hyperparameter optimization tasks.
AI.motivation: Bayesian optimization suffers from acquisition estimation noise due to finite-sample Monte Carlo error, which can flip candidate rankings and lead to suboptimal decisions, even with correctly specified models.
AI.method: Proposes an orthogonal acquisition estimator that subtracts an optimally weighted score-function control variate, creating an acquisition residual orthogonal to posterior score directions. Combined with ensemble surrogates and outer log transformation in the OrthoBO framework.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows estimator preserves target, reduces variance, and improves pairwise ranking stability. Numerical experiments confirm reduced acquisition estimation variance, stabilized candidate rankings, and strong performance in hyperparameter optimization for neural networks.
AI.conclusion: OrthoBO addresses the overlooked problem of acquisition estimation noise in Bayesian optimization, providing a theoretically grounded framework that improves decision stability and performance through variance reduction and ranking stabilization.

title: Invariant Features in Language Models: Geometric Characterization and Model Attribution
summary: Language models exhibit strong robustness to paraphrasing, suggesting that semantic information may be encoded through stable internal representations, yet the structure and origin of such invariance remain unclear. We propose a local geometric framework in which semantically equivalent inputs occupy structured regions in latent space, with paraphrastic variation along nuisance directions and semantic identity preserved in invariant subspaces. Building on this view, we make three contributions: (1) a geometric characterization of invariant latent features, (2) a contrastive subspace discovery method that separates semantic-changing from semantic-preserving variation, and (3) an application of invariant representations to zero-shot model attribution. Across models and layers, empirical results support these contributions. Invariant structure emerges in specific depth regions, semantic displacement lies largely outside the nuisance subspace, and representation-level interventions indicate a causal role of invariant components in model outputs. Invariant representations also capture model-specific geometric patterns, enabling accurate attribution. These findings suggest that semantic invariance can be viewed as a local geometric property of latent representations, offering a principled perspective on how language models organize meaning.

AI.tldr: Language models encode semantic meaning through geometrically structured invariant representations in latent space, with paraphrastic variations separated along nuisance directions and semantic identity preserved in invariant subspaces.
AI.motivation: While language models show strong robustness to paraphrasing, indicating stable semantic representations, the underlying structure and origin of this invariance remain unclear. The authors seek to understand how semantic information is geometrically organized in latent space.
AI.method: The authors propose a local geometric framework where semantically equivalent inputs form structured regions in latent space. They develop: (1) geometric characterization of invariant latent features, (2) contrastive subspace discovery method separating semantic-changing from semantic-preserving variation, and (3) application of invariant representations to zero-shot model attribution.
AI.result: Empirical results across models and layers show: invariant structure emerges in specific depth regions; semantic displacement occurs largely outside the nuisance subspace; representation-level interventions demonstrate causal role of invariant components; invariant representations capture model-specific geometric patterns enabling accurate model attribution.
AI.conclusion: Semantic invariance can be viewed as a local geometric property of latent representations, providing a principled perspective on how language models organize meaning through structured invariant subspaces that separate semantic identity from paraphrastic variation.

title: MINER: Mining Multimodal Internal Representation for Efficient Retrieval
summary: Visual document retrieval has become essential for accessing information in visually rich documents. Existing approaches fall into two camps. Late-interaction retrievers achieve strong quality through fine-grained token-level matching but store hundreds of vectors per page, incurring large index footprints and high serving costs. By contrast, dense single-vector retrievers retain storage and latency advantages but consistently lag in quality because they compress all information into a single final-layer embedding. In this work, we first conduct a layerwise diagnostic on single-vector retrievers, revealing that retrieval-relevant signal resides in internal representations. Motivated by these findings, we propose MINER (Mining Multimodal Internal RepreseNtation for Efficient Retrieval), a lightweight plug-in module that probes and fuses internal signals across transformer layers into a single compact embedding without modifying the backbone or sacrificing single-vector efficiency. The first Retrieval-Aligned Layer Probing stage attaches a lightweight probe at each layer, surfacing which dimensions carry retrieval-relevant information. The subsequent Adaptive Sparse Multi-Layer Fusion stage applies performance-adaptive neuron-level masking to the selected layers and fuses the surviving signals into the final dense vector. Across ViDoRe V1/V2/V3, MINER outperforms existing dense single-vector retrievers on the majority of benchmarks, with up to 4.5% nDCG@5 improvement over its corresponding backbone. Compared to strong late-interaction baselines, in some settings MINER substantially narrows the nDCG@$5$ gap to $0.2$ while preserving the storage and serving advantages of dense retrieval.

AI.tldr: MINER is a lightweight plug-in module that probes and fuses internal transformer layer signals into a single compact embedding for efficient visual document retrieval, bridging the quality gap between dense and late-interaction retrievers while maintaining storage/latency advantages.
AI.motivation: Existing visual document retrieval approaches have trade-offs: late-interaction retrievers achieve high quality but require large index footprints and high serving costs due to storing hundreds of vectors per page, while dense single-vector retrievers are storage/latency efficient but consistently lag in quality by compressing all information into a single final-layer embedding.
AI.method: MINER consists of two stages: 1) Retrieval-Aligned Layer Probing attaches lightweight probes at each transformer layer to identify which dimensions carry retrieval-relevant information, and 2) Adaptive Sparse Multi-Layer Fusion applies performance-adaptive neuron-level masking to selected layers and fuses surviving signals into the final dense vector, all without modifying the backbone or sacrificing single-vector efficiency.
AI.result: MINER outperforms existing dense single-vector retrievers on most ViDoRe V1/V2/V3 benchmarks with up to 4.5% nDCG@5 improvement. Compared to late-interaction baselines, it substantially narrows the nDCG@5 gap to 0.2 in some settings while preserving storage and serving advantages of dense retrieval.
AI.conclusion: MINER successfully bridges the quality-efficiency gap in visual document retrieval by mining internal transformer representations, offering a practical solution that combines near-late-interaction quality with dense retrieval efficiency through its plug-in architecture.

title: Invariant-Based Diagnostics for Graph Benchmarks
summary: Progress on graph foundation models is hindered by benchmark practices that conflate the contributions of node features and graph structure, making it hard to tell whether a model actually learns from connectivity, or whether it even needs to. We propose addressing this using graph invariants, i.e., permutation-invariant, task-agnostic structural descriptors that serve as a diagnostic framework for graph benchmarks. We show that (i) invariants are more expressive than standard GNNs, (ii) invariants characterize structural heterogeneity within and across benchmark datasets, (iii) invariants predict multi-task performance, and (iv) simple invariant-based models are competitive with, and sometimes exceed, transformer and message-passing baselines across 26 datasets. Our results suggest that expressivity is not the main driver of predictive performance, and that on tasks where structure matters, a non-trainable structural proxy often matches trained message-passing models. We thus posit that invariant baselines should become a standard for evaluating whether structure is required for a task and whether a model picks up on it, serving as a stepping stone towards graph foundation models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces graph invariants as diagnostic tools to evaluate whether graph models actually learn from connectivity or just rely on node features, showing these invariants can match or exceed traditional GNN performance.
AI.motivation: Current graph benchmark practices make it difficult to determine whether models actually learn from graph structure or just rely on node features, hindering progress on graph foundation models. There's a need to separate the contributions of structure and features in model evaluation.
AI.method: The authors propose using graph invariants - permutation-invariant, task-agnostic structural descriptors - as a diagnostic framework. They evaluate invariants across 26 datasets, comparing them with transformer and message-passing baselines, and analyze how well invariants characterize structural heterogeneity and predict performance.
AI.result: Graph invariants are more expressive than standard GNNs, characterize structural heterogeneity within and across datasets, predict multi-task performance, and simple invariant-based models are competitive with or sometimes exceed transformer and message-passing baselines across 26 datasets.
AI.conclusion: Expressivity is not the main driver of predictive performance in graph models. On tasks where structure matters, non-trainable structural proxies often match trained message-passing models. The authors propose invariant baselines should become standard for evaluating whether structure is required for a task and whether models actually learn from it.

title: Diversity Curves for Graph Representation Learning
summary: Graph-level representations are crucial tools for characterising structural differences between graphs. However, comparing graphs with different cardinalities, even when sampled from the same underlying distribution, remains challenging. Unsupervised tasks in particular require interpretable, scalable, and reliable size-aware graph representations. Our work addresses these issues by tracking the structural diversity of a graph across coarsening levels. The resulting graph embeddings, which we denote diversity curves, are interpretable by construction, efficient, and directly comparable across coarsening hierarchies. Specifically, we track the spread of graphs, a novel isometry invariant that is inherently well-suited for encoding the metric diversity and geometry of graphs. We utilise edge contraction coarsening and prove that this improves expressivity, thus leading to more powerful graph-level representations than structural descriptors alone. Demonstrating their utility over a range of baseline methods in practice, we use diversity curves to (i) cluster and visualise simulated graphs across varying sizes, (ii) distinguish the geometry of single-cell graphs, (iii) compare the structure of molecular graph datasets, and (iv) characterise geometric shapes.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "diversity curves" - interpretable, scalable graph embeddings that track structural diversity across coarsening levels using the novel "spread of graphs" metric, enabling comparison of graphs with different sizes.
AI.motivation: Graph comparison remains challenging for graphs with different cardinalities, even from the same distribution. Unsupervised tasks need interpretable, scalable, and reliable size-aware graph representations that can handle graphs of varying sizes.
AI.method: Proposes tracking structural diversity of graphs across coarsening levels using "diversity curves." Uses edge contraction coarsening and introduces "spread of graphs" - a novel isometry invariant that encodes metric diversity and geometry. Proves this approach improves expressivity over structural descriptors alone.
AI.result: Diversity curves outperform baseline methods in several applications: (i) clustering and visualizing simulated graphs of varying sizes, (ii) distinguishing geometry of single-cell graphs, (iii) comparing molecular graph dataset structures, and (iv) characterizing geometric shapes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed diversity curves provide interpretable, efficient, and comparable graph embeddings that address the challenge of comparing graphs with different cardinalities, demonstrating practical utility across diverse applications from biology to geometry.

title: No Triangulation Without Representation: Generalization in Topological Deep Learning
summary: Despite an ever-increasing interest in topological deep learning models that target higher-order datasets, there is no consensus on how to evaluate such models. This is exacerbated by the fact that topological objects permit operations, such as structural refinements, that are not appropriate for graph data. In this work, we extend MANTRA, a benchmark dataset containing manifold triangulations, to a larger class of manifolds with more diverse homeomorphism types. We show that, unlike prior claims, both graph neural networks (GNNs) and higher-order message passing (HOMP) methods can saturate the benchmark. However, we find that this is contingent on the right representation and feature assignment, emphasizing their importance in baseline models. We thus provide a novel evaluation protocol based on representational diversity and triangulation refinement. Surprisingly, we find no indication that existing models are capable of generalizing beyond the combinatorial structure of the data. This points towards a research gap in developing models that understand topological structure independent of scale. Our work thus provides the necessary scaffolding to evaluate future models and enable the development of topology-aware inductive biases.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a benchmark for evaluating topological deep learning models, showing both GNNs and HOMP methods can achieve high performance but only with proper representation and features, and reveals these models struggle to generalize beyond combinatorial structure.
AI.motivation: There is no consensus on how to evaluate topological deep learning models that target higher-order datasets, exacerbated by topological operations (like structural refinements) not being appropriate for graph data.
AI.method: Extends MANTRA benchmark to more diverse manifolds, evaluates both graph neural networks (GNNs) and higher-order message passing (HOMP) methods, introduces evaluation protocol based on representational diversity and triangulation refinement.
AI.result: Both GNNs and HOMP methods can saturate the benchmark but only with proper representation and feature assignment. No existing models generalize beyond combinatorial structure of data. New evaluation protocol provides scaffolding for future model development.
AI.conclusion: There is a research gap in developing models that understand topological structure independent of scale. The work provides necessary scaffolding to evaluate future models and enable topology-aware inductive biases.

title: Hitting Time Isomorphism for Multi-Stage Planning with Foundation Policies
summary: We present a new operator-theoretic representation learning framework for offline reinforcement learning that recovers the directed temporal geometry of a controlled Markov process from hitting time observations. While prior art often produces symmetric distances or fails to satisfy the triangle inequality, our framework learns a Hilbert-space displacement geometry where expected hitting times are realized as linear functionals of latent displacements. We prove that this representation exists under latent linear closure and is uniquely identifiable up to a bounded linear isomorphism. For finite-dimensional implementations, we show that global hitting-time error is bounded by one-step transition error amplified by the environment's transient spectral radius. Furthermore, we provide finite-sample guarantees accounting for approximation, statistical complexity, and trajectory-label mismatch. Derived from this theory, we curate Isomorphic Embedding Learning (IEL) as a new goal-agnostic foundation policy learning algorithm that anchors a HILP-style consistency objective with explicit hitting-time regression to ensure that the learned geometry reflects actual decision-time progress. This asymmetric and compositional structure enables robust graph-based multi-stage planning for long-horizon navigation. Our experiments demonstrate that IEL improves the state of the art of learning foundation policy policies from offline maze locomotion data. Our code can be found on https://github.com/MagnusBoock/IEL

AI.tldr: A new operator-theoretic representation learning framework for offline RL that learns asymmetric directed temporal geometry from hitting times, enabling robust long-horizon planning.
AI.motivation: Prior approaches in representation learning for offline RL often produce symmetric distances or fail to satisfy triangle inequality, which limits their ability to capture the directed temporal geometry of controlled Markov processes needed for effective planning.
AI.method: Proposes Isomorphic Embedding Learning (IEL) - a goal-agnostic foundation policy learning algorithm that learns Hilbert-space displacement geometry where hitting times are realized as linear functionals of latent displacements. Uses HILP-style consistency objective with explicit hitting-time regression.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees: representation exists under latent linear closure and is uniquely identifiable up to bounded linear isomorphism; finite-dimensional implementations bound global hitting-time error by one-step transition error amplified by environment's transient spectral radius. Experimental results show IEL improves state-of-the-art for learning foundation policies from offline maze locomotion data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed operator-theoretic framework successfully recovers the directed temporal geometry of controlled Markov processes, providing a foundation for robust graph-based multi-stage planning in long-horizon navigation tasks.

title: Efficient Serving for Dynamic Agent Workflows with Prediction-based KV-Cache Management
summary: LLM-based workflows compose specialized agents to execute complex tasks, and these agents usually share substantial context, allowing KV-Cache reuse to save computation. Existing approaches either manage KV-Cache at agent level and fail to exploit the reuse opportunities within workflows, or manage cache at the workflow level but assume that each workflow calls a static sequence of agents. However, practical workflows are typically dynamic, where the sequence of invoked agents and thus induced cache reuse opportunities depend on the context of each task. To serve such dynamic workflows efficiently, we build a system dubbed PBKV (\textbf{P}rediction-\textbf{B}ased \textbf{KV}-Cache Management). For each workflow, PBKV predicts the agent invocations in several future steps by fusing the guidance from historical workflows and context of the target workflow. Based on the predictions, PBKV estimates the reuse potential of cache entries and keeps the high-potential entries in GPU memory. To be robust to prediction errors, PBKV utilizes the predictions conservatively during both cache eviction and prefetching. Experiments on three workflow benchmarks show that PBKV achieves up to $1.85\times$ speedup over LRU on dynamic workflows, and up to $1.26\times$ speedup over the SOTA baseline KVFlow on the static workflow.

AI.tldr: PBKV is a system for managing KV-Cache in dynamic LLM-based workflows by predicting future agent invocations to optimize cache reuse, achieving significant speedups over existing approaches.
AI.motivation: Current KV-Cache management approaches are inefficient for dynamic LLM-based workflows. Agent-level approaches miss reuse opportunities within workflows, while workflow-level approaches assume static agent sequences. Real-world workflows are dynamic where agent sequences and cache reuse opportunities depend on task context.
AI.method: PBKV predicts future agent invocations by fusing historical workflow patterns with current workflow context. It estimates cache entry reuse potential to keep high-potential entries in GPU memory, and uses conservative prediction strategies during both cache eviction and prefetching to handle prediction errors robustly.
AI.result: Experiments on three workflow benchmarks show PBKV achieves up to 1.85× speedup over LRU on dynamic workflows, and up to 1.26× speedup over state-of-the-art baseline KVFlow on static workflows.
AI.conclusion: PBKV effectively addresses the challenge of KV-Cache management in dynamic LLM-based workflows through context-aware prediction of future agent invocations, enabling efficient cache reuse and significant performance improvements over existing approaches.

title: Q-MMR: Off-Policy Evaluation via Recursive Reweighting and Moment Matching
summary: We present a novel theoretical framework, Q-MMR, for off-policy evaluation in finite-horizon MDPs. Q-MMR learns a set of scalar weights, one for each data point, such that the reweighted rewards approximate the expected return under the target policy. The weights are learned inductively in a top-down manner via a moment matching objective against a value-function discriminator class. Notably, and perhaps surprisingly, a data-dependent finite-sample guarantee for general function approximation can be established under only the realizability of $Q^π$, with a dimension-free bound -- that is, the error does not depend on the statistical complexity of the function class. We also establish connections to several existing methods, such as importance sampling and linear FQE. Further theoretical analyses shed new light on the nature of coverage, a concept of fundamental importance to offline RL.

AI.tldr: Q-MMR is a novel off-policy evaluation method that learns data point weights to match value function moments, achieving dimension-free finite-sample guarantees under only Q-function realizability.
AI.motivation: Existing off-policy evaluation methods often face statistical complexity dependencies or require strong coverage assumptions. There's a need for methods with better theoretical guarantees, particularly dimension-free bounds that don't depend on the function class complexity.
AI.method: Q-MMR learns scalar weights for each data point in a top-down inductive manner through a moment matching objective. The weights are optimized to make the reweighted empirical rewards approximate the target policy's expected return using a value-function discriminator class.
AI.result: The method achieves finite-sample guarantees for general function approximation with dimension-free bounds, requiring only Q-function realizability. Connections are established to importance sampling and linear FQE, and theoretical analysis provides new insights into coverage concepts in offline RL.
AI.conclusion: Q-MMR provides a theoretically sound off-policy evaluation framework with strong dimension-free guarantees under minimal assumptions, advancing understanding of coverage and bridging connections to existing methods in offline reinforcement learning.

title: Operator-Guided Invariance Learning for Continuous Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) with continuous time and state/action spaces is often data-intensive and brittle under nuisance variability and shift, motivating methods that exploit value-preserving structures to stabilize and improve learning. Most existing approaches focus on special cases, such as prescribed symmetries and exact equivariance, without addressing how to discover more general structures that require nonlinear operators to transform and map between continuous state/action systems with isomorphic value functions. We propose \textbf{VPSD-RL} (Value-Preserving Structure Discovery for Reinforcement Learning). It models continuous RL as a controlled diffusion with value-preserving mappings defined through Lie-group actions and associated pullback operators. We show that a value-preserving structure exists exactly when pulling back the value function and pushing forward actions commute with the controlled generator and reward functional. Further, approximate value-preserving structures with rigorous guarantees can be found when the Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman mismatch is small. This framework discovers exact and approximate value-preserving structures by searching for the associated Lie group operators. VPSD-RL fits differentiable drift, diffusion, and reward models; learns infinitesimal generators via determining-equation residual minimization; exponentiates them with ODE flows to obtain finite transformations; and integrates them into continuous RL through transition augmentation and transformation-consistency regularization. We show that bounded generator/reward mismatch implies quantitative stability of the optimal value function along approximate orbits, with sensitivity governed by the effective horizon, and observe improved data efficiency and robustness on continuous-control benchmarks.

AI.tldr: VPSD-RL discovers exact and approximate value-preserving structures in continuous RL by finding Lie-group operators that commute with value functions, improving data efficiency and robustness through transformation-consistency regularization.
AI.motivation: Continuous RL with continuous state/action spaces is data-intensive and brittle under nuisance variability/shift. Existing methods focus on special cases like prescribed symmetries without addressing discovery of more general structures requiring nonlinear operators for continuous systems with isomorphic value functions.
AI.method: Models continuous RL as controlled diffusion with value-preserving mappings via Lie-group actions and pullback operators. Discovers structures by searching for associated Lie group operators through: learning differentiable drift/diffusion/reward models, learning infinitesimal generators via determining-equation residual minimization, exponentiating them with ODE flows for finite transformations, and integrating them into RL via transition augmentation and transformation-consistency regularization.
AI.result: Shows bounded generator/reward mismatch implies quantitative stability of optimal value function along approximate orbits with sensitivity governed by effective horizon. Demonstrates improved data efficiency and robustness on continuous-control benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: VPSD-RL provides a framework for discovering both exact and approximate value-preserving structures in continuous RL, going beyond special symmetry cases to find more general nonlinear transformations that preserve value functions, leading to more stable and efficient learning.

title: Cubit: Token Mixer with Kernel Ridge Regression
summary: Since its introduction in 2017, the Transformer has become one of the most widely adopted architectures in modern deep learning. Despite extensive efforts to improve positional encoding, attention mechanisms, and feed-forward networks, the core token-mixing mechanism in Transformers remains attention. In this work, we show that the attention module in Transformers can be interpreted as performing Nadaraya-Watson regression, where it computes similarities between tokens and aggregates the corresponding values accordingly. Motivated by this perspective, we propose Cubit, a potential next-generation architecture that leverages Kernel Ridge Regression (KRR), while the vanilla Transformer relies on Nadaraya-Watson regression. Specifically, Cubit modifies the classical attention computation by incorporating the closed-form solution of KRR, combining value aggregation through kernel similarities with normalization via the inverse of the kernel matrix. To improve the training stability, we further propose the Limited-Range Rescale (LRR), which rescales the value layer within a controlled range. We argue that Cubit, as a KRR-based architecture, provides a stronger mathematical foundation than the vanilla Transformer, whose attention mechanism corresponds to Nadaraya-Watson regression. We validate this claim through comprehensive experiments. The experimental results suggest that Cubit may exhibit stronger long-sequence modeling capability. In particular, its performance gain over the Transformer appears to increase as the training sequence length grows.

AI.tldr: Cubit is a new transformer architecture that replaces attention with Kernel Ridge Regression (KRR), providing stronger mathematical foundations and better long-sequence modeling than traditional transformers.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from interpreting the attention mechanism in Transformers as performing Nadaraya-Watson regression. The authors seek to improve upon this by leveraging the mathematically stronger Kernel Ridge Regression (KRR) instead, potentially providing better theoretical foundations and performance.
AI.method: Cubit replaces the classical attention computation with the closed-form solution of Kernel Ridge Regression. It combines value aggregation through kernel similarities with normalization via the inverse of the kernel matrix. To improve training stability, the authors introduce Limited-Range Rescale (LRR) which rescales the value layer within a controlled range.
AI.result: Experimental results show that Cubit exhibits stronger long-sequence modeling capability compared to vanilla Transformers. The performance gain over Transformers increases as the training sequence length grows.
AI.conclusion: Cubit, as a KRR-based architecture, provides a stronger mathematical foundation than the vanilla Transformer's Nadaraya-Watson regression approach. The proposed architecture represents a potential next-generation transformer design with improved long-sequence capabilities.

title: PACZero: PAC-Private Fine-Tuning of Language Models via Sign Quantization
summary: We introduce PACZero, a family of PAC-private zeroth-order mechanisms for fine-tuning large language models that delivers usable utility at $I(S^*; Y_{1:T})=0$. This privacy regime bounds the membership-inference attack (MIA) posterior success rate at the prior, an MIA-resistance level the DP framework matches only at $\varepsilon=0$ and infinite noise. All DP-ZO comparisons below are matched at the MIA posterior level. The key insight is that PAC Privacy charges mutual information only when the release depends on which candidate subset is the secret. Sign-quantizing subset-aggregated zeroth-order gradients creates frequent unanimity, steps at which every candidate subset agrees on the update direction; at these steps the released sign costs zero conditional mutual information. We propose two variants that span the privacy-utility trade-off: PACZero-MI (budgeted MI via exact calibration on the binary release) and PACZero-ZPL ($I=0$ via a uniform coin flip on disagreement steps). We evaluate on SST-2 and SQuAD with OPT-1.3B and OPT-6.7B in both LoRA and full-parameter tracks. On SST-2 OPT-1.3B full fine-tuning at $I=0$, PACZero-ZPL reaches ${88.99\pm0.91}$, within $2.1$pp of the non-private MeZO baseline ($91.1$ FT). No prior method produces usable utility in the high-privacy regime $\varepsilon<1$, and PACZero-ZPL obtains competitive SST-2 accuracy and nontrivial SQuAD F1 across OPT-1.3B and OPT-6.7B at $I=0$.

AI.tldr: PACZero is a new privacy mechanism for fine-tuning LLMs that achieves zero mutual information leakage while maintaining usable model utility, outperforming differential privacy in high-privacy regimes.
AI.motivation: Differential privacy approaches struggle to provide usable utility at high privacy levels (ε<1), requiring infinite noise to match the privacy level that PACZero achieves at I=0. There's a need for privacy mechanisms that can fine-tune LLMs effectively while providing strong protection against membership inference attacks.
AI.method: Uses PAC-private zeroth-order mechanisms with sign quantization of subset-aggregated gradients. Key insight: frequent unanimity in sign decisions allows zero conditional mutual information release. Two variants: PACZero-MI (budgeted MI with exact calibration) and PACZero-ZPL (I=0 via uniform coin flip on disagreements).
AI.result: On SST-2 with OPT-1.3B full fine-tuning at I=0, PACZero-ZPL achieves 88.99±0.91 accuracy, within 2.1 percentage points of non-private MeZO baseline (91.1). No prior method produced usable utility at ε<1. Competitive accuracy on SST-2 and nontrivial SQuAD F1 scores across both OPT-1.3B and OPT-6.7B at I=0.
AI.conclusion: PACZero enables effective LLM fine-tuning with strong privacy guarantees (I=0) that match the strongest MIA resistance level, addressing the privacy-utility trade-off gap in high-privacy regimes where DP methods fail.

title: Is One Layer Enough? Understanding Inference Dynamics in Tabular Foundation Models
comment: Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Transformer-based tabular foundation models (TFMs) dominate small to medium tabular predictive benchmark tasks, yet their inference mechanisms remain largely unexplored. We present the first large-scale mechanistic study of layerwise dynamics in 6 state-of-the-art tabular in-context learning models. We explore how predictions emerge across depth, identify distinct stages of inference and reveal latent-space dynamics that differ from those of language models. Our findings indicate substantial depthwise redundancy across multiple models, suggesting iterative refinement with overlapping computations during inference stages. Guided by these insights, we design a proof-of-concept, looped single-layer model that uses only 20% of the original model's parameters while achieving comparable performance. The code is available at https://github.com/amirbalef/is_one_layer_enough.

AI.tldr: Transformer-based tabular foundation models show significant depthwise redundancy, allowing a single-loop layer design to achieve comparable performance with only 20% of parameters.
AI.motivation: Transformer-based tabular foundation models (TFMs) dominate tabular predictive tasks but their inference mechanisms remain largely unexplored, creating a knowledge gap about how these models process tabular data compared to language models.
AI.method: Conducted first large-scale mechanistic study of layerwise dynamics across 6 state-of-the-art tabular in-context learning models, analyzing how predictions emerge across depth, identifying distinct inference stages, and revealing latent-space dynamics.
AI.result: Found substantial depthwise redundancy across multiple models with iterative refinement through overlapping computations. Based on these insights, designed a proof-of-concept looped single-layer model using only 20% of original parameters that achieves comparable performance.
AI.conclusion: Tabular foundation models exhibit different inference dynamics than language models with significant redundancy, enabling more efficient architectures like looped single-layer designs without sacrificing performance.

title: Efficient Techniques for Data Reconstruction, with Finite-Width Recovery Guarantees
summary: Data reconstruction attacks on trained neural networks aim to recover the data on which the network has been trained and pose a significant threat to privacy, especially if the training dataset contains sensitive information. Here, we propose a unified optimization formulation of the data reconstruction problem based on initial and trained parameter values, incorporating state-of-the-art proposals. We show that in the random feature model, this formulation provably leads to training data reconstruction with high probability, provided the network width is sufficiently large; this unprecedented finite-width result uses PAC-style bounds. Furthermore, when the data lies in a low-dimensional subspace, we show that the network width requirement for successful reconstruction can be relaxed, with bounds depending on the subspace dimension rather than the ambient dimension. For general neural network models and unknown data orientations, we propose an efficient reconstruction algorithm that approximates the low-dimensional data subspace through the change in the first-layer weights during training and uses only the last-layer weights for reconstruction, thus reducing the search space dimension and the required network width for high-quality reconstructions. Our numerical experiments on synthetic datasets and CIFAR-10 confirm that our subspace-aware reconstruction approach outperforms standard full-space techniques.

AI.tldr: A unified optimization framework for data reconstruction attacks on neural networks, showing provable reconstruction in random feature models, with improved efficiency when exploiting low-dimensional data subspaces.
AI.motivation: Data reconstruction attacks pose serious privacy threats when neural networks are trained on sensitive data, but existing approaches lack theoretical guarantees and efficiency for practical scenarios.
AI.method: Propose unified optimization formulation using initial and trained parameters; prove theoretical reconstruction guarantees in random feature models; develop subspace-aware algorithm that estimates low-dimensional data structure through first-layer weight changes and uses only last-layer weights for reconstruction.
AI.result: Show provable training data reconstruction with high probability for sufficiently wide networks using PAC-style bounds; demonstrate that subspace structure reduces width requirements; numerical experiments on synthetic data and CIFAR-10 show superior performance compared to full-space methods.
AI.conclusion: Theoretical and algorithmic framework establishes formal reconstruction guarantees and practical efficiency improvements by exploiting data subspace structure, making data reconstruction attacks more effective with reduced computational requirements.

title: Agentic AIs Are the Missing Paradigm for Out-of-Distribution Generalization in Foundation Models
summary: Foundation models (FMs) are increasingly deployed in open-world settings where distribution shift is the rule rather than the exception. The out-of-distribution (OOD) phenomena they face -- knowledge boundaries, capability ceilings, compositional shifts, and open-ended task variation -- differ in kind from the settings that have shaped prior OOD research, and are further complicated because the pretraining and post-training distributions of modern FMs are often only partially observed. Our position is that OOD for foundation models is a structurally distinct problem that cannot be solved within the prevailing model-centric paradigm, and that agentic systems constitute the missing paradigm required to address it. We defend this claim through four steps. First, we give a stage-aware formalization of OOD that accommodates partially observed multi-stage training distributions. Second, we prove a parameter coverage ceiling: there exist practically relevant inputs that no model-centric method (training-time or test-time) can handle within tolerance $\varepsilon$, for reasons intrinsic to parameter-based representation. Third, we characterize agentic OOD systems by four structural properties -- perception, strategy selection, external action, and closed-loop verification -- and show that they strictly extend the reachable set beyond the ceiling. Fourth, we respond to seven counterarguments, conceding two, and outline a research agenda. We do not claim that agentic methods subsume model-centric ones; we argue that the two are complementary, and that progress on FM-OOD requires explicit recognition of the agentic paradigm as a first-class research direction.

AI.tldr: OOD phenomena in foundation models require agentic systems, not just model-centric approaches, to handle distribution shifts beyond what parameter-based representations can achieve.
AI.motivation: Foundation models face unique OOD challenges in open-world settings (knowledge boundaries, capability ceilings, compositional shifts, task variations) that differ from traditional OOD problems, compounded by partially observed pretraining and post-training distributions.
AI.method: Four-step approach: 1) Formalize stage-aware OOD with multi-stage training distributions, 2) Prove parameter coverage ceiling showing limits of model-centric methods, 3) Characterize agentic OOD systems with four structural properties, 4) Respond to counterarguments and outline research agenda.
AI.result: Agentic systems with perception, strategy selection, external action, and closed-loop verification can handle OOD cases beyond what parameter-based model-centric methods can achieve, establishing agentic paradigm as complementary and necessary for FM-OOD.
AI.conclusion: OOD for foundation models is a structurally distinct problem requiring the agentic paradigm as a first-class research direction alongside model-centric approaches, as no single approach can handle all OOD scenarios.

title: On the Implicit Reward Overfitting and the Low-rank Dynamics in RLVR
summary: Recent extensive research has demonstrated that the enhanced reasoning capabilities acquired by models through Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) are primarily concentrated within the rank-1 components. Predicated on this observation, we employed Periodic Rank-1 Substitution and identified a counterintuitive phenomenon: RLVR may exhibit implicit reward overfitting to the training dataset. Specifically, the model can achieve satisfactory performance on the test set even when its rewards remain relatively low during the training process. Furthermore, we characterize three distinct properties of RL training: (1) The effective rank-1 component in RLVR don't maintain other model knowledge except mathematical reasoning capability. (2) RLVR fundamentally functions by optimizing a specific singular spectrum. The distribution of singular values of almost all linear layers in RLVR-trained model behaves like heavy-tailed distribution. (3) the left singular vectors associated with rank-1 components demonstrate a stronger alignment tendency during training, which echoes the discovery that RLVR is optimizing sampling efficiency in essence. Taken together, our findings and analysis further reveal how RLVR shapes model parameters and offer potential insights for improving existing RL paradigms or other training paradigms to implement continual learning.

AI.tldr: RLVR training concentrates reasoning capabilities in rank-1 components, but exhibits implicit reward overfitting where test performance remains good despite low training rewards, revealing insights about RL parameter shaping.
AI.motivation: Recent research shows enhanced reasoning from RLVR concentrates in rank-1 components, motivating investigation into how RLVR shapes model parameters and whether it suffers from reward overfitting issues.
AI.method: Used Periodic Rank-1 Substitution to analyze RLVR training, examining singular value distributions, singular vector alignments, and component substitution effects.
AI.result: Found RLVR exhibits implicit reward overfitting; rank-1 components don't preserve other knowledge; singular values follow heavy-tailed distributions; left singular vectors show alignment tendency during training.
AI.conclusion: RLVR fundamentally optimizes specific singular spectra and sampling efficiency, offering insights for improving RL paradigms and continual learning approaches through better understanding of parameter shaping.

title: Diffusion-Based Posterior Sampling: A Feynman-Kac Analysis of Bias and Stability
summary:
Diffusion-based posterior samplers use pretrained diffusion priors to sample from measurement- or reward-conditioned posteriors, and are widely used for inverse problems. Yet their theoretical behavior remains poorly understood: even with exact prior scores, their outputs are biased, and in low-temperature regimes their discretizations can become unstable. We characterize this bias by introducing a tractable surrogate path connecting the true posterior to a standard Gaussian and comparing it to the sampler's path. Their density ratio satisfies a parabolic PDE whose reaction term measures the accumulated bias. A Feynman-Kac representation then expresses the Radon-Nikodym correction as an explicit path expectation, identifying which posterior regions are over- or under-sampled.
  We apply this framework to DPS and STSL, a related sampler. For DPS, the correction is an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck path expectation coupling the data conditional covariance with the reward curvature, revealing where DPS over- or under-samples. Next, we reinterpret STSL as an auxiliary drift that steers trajectories toward low-uncertainty regions, flattening the spatially varying part of the DPS reaction term. Finally, we characterize early guidance-stopping, a common mitigation for low-temperature instabilities caused by forward-Euler integration of the vector field. Together, these results clarify sampler bias, explain existing correctives, and guide stable variant designs.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes bias in diffusion-based posterior samplers, develops theoretical framework to characterize sampler bias via PDE analysis and Feynman-Kac representation, and applies the framework to understand and improve existing samplers like DPS and STSL.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based posterior samplers are widely used for inverse problems but their theoretical behavior remains poorly understood - they produce biased outputs even with exact prior scores, and their discretizations can become unstable in low-temperature regimes.
AI.method: Introduces a tractable surrogate path connecting the true posterior to a standard Gaussian, compares it to the sampler's path, derives a parabolic PDE for their density ratio, uses Feynman-Kac representation to express Radon-Nikodym correction as an explicit path expectation.
AI.result: Framework applied to DPS reveals correction as Ornstein-Uhlenbeck path expectation coupling data conditional covariance with reward curvature; STSL reinterpreted as auxiliary drift that steers trajectories toward low-uncertainty regions; early guidance-stopping characterized as mitigation for low-temperature instabilities.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical framework clarifies sampler bias, explains existing corrective measures, and provides guidance for designing stable variants of diffusion-based posterior samplers used in inverse problems.

title: Hedging Memory Horizons for Non-Stationary Prediction via Online Aggregation
summary: We study online prediction under distribution shift, where inputs arrive chronologically and outcomes are revealed only after prediction. In this setting, predictors must remain stable in quiet regimes yet adapt when regimes shift, and the right adaptation memory is unknown in advance. We propose MELO (Memory-hedged Exponentially Weighted Least-Squares Online aggregation), a model-agnostic method that hedges across adaptation scales: it wraps any non-anticipating base-predictor pool with exponentially weighted least-squares (EWLS) adaptation experts at multiple forgetting factors, and aggregates raw and EWLS-adapted forecasts with MLpol, a parameter-free online aggregation rule. Under boundedness conditions, we establish deterministic oracle inequalities showing that it competes with both the best raw predictor and the best bounded, time-varying affine combinations of the base predictions, up to a path-length-dependent tracking cost and a sublinear aggregation overhead. We evaluate MELO on French national electricity-load forecasting through the COVID-19 lockdown using no regime indicators, lockdown dates, or policy covariates. MELO reduces overall RMSE by 34.7\% relative to base-only MLpol and achieves lower overall RMSE than a TabICL reference supplied with an external COVID policy-response covariate. Moreover, MELO requires only lightweight per-step recursive updates without model retraining.

AI.tldr: MELO is a model-agnostic online prediction method that hedges across adaptation scales to handle distribution shift without needing regime indicators or model retraining, achieving 34.7% RMSE reduction in electricity load forecasting during COVID-19 lockdowns.
AI.motivation: Online prediction faces challenges of distribution shift where predictors must balance stability during quiet regimes with adaptation during regime shifts, without knowing the optimal adaptation memory in advance.
AI.method: MELO (Memory-hedged Exponentially Weighted Least-Squares Online aggregation) wraps base predictors with EWLS adaptation experts at multiple forgetting factors, then aggregates raw and adapted forecasts using the parameter-free MLpol aggregation rule.
AI.result: Theoretical: deterministic oracle inequalities showing competition with best raw predictor and best time-varying affine combinations. Empirical: 34.7% RMSE reduction in French electricity-load forecasting during COVID-19 lockdowns, outperforming methods with external COVID policy covariates.
AI.conclusion: MELO provides an effective, lightweight approach for online prediction under distribution shift without needing regime indicators, requiring only per-step recursive updates and no model retraining, making it practical for real-world applications.

title: Sequential Design of Genetic Circuits Under Uncertainty With Reinforcement Learning
summary: The design of biological systems is hindered by uncertainty arising from both intrinsic stochasticity of biomolecular reactions and variability across laboratory or experimental conditions. In this work, we present a sequential framework to optimize genetic circuits under both forms of uncertainty. By employing simulator models based on differential equations or Markov jump processes alongside a reinforcement learning (RL) policy-based approach, our method suggests experiments that adapt to unknown laboratory conditions while accounting for inherent stochasticity. While previous Bayesian methods address uncertainty through iterative experiment-inference-optimization cycles, they typically require computationally expensive inference and optimization steps after each experimental round, leading to delays. To overcome this bottleneck, we propose an amortized approach trained up-front across a distribution of possible uncertain parameters. This strategy sidesteps the need for explicit parameter inference during the design cycle, enabling immediate, observation-based adaptation. We demonstrate our framework on models for heterologous gene expression and a repressilator circuit, showing that it efficiently handles both molecular noise and cross-laboratory variability.

AI.tldr: A sequential framework for optimizing genetic circuits under dual uncertainty (intrinsic stochasticity + lab variability) using reinforcement learning and amortized training to enable immediate adaptation without costly parameter inference.
AI.motivation: Biological system design is hindered by two forms of uncertainty: intrinsic stochasticity of biomolecular reactions and variability across laboratory/experimental conditions. Existing Bayesian methods require computationally expensive inference and optimization steps after each experimental round, causing delays.
AI.method: Proposes a sequential framework using simulator models (differential equations or Markov jump processes) with reinforcement learning policy-based approach. Features an amortized approach trained upfront across a distribution of possible uncertain parameters, eliminating explicit parameter inference during design cycles for immediate observation-based adaptation.
AI.result: Demonstrated on models for heterologous gene expression and repressilator circuits, showing efficient handling of both molecular noise and cross-laboratory variability.
AI.conclusion: The amortized reinforcement learning framework overcomes computational bottlenecks of previous methods by enabling immediate adaptation to uncertainties without expensive parameter inference, making genetic circuit optimization more efficient under dual uncertainty conditions.

title: Diverse Sampling in Diffusion Models with Marginal Preserving Particle Guidance
summary: We present EDDY (Exact-marginal Diversification via Divergence-free dYnamics), a guidance mechanism for diffusion and flow matching models that promotes diversity among samples generated while maintaining quality. EDDY exploits symmetries of the Fokker-Planck equation, using drift perturbations that change particle trajectories while preserving the evolving marginal distribution. We instantiate this principle through kernel-based anti-symmetric pairwise matrix fields, constructed from the repulsive directions. The resulting divergence-free dynamics promote diversity at the joint particle level while preserving each particle's marginal distribution without any additional training. As computing the guidance can be computationally expensive in cases such as text-to-image generation with perceptual embeddings, we propose practical approximations as an effective and efficient solution. Experiments on synthetic distributions and text-to-image generation show that EDDY improves diversity while maintaining strong distributional fidelity compared to common baselines.

AI.tldr: EDDY is a guidance mechanism for diffusion/flow matching models that promotes sample diversity while maintaining quality by using divergence-free dynamics that preserve marginal distributions.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion and flow matching models often generate low-diversity samples, especially in text-to-image generation where multiple high-quality samples tend to be similar. There's a need to increase diversity among generated samples without sacrificing quality or requiring retraining.
AI.method: EDDY exploits symmetries of the Fokker-Planck equation by applying drift perturbations that change particle trajectories while preserving the evolving marginal distribution. It uses kernel-based anti-symmetric pairwise matrix fields constructed from repulsive directions to create divergence-free dynamics. The approach works without additional training, though computational approximations are proposed for expensive cases like text-to-image generation with perceptual embeddings.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic distributions and text-to-image generation show EDDY improves diversity while maintaining strong distributional fidelity compared to common baselines. The guidance mechanism effectively promotes diversity at the joint particle level while preserving each particle's marginal distribution.
AI.conclusion: EDDY provides an effective guidance mechanism for diffusion/flow matching models that successfully addresses the diversity-quality trade-off, offering practical solutions through approximations for computationally expensive applications while maintaining distributional fidelity.

title: Optimal Counterfactual Search in Tree Ensembles: A Study Across Modeling and Solution Paradigms
summary: Trust in counterfactual explanations depends critically on whether their recommended changes are truly minimal: suboptimal explanations may vastly overshoot the actual changes needed to alter a decision, and heuristic errors can affect individuals unevenly, giving some users relevant recourse while assigning others unnecessarily costly recommendations. Consequently, we study the problem of computing optimal counterfactual explanations for tree ensembles under plausibility and actionability constraints. This is a combinatorial problem: for a fixed model, counterfactual search boils down to selecting consistent branching decisions and threshold-defined regions under a distance objective. We exploit this structure through CPCF, a constraint programming (CP) formulation in which numerical features are encoded as interval domains induced by split thresholds, while discrete features retain native finite-domain representations. This yields a compact finite-domain formulation that supports multiple distance objectives without continuous split-boundary search. We then place CPCF in a broader comparison across mathematical programming paradigms: we extend a maximum Boolean satisfiability (MaxSAT) formulation, originally designed for hard-voting random forests, to soft-voting ensembles, and compare against the current state-of-the-art mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) optimal approach. Across ten datasets and three types of tree ensembles, we analyze scalability, anytime performance, and sensitivity to distance metrics. We observe that CP achieves the best overall performance. More importantly, our results identify regimes in which the specific strengths of each paradigm make it best suited: CP is most versatile overall, MaxSAT handles hard-voting ensembles particularly well, and MILP remains competitive in amortized inference settings with a moderate number of split levels.

AI.tldr: CPCF is a constraint programming approach for computing optimal counterfactual explanations in tree ensembles that outperforms MaxSAT and MILP methods, with CP being most versatile overall.
AI.motivation: Trust in counterfactual explanations depends on whether recommended changes are truly minimal. Heuristic errors in current methods can lead to overshooting necessary changes and affect individuals unevenly, giving some users unnecessarily costly recommendations.
AI.method: The authors propose CPCF, a constraint programming formulation that encodes numerical features as interval domains induced by split thresholds and discrete features as finite-domain representations. They compare it with extended MaxSAT formulation (for soft-voting ensembles) and state-of-the-art MILP optimal approach across mathematical programming paradigms.
AI.result: Across ten datasets and three types of tree ensembles, CP achieves the best overall performance. The analysis identifies specific regimes where each paradigm excels: CP is most versatile, MaxSAT handles hard-voting ensembles particularly well, and MILP remains competitive in amortized inference settings with moderate split levels.
AI.conclusion: Constraint programming (CP) provides the most effective overall approach for computing optimal counterfactual explanations in tree ensembles, though different mathematical programming paradigms have distinct strengths suitable for specific regimes, allowing practitioners to choose based on ensemble type and inference context.

title: Feature Dimensionality Outweighs Model Complexity in Breast Cancer Subtype Classification Using TCGA-BRCA Gene Expression Data
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Independent research study using TCGA-BRCA RNA-seq data
summary:
Accurate classification of breast cancer subtypes from gene expression data is critical for diagnosis and treatment selection. However, such datasets are characterized by high dimensionality and limited sample size, posing challenges for machine learning models.
  In this study, we evaluate the impact of model complexity and feature selection on subtype classification performance using TCGA-BRCA gene expression data. Logistic regression, random forest, and support vector machine (SVM) models were trained using varying numbers of highly variable genes (50 to 20,518). Performance was evaluated using stratified 5-fold cross-validation and assessed with accuracy and macro F1 score. While all models achieved high accuracy, macro F1 analysis revealed substantial differences in subtype-level performance. Logistic regression demonstrated the most stable and balanced performance across subtypes, including improved detection of rare classes. Random forest underperformed on minority subtypes despite strong overall accuracy, while SVM showed sensitivity to feature dimensionality. These findings highlight the importance of model simplicity, evaluation metrics, and feature selection in high-dimensional biological classification tasks.

AI.tldr: Study compares logistic regression, random forest, and SVM for breast cancer subtype classification using TCGA gene expression data, finding logistic regression performs most consistently across subtypes despite high dimensionality challenges.
AI.motivation: Breast cancer subtype classification from gene expression data is crucial for diagnosis and treatment, but faces challenges due to high dimensionality and limited sample sizes, creating difficulties for machine learning models.
AI.method: Used TCGA-BRCA gene expression data with varying numbers of highly variable genes (50 to 20,518). Trained logistic regression, random forest, and SVM models. Evaluated performance using stratified 5-fold cross-validation with accuracy and macro F1 score metrics.
AI.result: All models achieved high accuracy, but macro F1 analysis revealed significant subtype-level differences. Logistic regression showed most stable and balanced performance across all subtypes, including better detection of rare classes. Random forest underperformed on minority subtypes despite high overall accuracy, while SVM was sensitive to feature dimensionality.
AI.conclusion: Highlights the importance of model simplicity, appropriate evaluation metrics (macro F1 over accuracy), and careful feature selection in high-dimensional biological classification tasks, with logistic regression performing best despite its simplicity.

title: Criticality and Saturation in Orthogonal Neural Networks
summary: It has been known for a long time that initializing weight matrices to be orthogonal instead of having i.i.d. Gaussian components can improve training performance. This phenomenon can be analyzed using finite-width corrections, where the infinite-width statistics are supplemented by a power series in $1/\mathrm{width}$. In particular, recent empirical results by Day et al. show that the tensors appearing in this treatment stabilize for large depth, as opposed to the tensors of i.i.d.-initialized networks. In this article, we derive explicit layer-wise recursion relations for the tensors appearing in the finite-width expansion of the network statistics in the case of orthogonal initializations. We also provide an extension of recently-introduced Feynman diagrams for the corresponding recursions in the i.i.d.-case which are valid to all orders in $1/\mathrm{width}$. Finally, we show explicitly that the recursions we derive reproduce the stability of the finite-width tensors which was observed for activation functions with vanishing fixed point. This work therefore provides a theoretical explanation for the stability of nonlinear networks of finite width initialized with orthogonal weights, closing a long-standing gap in the literature. We validate our theoretical results experimentally by showing that numerical solutions of our recursion relations and their analytical large-depth expansions agree excellently with Monte-Carlo estimates from network ensembles.

AI.tldr: The paper develops theoretical tools to explain why orthogonal weight initialization stabilizes neural network training better than i.i.d. Gaussian initialization, particularly at large depth.
AI.motivation: Orthogonal weight initialization is known to improve training performance compared to i.i.d. Gaussian initialization, but the theoretical understanding of this phenomenon has been incomplete. Recent empirical results showed that finite-width tensors stabilize for large depth in orthogonal-initialized networks, unlike in i.i.d.-initialized networks.
AI.method: The authors derive explicit layer-wise recursion relations for tensors appearing in the finite-width expansion of network statistics for orthogonal initializations. They extend Feynman diagram techniques from i.i.d. case to work to all orders in 1/width. They analytically show that their recursions reproduce the stability observed for activation functions with vanishing fixed point.
AI.result: The paper provides theoretical explanation for stability of finite-width orthogonal-initialized networks. The derived recursions successfully reproduce the observed tensor stabilization.
